name: Recognise the place value of each digit in whole numbers up to millions
masteryDefinition: A student has mastered this skill when they can accurately identify and state the place value of any digit in a whole number up to 1,000,000, using both numerical notation (e.g., 3,000) and place value language (e.g., 'three thousands'). They can explain why a digit's value depends on its position and apply this understanding to compare, order, and partition numbers.

misconceptions.label: Confusing digit value with place value
misconceptions.description: Students state the digit itself rather than its place value. For example, in 24,567, when asked 'what is the place value of the 2?', they answer '2' instead of '20,000'. They may conflate the digit's numeral with the magnitude it represents in that position.

misconceptions.label: Misplacing the value of zeros in place value columns
misconceptions.description: Students struggle with numbers containing zeros (e.g., 305,020) and either ignore zeros entirely, treat them as having a value, or miscalculate the place value of digits adjacent to them. For instance, they may think the 3 in 305,020 is worth 300 instead of 300,000.

misconceptions.label: Counting place value columns from right to left without understanding position names
misconceptions.description: Students mechanically count positions from the right but cannot reliably name or identify places (ones, tens, hundreds, thousands, ten thousands, hundred thousands, millions). They may correctly identify 'the 4th position from the right' but fail to recognise it as ten thousands, leading to errors when translating to value.

misconceptions.label: Assuming place value patterns break after thousands
misconceptions.description: Students generalise place value patterns only up to thousands (ones, tens, hundreds, thousands) and fail to extend the pattern correctly to ten thousands, hundred thousands, and millions. They may think the place after thousands is 'ten-thousands-thousands' or incorrectly assume it resets.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Two or three-digit numbers (up to 999) with no zeros
difficultyDimensions.hard: Six-digit numbers (hundreds of thousands, up to 999,999) with multiple zeros in different positions

difficultyDimensions.easy: Numbers with no zeros (e.g., 456, 23,456)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Numbers with zeros in medial or leading positions within a number segment (e.g., 405,030; 10,205)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Identifying the place value of a single digit in a given number
difficultyDimensions.hard: Partitioning a multi-digit number into its component place values, or identifying which digit occupies a given place value (e.g., 'which digit is in the ten thousands place?')

transferContexts.example: To round 347,852 to the nearest thousand, a student must first recognise that 7 is in the thousands place and 8 is in the hundreds place, and understand that 8 hundreds is enough to round the 7 thousands up to 8 thousands.

When comparing 345,670 and 354,067, understanding place value allows a student to immediately see that both have the same hundred thousands digit (3) but the second has 5 ten thousands vs 4 ten thousands, so 354,067 is larger.
When adding 28,456 + 13,789, understanding place value explains why we regroup: 6 + 9 = 15 (5 ones and 1 ten), and why the 1 ten moves to the tens column, not stays in the ones place.

generativeContext: This skill forms the foundation for all subsequent number work. Students must internalise that a numeral's value is determined entirely by its position within a number. For whole numbers up to 1,000,000, the place value hierarchy is: ones, tens (×10), hundreds (×100), thousands (×1,000), ten thousands (×10,000), hundred thousands (×100,000), millions (×1,000,000). Emphasise that each step left represents multiplying by 10, and zeros are placeholders that hold positions but contribute zero to the total value. Use concrete resources (place value grids, counters, base-ten materials) before moving to abstract notation. Common error patterns: (1) students state the digit rather than its value; (2) students mishandle zeros, especially in numbers like 203,050 where zeros in non-terminal positions confuse magnitude estimation; (3) students fail to recognise the repeating ×10 pattern and instead treat each new place name as arbitrary; (4) for larger numbers, students revert to memorised place names without understanding the underlying multiplicative structure. Scaffold by repeatedly asking 'how many tens are in this number?' vs 'what is the digit in the tens place?' to clarify the distinction. Use number lines and place value charts to make positions explicit. Assessment should include both standard notation (342,567) and expanded form (300,000 + 40,000 + 2,000 + 500 + 60 + 7) to check whether students can fluidly translate between representations.

name: Write integers in words and figures
masteryDefinition: Students can accurately convert between written word form and numerical figure form for integers up to at least 1,000,000. They understand place value structure and can correctly sequence digits, correctly name place values (ones, tens, hundreds, thousands, ten thousands, hundred thousands, millions), and avoid dropping zeros or digits when converting. They can handle integers with zeros in any position and recognize that words like 'and' are not used in standard number naming for whole numbers.

misconceptions.label: Omitting zeros when writing figures
misconceptions.description: Students write the number 'five thousand and twelve' as 512 instead of 5012, dropping the zero placeholders in the hundreds place. This reflects a misunderstanding that each digit position must be explicitly represented, even when the value is zero.

misconceptions.label: Inserting 'and' in figure form or misplacing it in word form
misconceptions.description: Students write '2,045' as 'two thousand and forty-five' or write 'two thousand forty five' as '2,000,45', treating 'and' as a place-value separator rather than understanding it is not standard in British English number naming for whole numbers, or using it inconsistently.

misconceptions.label: Reversing digit order when writing large numbers
misconceptions.description: Students write 'three hundred and twenty-seven thousand' as 72,300 or 327,003 instead of 327,000, particularly struggling with place value alignment when numbers span multiple comma groups.

misconceptions.label: Misreading or misnaming hyphenated compound numbers
misconceptions.description: Students write 'forty-two' as 24 instead of 42, or 'ninety-seven' as 79, reversing the tens and ones digits because they process the word sequence left-to-right without attending to place value naming conventions for compound numbers between 21 and 99.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Numbers up to 99 (single digit and two-digit numbers without zero placeholders)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Numbers in the millions with multiple zeros in non-consecutive positions (e.g., 3,050,007)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Numbers with no zeros or zeros only at the end (e.g., 200, 3,000)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Numbers with zeros in middle and final positions (e.g., 100,050 or 5,008)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Numbers within a single group (units to hundreds, e.g., 1–999)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Numbers spanning three or more groups (millions, hundred thousands, ten thousands, thousands, e.g., 4,567,890)

transferContexts.context: Reading and writing numbers in data tables and statistical reports
transferContexts.example: A table shows 'Population: two million, three hundred thousand, forty-seven'. Write this figure in numbers. Or: A sales report shows '£567,089'. Write this amount in words.

transferContexts.context: Interpreting numbers in real-world contexts (money, distance, population)
transferContexts.example: A news article states 'The city has a population of five hundred and sixty-two thousand'. Write the exact figure. Or: A distance sign reads '1,250 metres'. How would you write this in words for a description?

transferContexts.context: Recording and communicating measurements and counts in science or geography
transferContexts.example: A ruler measurement is 423 mm. Write this in words. Or: A map scale shows '50,000'. Describe in words what this number represents.

generativeContext: N1.2 tests bidirectional fluency between English word form and standard numerical notation for integers. The core challenge is place-value alignment: students must track that each digit position (ones, tens, hundreds, thousands, etc.) has a fixed value, and that zero is a placeholder with real positional meaning, not an absence of information. Particular trouble spots: (1) zero omission (saying 'five thousand twelve' but writing only 3 digits), (2) hyphenated number reversal in the 21–99 range, (3) mishandling of 'and' as a word-to-figure boundary marker, (4) confusion with comma grouping and read-left-to-right vs read-by-magnitude. Generate questions that isolate these; avoid mixing conversion with arithmetic. Ask for one clear direction at a time (words-to-figures or figures-to-words). Include zeros in middle positions, use numbers spanning 4–7 digits, and vary whether zeros are terminal, medial, or absent. Scaffold by offering digit-position labels or partial figure/word templates early, then remove scaffolds. Do not use commas in written form ('two million three hundred thousand' not 'two million, three hundred thousand'), matching UK curriculum convention.

name: Compare two numbers using =, ≠, <, >, ≤, ≥
masteryDefinition: Students can accurately select and use the correct comparison symbol (=, ≠, <, >, ≤, ≥) to express the relationship between two numbers. They understand that < and > are strict inequalities, while ≤ and ≥ include equality. They can interpret and write comparison statements involving positive and negative integers, decimals, and fractions, and explain their reasoning.

misconceptions.label: Direction confusion with < and >
misconceptions.description: Students reverse the direction of inequality symbols, writing 5 < 8 as 5 > 8, or treating < and > as interchangeable. Often stems from reading left-to-right without attending to symbol direction or visualising number lines incorrectly.

misconceptions.label: Confusing ≤ and ≥ with < and >
misconceptions.description: Students use ≤ when < is required (or vice versa), failing to distinguish between 'strictly less than' and 'less than or equal to'. Often writes 3 ≤ 5 when only 3 < 5 is correct, or misinterprets 'at least 10' as requiring <.

misconceptions.label: Misunderstanding ≠ as a direction symbol
misconceptions.description: Students treat ≠ as indicating a direction (< or >) rather than non-equality, or confuse it with = when comparing numbers that are close in value.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect comparison with negative numbers
misconceptions.description: Students apply comparison rules for positive numbers to negatives, incorrectly stating −5 > −2 or −8 < −3, failing to recognise that numbers further left on a number line (more negative) are smaller.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Comparing two positive single-digit integers (3 and 7)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Comparing negative numbers, decimals, or fractions (−4.5 and −2.1, or −3/4 and −1/2)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Using only < or > symbols with clear, unambiguous relationships
difficultyDimensions.hard: Choosing between ≤ and < or between ≥ and > based on context; using ≠

difficultyDimensions.easy: Direct comparison with explicit question (e.g. 'Write the correct symbol: 6 ___ 9')
difficultyDimensions.hard: Contextual or reverse-direction problems (e.g. 'Write a number that makes this true: ___ > 15'; or interpreting inequalities from worded scenarios)

transferContexts.context: Ordering and sorting in real-world contexts
transferContexts.example: Order the temperatures from coldest to warmest: −3°C, 2°C, −7°C, 5°C using < and > symbols.

transferContexts.context: Understanding constraints and ranges in problems
transferContexts.example: A cinema has no more than 200 seats. Write an inequality using ≤ or < to describe the number of seats available.

transferContexts.context: Reading and writing inequalities on a number line
transferContexts.example: Mark all numbers that satisfy x ≥ −2 on a number line; explain why the circle is filled or unfilled.

generativeContext: N1.3 focuses on symbolic comparison of two quantities. Students must distinguish between strict inequality (< and >) and inclusive inequality (≤ and ≥), recognise non-equality (≠), and apply these consistently across positive integers, negative integers, decimals, and fractions. A critical conceptual foundation is the number line: reinforce that symbols point to the smaller value, and that negative numbers reverse intuition (−10 < −5, not −10 > −5). Common generation prompts should include: direct comparison ('Write the symbol'), reverse problems ('Find a number that satisfies…'), and worded scenarios requiring symbol selection. When generating harder items, combine negative numbers or decimals with inclusive/exclusive inequality choices. Avoid generating ambiguous items where both < and ≤ could technically fit; ensure problem context makes the choice unambiguous.

name: Use and interpret inequalities in context (incl number lines and statements)
masteryDefinition: Students can correctly interpret and use inequality symbols (>, <, ≥, ≤, ≠) in mathematical statements and in context. They can represent inequalities on number lines with appropriate notation (open/closed circles), translate between symbolic and word form, and apply inequalities to real-world constraints. They understand that an inequality describes a range of values rather than a single solution.

misconceptions.label: Treating inequality symbols as directional arrows only
misconceptions.description: Students believe < means 'go left' and > means 'go right' on a number line without understanding the relational meaning. This leads to reversed inequalities when the variable is on the right: e.g. writing '5 < x' as 'x is less than 5' instead of 'x is greater than 5'. Students may also confuse the symbol direction with the actual comparison.

misconceptions.label: Confusing open and closed circle notation on number lines
misconceptions.description: Students use a closed circle for < and > (strict inequalities) and an open circle for ≤ and ≥ (non-strict inequalities), reversing the correct convention. They may not understand that an open circle means the value is excluded, and a closed circle means it is included.

misconceptions.label: Treating inequality symbols as equals signs in context problems
misconceptions.description: When interpreting inequalities in real-world scenarios (e.g. 'height ≥ 150 cm for a theme park ride'), students write single values instead of describing a range. They may state '150 cm' rather than 'any height of 150 cm or more', losing the idea that an inequality represents multiple valid solutions.

misconceptions.label: Incorrectly reading compound inequalities or multiple conditions
misconceptions.description: Students struggle with statements like '2 < x ≤ 8' and may misinterpret which values satisfy the condition, or fail to recognise that both conditions must be satisfied simultaneously. They may also write disjoint ranges as if they were single inequalities.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Variable on the left side of the inequality symbol (e.g. x > 5)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Variable on the right side or in compound form (e.g. 3 < x, or 2 < x ≤ 9)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Type of inequality and boundary inclusion
difficultyDimensions.easy: Strict inequalities (< and >) with open circles on number lines
difficultyDimensions.hard: Non-strict inequalities (≤ and ≥) with closed circles, and distinguishing between strict and non-strict in the same problem

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single inequality in abstract form or simple real-world context (e.g. 'x < 10')
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple constraints in realistic scenarios (e.g. 'at least 18 years old AND at most 65 years old') requiring translation and representation

transferContexts.example: A swimming pool allows entry for ages between 5 and 60 inclusive. Write an inequality for the ages allowed and show this on a number line.

transferContexts.context: Algebraic inequality solving and solution sets
transferContexts.example: If 2x + 3 > 11, write the solution as an inequality and represent it on a number line. Identify three values that satisfy the inequality.

A manufacturing process accepts components where the diameter is between 4.8 cm and 5.2 cm (inclusive). Write an inequality and explain which of these measurements (4.7, 4.8, 5.0, 5.3) would be accepted.

generativeContext: N1.4 focuses on reading, interpreting, and using inequalities in both symbolic and real-world forms. Core idea: inequalities denote ranges or sets of values, not single solutions. Key notation: < (less than, strict), > (greater than, strict), ≤ (less than or equal, non-strict), ≥ (greater than or equal, non-strict), ≠ (not equal). Number line representation uses open circles for strict inequalities (value excluded) and closed circles for non-strict (value included). Variable position matters—students must correctly translate '5 < x' to 'x is greater than 5', not the reverse. Compound inequalities like '2 < x ≤ 8' require both conditions to hold simultaneously; students must shade or mark only the region satisfying both. In context, inequalities model constraints: age, temperature, cost, distance. Generation should vary variable position, mix strict and non-strict symbols, include single and compound inequalities, and scaffold from symbolic to contextual forms. Avoid ambiguity in number line diagrams; require explicit open/closed circle identification. High-frequency errors centre on reversed symbol interpretation, inverted circle notation, and loss of 'range' understanding in real-world scenarios.

name: Find the median from a set of numbers (incl midpoint using a calculator)
masteryDefinition: Students can identify the median of any ordered dataset by locating the middle value (or calculating the mean of the two middle values for even-sized sets). They can verify their answer using a calculator when needed and understand that the median is robust to outliers unlike the mean.

misconceptions.label: Forgetting to order the data before finding the median
misconceptions.description: Students find the middle position in the list as given without sorting in ascending or descending order first. For example, given {7, 2, 9, 1, 5}, they identify 9 as the median because it is in the middle position, rather than ordering to {1, 2, 5, 7, 9} to get median 5.

misconceptions.label: Confusing median with mean when an even number of data points exists
misconceptions.description: When there are two middle values in an even-sized dataset, students add them together instead of dividing by 2, or simply pick one of the two middle values. For example, given {3, 5, 7, 8}, they state the median is 12 (5+7) or just 7, rather than 6 (the mean of 5 and 7).

misconceptions.label: Including duplicate values as separate entries when counting position
misconceptions.description: When data contains repeated values, students miscount the position of the median by treating duplicates as though they occupy different logical positions. For example, in {2, 2, 3, 4, 4}, they incorrectly identify position 3 as the median instead of recognising there are 5 values and position 3 is the median (value 3).

misconceptions.label: Misunderstanding calculator output for two-middle-value scenarios
misconceptions.description: When using a calculator to find the mean of two middle values (the median for even datasets), students misinterpret the decimal result or round incorrectly. For example, (5 + 8) ÷ 2 = 6.5, but they record 6 or 7, losing the fractional part or rounding without justification.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Small odd-sized datasets (5 values or fewer, e.g. {1, 3, 5, 7, 9})
difficultyDimensions.hard: Large or even-sized datasets requiring calculation of the mean of two middle values (e.g. {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} or 20 data points with duplicates)

difficultyDimensions.easy: All values are distinct (e.g. {2, 5, 8, 10, 15})
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple repeated values or clustered duplicates (e.g. {1, 2, 2, 2, 3, 5, 5, 7, 7, 7})

difficultyDimensions.easy: Data is pre-sorted in ascending order with positive integers only
difficultyDimensions.hard: Data is unsorted and includes negative numbers and/or decimals (e.g. {-3.5, -1, 0.2, 2, 4.8})

transferContexts.context: Real-world data interpretation (e.g. house prices, test scores, heights)
transferContexts.example: A property developer recorded the selling prices of 7 houses: £250,000, £180,000, £320,000, £205,000, £295,000, £240,000, £310,000. Find the median house price and explain why it is a better measure of central tendency than the mean for this dataset.

transferContexts.context: Comparing datasets using median (e.g. comparing performance between two groups or conditions)
transferContexts.example: Two classes took a maths test. Class A scored: {45, 67, 72, 58, 61}. Class B scored: {52, 55, 88, 48, 60}. Calculate the median for each class and state which class performed more consistently. Why might the median be more informative here than the range?

transferContexts.context: Statistical reasoning with outliers and robustness
transferContexts.example: A survey recorded daily rainfall (in mm) over 9 days: {2, 3, 1, 28, 2, 1, 3, 2, 1}. Find the median rainfall and explain why the median is less affected by the unusual value (28 mm) than the mean would be.

generativeContext: N1.5 requires students to locate the central position of an ordered dataset. The core procedure is: (1) sort all values in ascending order, (2) count the total number of values n, (3) if n is odd, the median is the value at position (n+1)/2, (4) if n is even, the median is the average of the values at positions n/2 and (n/2)+1. For even-sized datasets, students must use division (often a calculator) to find the mean of the two middle values. Emphasise that the median is a measure of central tendency distinct from the mean (average) and mode (most frequent value). The median is particularly robust to outliers—a key insight for later statistical work. Common procedural errors include: failing to sort before finding the middle, confusing the position calculation, miscounting when duplicates are present, and mishandling the division step for even datasets. Use datasets with 5–10 values for most practice; include both pre-sorted and unsorted data. For stretch, introduce frequency tables and grouped data. For support, use small odd-sized sets with no duplicates and pre-sorted data.

name: Decimal place value
masteryDefinition: Students can identify, name and understand the value of each digit in a decimal number to at least two decimal places. They can partition decimals into units, tenths and hundredths, compare and order decimals, and use place value to justify reasoning about decimal magnitude. They understand that 0.1 = 1/10 and 0.01 = 1/100, and can relate decimal notation to fractional equivalents.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring or misinterpreting the decimal point as a separator only
misconceptions.description: Students treat the decimal point as merely a separator between two unrelated whole numbers rather than a symbol denoting place value scaling. For example, they read 3.24 as 'three point twenty-four' and believe it is somehow between 3 and 4, or treat the digits after the point as a separate whole number (3.24 = 3 and 24, not 3 and 2-tenths and 4-hundredths). This leads to incorrect comparisons (e.g., 3.4 < 3.24 because 4 > 24 is read as '4 > 24').

misconceptions.label: Counting decimal places incorrectly or confusing place value column names
misconceptions.description: Students miscounts place values or conflates tenths with tens, or hundredths with hundreds. For example, they believe 0.3 is in the 'tens' place, or claim that 2.45 has a '4 in the tens place'. They may also confuse the direction of place value scaling, thinking that moving right increases place value magnitude instead of decreasing it, or that 0.05 is larger than 0.5.

misconceptions.label: Assuming trailing zeros do not affect decimal value or understanding
misconceptions.description: Students believe that 2.3 and 2.30 are 'different' decimals rather than equivalent, or claim that writing 0.10 changes the value. They may incorrectly compare or order decimals because they treat 0.3, 0.30 and 0.300 as distinct. This often stems from whole-number experience where trailing zeros (e.g., 10 vs 100) change magnitude.

misconceptions.label: Incorrectly partitioning decimals using additive errors
misconceptions.description: Students partition a decimal such as 2.47 as '2 + 4 + 7' instead of '2 + 0.4 + 0.07', or write partitions with incorrect place values (e.g., '2 + 0.04 + 0.7' for 2.47). They may also fail to recognise that 0.47 = 0.4 + 0.07 and instead write 0.47 = 0.4 + 0.7 or similar incorrect sums.

difficultyDimensions.easy: One decimal place (e.g., 3.5, 0.7)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Two or more decimal places with zeros in intermediate positions (e.g., 5.03, 0.401)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Magnitude of numbers and use of zero
difficultyDimensions.easy: Decimals between 0 and 10 with no leading or trailing zeros (e.g., 2.3, 7.8)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Decimals less than 1 with leading zeros, or numbers with internal zeros (e.g., 0.03, 4.02, 0.001)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Identifying a single digit's place value in a given decimal (e.g., 'What is the place value of the 4 in 2.45?')
difficultyDimensions.hard: Partitioning a decimal, comparing/ordering multiple decimals, or relating decimals to fractions in a single problem

transferContexts.example: A notebook costs £2.35. Identify the value of each digit and explain why this is different from £2.53. Write this cost as a sum of pounds and pence.
Express 0.6, 0.35 and 0.09 as fractions in simplest form, and arrange them in order. Explain how place value helps you convert between decimals and fractions.
A scientist records a plant's height as 3.42 cm. Compare this to another plant that is 3.24 cm tall. Use place value to explain which is taller and by how much.

generativeContext: Decimal place value is the foundation of understanding magnitude, ordering and equivalence in the decimal system. The key insight is that the decimal point marks a boundary: digits to the left represent whole units and increasing powers of 10; digits to the right represent fractional units (tenths, hundredths, etc.) in decreasing powers of 10. Each digit's contribution to the overall value depends on its position. Students must recognise that 0.1 = 1/10, 0.01 = 1/100, and so on. Common errors arise when students treat the decimal as a separator between two disconnected numbers, confuse the names or order of decimal places, fail to recognise equivalence (2.3 = 2.30), or partition decimals using whole-number logic (treating 2.47 as 2 + 4 + 7 rather than 2 + 0.4 + 0.07). When generating questions, vary the number of decimal places, the presence of zeros (leading, internal, trailing), the magnitude of the numbers, and whether the task involves identification, partitioning, comparison, ordering or conversion to fractions. Ensure misconception-targeting items explicitly test whether students understand place value scaling, can justify equivalence, and can connect decimals to their fractional meanings. Use concrete contexts (money, length) to anchor abstract understanding.

name: Compare decimals using =, ≠, <, >, ≤, ≥
masteryDefinition: Students can correctly compare two or more decimal numbers using all six relational symbols (=, ≠, <, >, ≤, ≥), including decimals with different numbers of decimal places, and can justify their comparisons by reference to place value or by converting to equivalent decimals.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring zeros after the decimal point
misconceptions.description: Students believe 0.5 and 0.50 are different, or think that 0.3 is less than 0.30. They do not recognise that trailing zeros do not change the value of a decimal.

misconceptions.label: Comparing decimal strings as integers
misconceptions.description: Students compare 0.45 and 0.5 by treating 45 and 5 as whole numbers, concluding 0.45 > 0.5 because 45 > 5. They ignore the place value of decimal digits.

misconceptions.label: Reversing the inequality symbol
misconceptions.description: Students write 3.2 < 2.8 when they mean 3.2 > 2.8, or confuse the direction of < and > symbols, particularly under time pressure or when switching between comparisons.

misconceptions.label: Assuming more decimal places means larger number
misconceptions.description: Students think 0.234 > 0.24 because it has three decimal places rather than two, without considering the actual magnitude of digits in each place.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Comparing decimals with the same number of decimal places (e.g. 0.35 vs 0.42)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Comparing decimals with different numbers of decimal places (e.g. 0.5 vs 0.48, 2.3 vs 2.31)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Comparing decimals that are far apart in magnitude (e.g. 0.1 vs 0.9)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Comparing decimals that are very close in value (e.g. 0.301 vs 0.31, or 4.199 vs 4.2)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Using only < or > symbols in straightforward comparisons
difficultyDimensions.hard: Using ≤ or ≥ in context, or choosing between all six symbols when ≠ is also an option

transferContexts.example: A recipe requires 0.75 cups of sugar. You have 0.8 cups available. Is this enough? Use ≥ or < to justify your answer.
Item A costs £3.45 and Item B costs £3.5. Which is cheaper? Write the comparison using the correct symbol.
Temperature readings are 12.3°C and 12.30°C. Are these equal? Explain why, then determine if 12.3 ≤ 12.31.

generativeContext: N1.7 requires secure understanding of place value extended to decimal positions. Students must recognise that 0.3, 0.30, and 0.300 are equivalent, and that each decimal digit's magnitude depends on its position (tenths, hundredths, thousandths, etc.). When comparing two decimals with different numbers of decimal places, students should either align them by appending zeros or use mental strategies based on place value to determine the larger value. Common errors stem from treating the digits after the decimal point as if they form a whole number (so 0.45 appears larger than 0.5 because 45 > 5), or from conflating more decimal places with larger magnitude. The six comparison symbols must be used precisely: = (equal value), ≠ (not equal), < (strictly less than), > (strictly greater than), ≤ (less than or equal to), ≥ (greater than or equal to). Emphasis should be placed on checking work by converting to the same number of decimal places or by reasoning about tenths, hundredths, etc. independently. This skill underpins later work on ordering, rounding, and estimation of decimals.

name: Order a list of decimals
masteryDefinition: Students can correctly order a set of decimal numbers (positive and negative, with varying decimal places) in ascending or descending order by comparing place values systematically, including cases where decimals have different numbers of decimal places.

misconceptions.label: Comparing decimals as whole numbers
misconceptions.description: Students treat the decimal part as a separate whole number and compare it without attending to place value. For example, ordering 3.7, 3.25, 3.9 as 3.25, 3.7, 3.9 because they compare 7, 25, 9 as if 25 is larger than 7 or 9.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring trailing zeros or assuming more decimal places means larger number
misconceptions.description: Students believe 3.2 < 3.20 or that 3.200 is larger than 3.2, not recognizing that 3.2 = 3.20 = 3.200. This leads to incorrect orderings when decimal places vary.

misconceptions.label: Ordering by number of decimal places rather than magnitude
misconceptions.description: Students assume that more decimal places means a larger number. For example, ordering 0.5, 0.15, 0.105 as 0.105 < 0.15 < 0.5 based on digit count rather than place value.

misconceptions.label: Confusion with negative decimals
misconceptions.description: Students struggle to order negative decimals correctly, often treating -3.7 as smaller than -3.2 without recognizing that -3.7 is further from zero and therefore smaller (more negative).

difficultyDimensions.easy: All decimals have the same number of decimal places (e.g., 2.34, 5.67, 1.89)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Decimals have varying numbers of decimal places, requiring conversion or careful comparison (e.g., 2.3, 2.07, 2.301)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Only positive decimals, all close in value and magnitude (e.g., 5.2, 5.4, 5.6)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Mix of positive and negative decimals with different magnitudes (e.g., -0.8, 3.2, -2.1, 0.4)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Values are well-spaced and easily distinguishable (e.g., 1.5, 3.2, 5.7)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Values are very close together, requiring careful place-value comparison (e.g., 2.301, 2.31, 2.3, 2.103)

transferContexts.context: Comparing weights or measurements in recipes and cooking
transferContexts.example: Order these ingredient weights from lightest to heaviest: 2.5 kg, 2.05 kg, 2.25 kg, 2.505 kg

transferContexts.context: Comparing prices or costs in financial contexts
transferContexts.example: Arrange these shop prices from cheapest to most expensive: £3.49, £3.94, £3.4, £3.409

transferContexts.context: Comparing athletic performance times or distances
transferContexts.example: Order these sprint times from fastest to slowest: 12.45 seconds, 12.4 seconds, 12.54 seconds, 12.405 seconds

generativeContext: N1.8 tests place-value understanding in decimal contexts. Students must recognize that each position (tenths, hundredths, thousandths) has fixed value regardless of how many decimal places are written. The skill requires systematic left-to-right comparison: first compare whole-number parts, then tenths, then hundredths, etc. Critical contexts: (1) varying decimal places—students must pad with zeros mentally or understand equivalence (3.2 = 3.20); (2) negative decimals—requires understanding that -3.7 is further left on the number line than -3.2; (3) proximity—when decimals are very close (2.301 vs 2.31), students cannot rely on quick approximation and must compare place by place. Avoid purely algorithmic approaches: students need conceptual understanding that 0.25 is not 'twenty-five' but 'twenty-five hundredths'. Generate questions with explicit place-value language and scaffold toward mixed-sign, mixed-precision lists. Common errors cluster around treating decimals as paired integers (digit-sequence comparison rather than magnitude comparison) and misunderstanding trailing zeros.

name: Position integers on a number line
masteryDefinition: Students can accurately place positive and negative integers on a number line, understanding that integers are whole numbers (positive, negative, or zero) and that position reflects magnitude and direction from zero. They can identify unlabelled positions and determine the integer value at any marked point, recognising that equal spacing represents equal intervals.

misconceptions.label: Treating negative numbers as smaller in magnitude
misconceptions.description: Students position negative integers closer to zero than their positive counterparts (e.g., placing -8 between 0 and -3), confusing the negative sign with smallness rather than direction. They may think -5 is 'less far' from zero than -2.

misconceptions.label: Reversing the direction of negative numbers
misconceptions.description: Students position negative integers to the right of zero instead of to the left, treating the number line as having no inherent direction, or assuming negative means 'add' rather than 'subtract' from zero.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring scale and spacing
misconceptions.description: Students place integers correctly in order but fail to maintain proportional spacing. For example, on a line marked 0, 10, 20, they position 5 and 15 without regard to the scale, placing 5 at the same relative distance from 0 as 15 is from 10.

misconceptions.label: Confusing position with the value of the integer
misconceptions.description: Students believe that the integer at a position is defined by its physical location on the page (e.g., 'this mark is 3 cm from the left, so it must be 3') rather than by the labelled scale and intervals.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Positive integers only, or small integers close to zero (e.g., -3 to 5)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Large positive and large negative integers with wide separation (e.g., -100 to 100)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Number line with all integers labelled (e.g., -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Number line with sparse labels and large intervals (e.g., only 0, 20, 40 marked; position -30)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Selecting from multiple-choice positions for a given integer
difficultyDimensions.hard: Identifying the integer at an unmarked position given only two reference points

transferContexts.example: A thermometer shows -5°C. If the temperature drops by 3°C, where is the new temperature positioned on a thermometer number line from -10 to 10?
A submarine is at -40 metres below sea level. Mark its position on a vertical number line showing depths from -100 to 20 metres, then identify the position of a plane at 500 metres above sea level.
A shop has a profit of £15 one day and a loss of £8 the next. Position both values on a number line from -50 to 50, identifying which day was further from break-even.

generativeContext: N1.9 focuses on the spatial and directional representation of integers. Students must integrate three concepts: (1) integers as abstract quantities with sign and magnitude; (2) the number line as a scaled, directional model with zero as an origin; (3) proportional reasoning about spacing. Misconceptions often stem from treating negative as a qualifier of size rather than direction, or treating the number line as a visual-spatial object disconnected from its numerical structure. Vary difficulty by (a) changing the range and separation of reference points, (b) requiring interpolation between marked points, (c) using integers with large magnitudes, (d) asking students to justify spacing. Avoid conflating 'position on number line' with 'distance from zero' – emphasise signed position. Use transfer contexts (temperature, elevation, time, money, scoring) to anchor the number line in real contexts where direction matters. Check for spacing consistency: incorrect scaling is a subtle form of non-mastery that multiple-choice answers may hide.

name: Rounding to the nearest 10, 100, 1000, integer
masteryDefinition: Students can reliably round whole numbers and decimals to a specified place value (nearest 10, 100, 1000, or to a given number of decimal places) using the standard rounding rule: identify the digit in the target place, look at the digit immediately to the right, round up if ≥5, round down if <5, and correctly adjust all digits to the right of the target place.

misconceptions.label: Always rounding 5 up
misconceptions.description: Student believes that any digit 5 or above in the rounding position always rounds up, but fails to apply the 'round half to even' or standard convention consistently, or rounds 5 up without understanding it's a convention boundary. This is reinforced by colloquial speech ('5 and above give it a shove').

misconceptions.label: Looking at the wrong digit
misconceptions.description: Student looks at the digit in the target place value (instead of the digit to the right of it) to decide whether to round up or down. For example, when rounding 347 to the nearest 10, they look at the 4 rather than the 7, leading to 340 instead of 350.

misconceptions.label: Not replacing rounded digits with zeros
misconceptions.description: Student correctly identifies which way to round but fails to replace digits to the right of the target place with zeros. For example, rounding 2847 to the nearest 100 gives 2847 or 285 instead of 2800.

misconceptions.label: Confusing place value columns when rounding decimals
misconceptions.description: When rounding decimals, student miscounts decimal places or confuses which digit is in the target column. For example, rounding 3.6742 to 1 d.p. by looking at the 4 instead of the 7, or treating the integer part as a decimal place.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Rounding to the nearest 10 (e.g. 27 → 30)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Rounding to the nearest 1000 or to decimal places (e.g. 2847 → 3000, or 4.6382 → 4.6)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Rounding numbers where the deciding digit is clearly <5 or clearly >5 (e.g. 42, 58)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Rounding numbers where the deciding digit is exactly 5, or where the result requires carrying/cascade changes (e.g. 349 → 350)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Rounding whole numbers to nearest 10 or 100
difficultyDimensions.hard: Rounding decimals to a stated number of decimal places, or rounding negative numbers, or mixed numbers

transferContexts.example: A shop assistant counts 287 items in stock. Round this to the nearest 10 to give a quick estimate. What is 287 rounded to the nearest 10?
A scientist measures a length as 4.6738 cm. Round this measurement to 1 decimal place. What do you write on the lab report?
A bill comes to £23.67. If you round to the nearest pound to estimate how much cash to bring, what is the rounded amount?

generativeContext: N1.10 involves the systematic application of place-value understanding to compress numbers to a coarser granularity. The core operation is: (1) identify the target column, (2) inspect the digit one place to the right, (3) apply the comparison rule (≥5 → round up, <5 → round down), (4) set all digits to the right of target to zero (for integers) or truncate (for decimals). Students must handle the boundary case of 5 carefully, and understand why we use this rule conventionally. The skill applies to both whole numbers and decimals, and requires secure place-value language ('nearest 100' vs 'to 1 d.p.'). Common errors include using the wrong digit for inspection, not zeroing/truncating properly, and misapplying the ≥5 rule. The skill transfers to estimation, measurement rounding, and data reporting in science and statistics. Contextualise via money, population counts, and measurement scenarios to ground the convention in purpose.

name: Position decimals on a number line (incl midpoint using a calculator)
masteryDefinition: Students can accurately place decimal numbers on a number line with appropriate scale and precision, identify positions of given decimals, find midpoints between two decimals using a calculator, and understand that decimal position reflects place value magnitude.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring scale equivalence
misconceptions.description: Students treat each division on a number line as representing 1 unit regardless of the span shown. For example, on a line from 0 to 10 with 5 divisions, they assume each division is 1 rather than 2. This leads to placing 3.5 at the wrong position.

misconceptions.label: Confusing decimal position with digit value
misconceptions.description: Students believe 2.3 should be placed closer to 3 than to 2 because they focus on the digit 3 rather than understanding 2.3 is only 0.3 away from 2. This typically manifests when placing decimals between whole numbers.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect midpoint calculation using calculator
misconceptions.description: Students add the two decimal numbers instead of finding the mean (adding then dividing by 2). For instance, finding midpoint of 1.2 and 2.8, they calculate 1.2 + 2.8 = 4 rather than (1.2 + 2.8) ÷ 2 = 2.

misconceptions.label: Treating decimals as separate integers
misconceptions.description: Students view 0.7 and 0.9 as unrelated points rather than understanding both lie between 0 and 1, leading to incorrect comparisons or placements. They may struggle to order decimals like 0.23, 0.3, 0.32 on the same line.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Number lines spanning 0–10 or 0–1 with clearly marked unit intervals
difficultyDimensions.hard: Number lines with non-unit scales (e.g., 2.5–7.8) or compressed scales requiring fractional division interpretation

difficultyDimensions.easy: One decimal place (e.g., 3.2, 5.7) on number lines with divisions at 0.1 intervals
difficultyDimensions.hard: Two or three decimal places (e.g., 2.47, 0.036) with unmarked intermediate positions or scaled differently

difficultyDimensions.easy: Decimals far from whole numbers (e.g., 0.9 on 0–1 line) or near marked divisions
difficultyDimensions.hard: Decimals between unmarked divisions requiring estimation or those very close together (e.g., 2.34 and 2.36)

transferContexts.example: A thermometer shows temperatures between 20°C and 30°C with 5 divisions. Where should 23.5°C be marked? A rainfall chart marks 0mm and 100mm; position 47.3mm.
Heights of students recorded as 1.52m, 1.68m, 1.45m must be positioned on a line from 1.4m to 1.7m to compare and find the median height.
Stock prices recorded as £3.47, £3.52, £3.61 over three days. Position on a line from £3.40 to £3.70 and find the midpoint price using a calculator.

generativeContext: Position decimals on a number line requires students to coordinate two conceptual layers: (1) understanding the scale of the line (the 'ruler' structure) and (2) locating a decimal value at the correct proportion within that span. Mastery involves recognizing that a number line is a proportional representation where equal physical distances represent equal numerical distances. When students are asked to find a midpoint using a calculator, they must combine addition and division operations in correct order: sum the endpoints, then divide by 2. Common errors arise from: not adjusting perception when scale changes (every division ≠ 1 unit), treating the decimal portion in isolation from its distance from the whole number, and procedural errors in calculator use (forgetting the ÷2 step). Vary difficulty by changing span magnitude (0–1 vs 0–100), decimal precision (1 or 2 d.p.), mark density (all divisions vs some unmarked), and spatial proximity (well-separated vs tightly clustered values). Emphasize the place value structure underlying decimal position: 0.7 is 7 tenths, placing it 7/10 of the way from 0 to 1, not arbitrarily near 7.

name: Rounding to decimal places
masteryDefinition: Students can round any decimal number to a specified number of decimal places by identifying the digit in the target place, examining the digit immediately to the right, and applying the rounding rule (5 and above rounds up, below 5 rounds down). They understand that rounding truncates unwanted digits and can justify their rounding decisions with reference to place value.

misconceptions.label: Rounding up every digit after the required place
misconceptions.description: Student rounds 3.2456 to 2 d.p. as 3.25 instead of 3.25 because they round 4 up to 5, then round the 5 up to 6. They apply the rounding rule multiple times sequentially rather than looking only at the single digit immediately after the target place.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring the digit after the target place entirely
misconceptions.description: Student rounds 2.749 to 1 d.p. as 2.7 by simply deleting everything after the first decimal place without checking the next digit. They confuse rounding with truncation.

misconceptions.label: Treating the rounding rule as 'round up at 4'
misconceptions.description: Student rounds 3.142 to 1 d.p. as 3.2 because they believe any digit 4 or above triggers rounding up, rather than understanding the boundary at 5. Often stems from misremembering the rule or confusing it with significant figures conventions.

misconceptions.label: Rounding by considering all digits after the target place as a single entity
misconceptions.description: Student rounds 5.2489 to 1 d.p. by treating '489' as a number and rounding based on whether 489 > 500, leading to 5.3, rather than looking only at the first digit after the target (4). They create a false composite digit.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of decimal places in the original number
difficultyDimensions.easy: Rounding 3.45 to 1 d.p. (only one digit after target place)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Rounding 0.000472 to 3 d.p. (many zeros followed by significant figures)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Position of the critical digit relative to the rounding boundary
difficultyDimensions.easy: Rounding 2.36 to 1 d.p. (digit is 6, clearly above 5)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Rounding 4.15 to 1 d.p. (digit is exactly 5, requires knowledge of tie-breaking rule)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Size and context of the original number
difficultyDimensions.easy: Rounding simple decimals like 1.234 to 2 d.p.
difficultyDimensions.hard: Rounding numbers in context, e.g. 0.00567 in a scientific or currency scenario to 2 d.p.

transferContexts.context: Financial/currency contexts where precise monetary amounts must be expressed to 2 d.p.
transferContexts.example: A shop calculates the cost of an item as £3.456. Round this to the nearest penny (2 d.p.).

transferContexts.context: Measurement and science, where readings are recorded to a specified precision
transferContexts.example: A thermometer shows 36.7834°C. Round this temperature to 1 d.p. for recording in a lab notebook.

transferContexts.context: Data presentation and reporting, where averages or test scores must fit a standard format
transferContexts.example: A student's average score is 87.456%. Round this to 1 d.p. for the school report.

generativeContext: N1.12 is the foundational procedural skill for rounding decimals to a specified number of decimal places. Core procedure: (1) Identify the target decimal place (e.g., if rounding to 2 d.p., look at the second digit after the decimal point). (2) Examine the digit immediately to the right of the target place (the 'critical digit'). (3) If critical digit ≥ 5, round the target digit up by 1; if critical digit < 5, leave the target digit unchanged. (4) Delete all digits to the right of the target place. Emphasise that we only ever look at one digit (the one immediately after), not multiple digits. Common error: students round sequentially through each digit, or they truncate without checking the next digit. Rounding is distinct from truncation and from significant figures. Use place value language: 'the digit in the hundredths place' not just 'the second decimal.' Connect to real contexts early (money, measurements) to anchor meaning. For students struggling, use a number line or place value grid to visualize why 3.142 is closer to 3.1 than 3.2. Tie-breaking rule (5 → round up) should be stated explicitly. Practice should progress from single-step rounding (3.45 → 1 d.p.) to nested contexts (given a measurement, round to a specified precision in a word problem).

name: Position negatives on a number line
masteryDefinition: Students can accurately place negative integers on a number line, understanding that negative numbers are ordered opposites of positive numbers with zero as the reference point. They can read positions of negative numbers on unmarked and marked number lines, compare negative numbers by their position, and explain why a number further left is smaller. They recognise that the distance from zero determines magnitude regardless of sign.

misconceptions.label: Treating negatives as mirror without understanding ordering
misconceptions.description: Students place negative numbers on the correct side of zero but reverse the order, e.g. placing -1 to the left of -5, or thinking -5 is 'bigger' because 5 > 1. They may view negatives as purely a reflection without grasping that ordering is reversed: further left = smaller value.

misconceptions.label: Confusing negative position with negative distance
misconceptions.description: Students correctly identify that -3 is 3 units from zero but then claim -3 > -7 because '3 is closer to zero', conflating proximity to zero with size. They treat distance from zero as the value rather than understanding that -7 is actually smaller (further left) than -3.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring the negative sign when positioning
misconceptions.description: When given a number like -4, students position it at +4 on the line, essentially dropping the sign. This often happens when copying coordinates or when number lines lack clear labelling, and the student defaults to the positive version.

misconceptions.label: Assuming all negatives are smaller than zero by an arbitrary rule
misconceptions.description: Students accept that negatives go left of zero but have no principled understanding of comparative size. They may randomly order -2 and -8, or claim both are equally 'small' without recognising the magnitude difference.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Scale and labelling of number line
difficultyDimensions.easy: Fully labelled number line with every integer marked (e.g., -5, -4, -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2); scale of 1 unit per mark
difficultyDimensions.hard: Unlabelled or partially labelled line with larger intervals (e.g., scale of 5 or 10 units); student must infer positions between marked points

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Range and density of negatives to position
difficultyDimensions.easy: Single negative number or a small set of negatives close to zero (e.g., -1 to -3) with clear positive reference points
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple negatives spread across a wider range (e.g., -12, -3, -27, -5) requiring finer ordering and no obvious positive anchors

difficultyDimensions.easy: Place a given negative number on a prepared line
difficultyDimensions.hard: Compare two negatives, explain why one is smaller, or determine missing numbers in a sequence of negatives

transferContexts.example: A thermometer shows temperatures from -10°C to 10°C. Where would you position -4°C? Which is colder: -2°C or -8°C? Explain using the number line.
A diagram shows sea level as 0. A submarine is at -250 metres and a seabird is at 50 metres. Mark both positions on a vertical number line and state which is further from sea level.

transferContexts.context: Directed movement and change in position
transferContexts.example: A lift starts at ground floor (0). It goes down 3 floors to -3. Then down 2 more to -5. Draw a number line showing these positions and explain why -5 is lower than -3.

generativeContext: N1.13 focuses on the spatial and ordinal understanding of negative integers using the number line as a primary representational tool. The core conceptual demand is recognising that negative numbers form an ordered set where (a) zero is the reference point, (b) negative numbers extend indefinitely left, and (c) the further left a number sits, the smaller its value — a reversal of intuition from the natural numbers. Students must simultaneously develop: (i) procedural fluency in locating a given negative on a prepared or blank line, (ii) comparative reasoning (which of two negatives is smaller?), and (iii) the semantic link between position and magnitude. Common errors stem from treating the negative sign as a label rather than a directional operator, or from conflating distance from zero with numerical size. Avoid ambiguous notation; use consistently labelled lines with clear zero reference and integer marks. Connect early to contexts where 'below' or 'opposite' has intuitive meaning (temperature, depth, debt). Progression beyond this skill requires secure understanding of this ordering to enable integer arithmetic (addition and subtraction with negatives) and later coordinate geometry.

name: Compare negatives using =, ≠, <, >, ≤, ≥
masteryDefinition: Students can correctly order and compare negative integers and decimals using all six relational operators (=, ≠, <, >, ≤, ≥), understanding that negative numbers farther from zero are smaller, and can justify comparisons with reference to a number line or magnitude reasoning.

misconceptions.label: Treating negatives like positives (magnitude confusion)
misconceptions.description: Students compare only the absolute value and ignore the negative sign, concluding -7 < -2 is false because 7 > 2. They confuse 'larger magnitude' with 'larger number' when negatives are involved.

misconceptions.label: Reversing inequality direction inconsistently
misconceptions.description: Students correctly identify that -8 < -3 but then claim -8 > -3 in a different context, or flip the symbol direction without reason when working through a sequence of comparisons.

misconceptions.label: Confusing ≤ and ≥ with strict inequalities
misconceptions.description: Students treat -5 ≤ -5 as false, believing the 'or equal' component only applies to positive numbers, or they cannot distinguish between when to use ≤ versus < in a given context.

misconceptions.label: Spatial/directional error on number line
misconceptions.description: Students place negative numbers incorrectly on a number line (e.g. -2 to the right of -8) or confuse left/right direction with the comparison operators, leading to systematic reversal of answers.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Comparing integers far from zero (e.g. -50 and -1)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Comparing integers close to zero (e.g. -3 and -2, or -1 and 0)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Comparing whole negative integers only
difficultyDimensions.hard: Comparing negative decimals or fractions (e.g. -2.5, -3/4) alongside integers

difficultyDimensions.easy: Using strict inequality symbols (< or >) in isolated comparisons
difficultyDimensions.hard: Using non-strict symbols (≤ or ≥) or inequality symbols in word problems requiring interpretation

transferContexts.example: On Monday the temperature was -3°C, on Tuesday it was -7°C. Which day was colder? Use < or > to compare the temperatures.
A diver is at -15 m (below sea level) and a submarine is at -35 m. Which is deeper? Write the comparison using an inequality symbol.
Account A has a balance of -£50, Account B has a balance of -£20. Which account is more overdrawn? Use ≤ or ≥ to express which balances are at least as negative.

generativeContext: N1.14 focuses on the relational comparison of negative numbers. Students must internalise that on the number line, moving left (becoming more negative) makes numbers smaller, not 'bigger in magnitude'. The core challenge is decoupling the concept of 'size' (distance from zero) from 'order' (position on a number line). Key cognitive landmarks: -5 is to the left of -2, so -5 < -2; closer to zero means closer to positive, hence less negative. Operators =, ≠, <, >, ≤, ≥ must be applied consistently regardless of sign. Special attention: symmetric understanding that if -8 < -3 then -3 > -8. Use number line visualisation heavily in generation. Misconception n1-14-m1 (magnitude confusion) is the dominant error—generate adversarial examples where magnitude and order conflict (e.g. -0.5 vs -10). Misconception n1-14-m3 (confusion with ≤ and ≥) requires explicit boundary cases like -5 ≤ -5. Avoid mixing with addition/subtraction; focus purely on comparison. Expect students to reason: 'Which is further left on the number line?' Generate contexts (temperature, altitude, debt) that reinforce negative as 'worse' or 'lower', strengthening the directionality intuition.

name: Order any integers, negatives and decimals
masteryDefinition: Students can correctly order a set of integers, negative numbers, and decimals (to any number of decimal places) in ascending or descending order, using inequality symbols appropriately and explaining their reasoning with reference to place value and magnitude on a number line.

misconceptions.label: Treating negative numbers as if the numeral magnitude determines order
misconceptions.description: Students order negatives by absolute value, e.g., placing −8 before −3 because 8 > 3, ignoring that −8 is further left on the number line. Arises from conflating 'size of number' with 'distance from zero'.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring place value when comparing decimals with different lengths
misconceptions.description: Students compare 0.5 and 0.05 by treating them as 5 and 5, or append zeros without understanding value equivalence, e.g., 0.5 = 0.50 but then confuse 0.5 with 0.05. May also incorrectly state 0.5 < 0.05 because '5' appears in the hundredths column.

misconceptions.label: Applying whole number comparison rules to decimals
misconceptions.description: Students order decimals by comparing digit count or reading them as whole numbers, e.g., stating 0.7 < 0.65 because '7 > 65 is false' or reading 0.7 as 'seven' and 0.65 as 'sixty-five'. Reflects failure to recognise decimal point as a separator that fundamentally changes place value.

misconceptions.label: Assuming negative decimals or small-magnitude negatives are 'greater' than larger negatives
misconceptions.description: Students order −0.5 and −2 incorrectly, claiming −0.5 > −2 because 0.5 < 2, without recognising that −0.5 is closer to zero and therefore greater (further right on the number line).

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Presence and range of negative numbers
difficultyDimensions.easy: Ordering positive integers only, e.g., 3, 7, 12, 5
difficultyDimensions.hard: Ordering a mix including negative integers, negative decimals, and positive decimals with varying decimal places, e.g., −2.15, 0.5, −3, 1.08, −0.25

difficultyDimensions.easy: Decimals with equal number of places, e.g., 0.3, 0.7, 0.2
difficultyDimensions.hard: Decimals with different numbers of decimal places requiring place value alignment, e.g., 0.5, 0.05, 0.505, 0.055

difficultyDimensions.easy: Numbers well separated in value, all same sign, e.g., 1, 5, 9
difficultyDimensions.hard: Numbers close in magnitude straddling zero, e.g., −0.2, 0, 0.2, −0.02, with decimals requiring careful place value attention

transferContexts.example: Order these daily minimum temperatures: −2.5°C, 0.5°C, −0.8°C, 1.2°C, −3°C from coldest to warmest.

transferContexts.context: Financial contexts with debts and deposits
transferContexts.example: A bank statement shows: −£15.50 (overdraft), £0.75 (interest), −£2.30 (fee), £100.00 (deposit). Order from smallest to largest amount in the account.
Five athletes' 100m times were recorded as: 12.5s, 12.05s, 12.50s, 12.005s, 12.55s. Order from fastest to slowest.

generativeContext: N1.15 requires secure understanding of place value across integers, negatives, and decimals, and the ability to map numbers onto a mental or explicit number line. Key conceptual moves: (1) Negative numbers: reinforce that −8 < −3 because −8 is further left; use a number line constantly; emphasize that 'more negative' means smaller. (2) Decimals of different lengths: teach explicit alignment by place value (tenths, hundredths, etc.); 0.5 = 0.50, not 0.05; avoid digit-by-digit reading. (3) Mixed contexts: scaffold by first fixing all numbers to the same form (e.g., all to 2 d.p.) before comparing. Use inequality symbols and number line references to justify every ordering decision. Questions should mix sign changes, decimal place variation, and near-magnitude pairs to expose fragile reasoning. Avoid questions that only test one type (e.g., all decimals, all negatives) as these mask incomplete mastery.

name: Rounding to significant figures
masteryDefinition: Students can identify the required number of significant figures in a number, locate the rounding digit and the deciding digit, apply rounding rules correctly, and communicate their reasoning. They understand that all non-zero digits are significant, that zeros between non-zero digits are significant, that leading zeros are not significant, and that trailing zeros in the decimal part are significant. They can round to 1, 2, 3, or more significant figures and verify their answer makes sense in context.

misconceptions.label: Counting leading zeros as significant figures
misconceptions.description: Students incorrectly count leading zeros (zeros to the left of the first non-zero digit) as significant figures. For example, rounding 0.00456 to 2 sf, they count the leading zeros and arrive at 0.0046 instead of 0.0046 (or state there are 5 sf instead of 3).

misconceptions.label: Misidentifying the rounding digit position
misconceptions.description: Students locate the deciding digit (the one after the required sf) but round the wrong digit, or round multiple digits instead of just the rounding digit. For example, rounding 47382 to 2 sf, they round both the 7 and the 3, giving 4700 instead of 47000.

misconceptions.label: Discarding trailing zeros in the integer part after rounding
misconceptions.description: Students round correctly but then remove trailing zeros that are now part of the answer. For example, rounding 4872 to 2 sf, they correctly identify it rounds to 4900 but write 49 instead, losing the significant trailing zeros.

misconceptions.label: Treating significant figures as 'number of decimal places'
misconceptions.description: Students confuse significant figures with decimal places. For example, rounding 45.678 to 2 sf, they round to 2 decimal places instead, giving 45.68 rather than 46.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Number with first non-zero digit in the ones place or tens place (e.g. 4567 or 456.7)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Number with leading zeros requiring careful identification of the first significant figure (e.g. 0.00456 or 0.0789)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Presence and interpretation of trailing zeros
difficultyDimensions.easy: Rounding numbers with no trailing zeros in the original number (e.g. 4567 to 2 sf)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Rounding decimals where trailing zeros must be retained or deciding whether zeros are significant (e.g. 4.050 to 2 sf or 4050 to 2 sf)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Rounding to 1 significant figure
difficultyDimensions.hard: Rounding to 3 or 4 significant figures, especially when the deciding digit is small (e.g. 0.0045678 to 3 sf)

transferContexts.context: Scientific notation and very large or very small numbers
transferContexts.example: The radius of an atom is approximately 0.000000000053 m. Round this to 2 significant figures and write the answer in standard form.
A student measures the mass of a substance as 12.456 g using a balance accurate to the nearest 0.01 g. Round the measurement to an appropriate number of significant figures.
A calculation gives 47382.6 for the number of people at an event. Round to 2 significant figures to estimate the attendance order of magnitude.

generativeContext: Significant figures represent the number of meaningful digits in a number, reflecting the precision of measurement or calculation. The first significant figure is the first non-zero digit reading from left to right; all digits from that point onward (including zeros between non-zero digits) count as significant, except leading zeros which never count. Trailing zeros after the decimal point are always significant; trailing zeros in whole numbers may or may not be significant depending on context and notation (use scientific notation to remove ambiguity). To round to n significant figures: (1) count from the leftmost non-zero digit and mark the nth digit (the rounding digit); (2) look at the digit immediately after it (the deciding digit); (3) if the deciding digit is 5 or more, round up; if 4 or less, round down; (4) replace all digits after the rounding digit with zeros (or drop them if they were after a decimal point), preserving place value. Common errors involve miscounting zeros at the start, rounding the wrong position, dropping necessary trailing zeros, or confusing sf with dp. Stretch students by asking them to round very small decimals, to identify significant figures in numbers with ambiguous trailing zeros, to explain why 0.0050 rounded to 1 sf is 0.005 (not 0.01), and to apply sf in multi-step problems where precision compounds.

name: Write 10, 100, 1000 etc. as powers of 10
masteryDefinition: Students can fluently convert standard form whole numbers that are powers of 10 (10, 100, 1000, 10000, etc.) into exponential notation (10¹, 10², 10³, 10⁴, etc.), understand that the exponent represents the number of times 10 is multiplied by itself, and can reverse the process to convert from exponential to standard form. They can also extend this to negative powers of 10 (10⁻¹, 10⁻², etc.) representing decimals, and recognize patterns in place value.

misconceptions.label: Confusing exponent with the number of zeros
misconceptions.description: Students write 1000 = 10⁴ instead of 10³, counting the zeros in the number itself rather than recognizing that 10³ means 10 × 10 × 10. They treat the exponent as 'count the zeros' rather than 'how many times 10 is a factor'.

misconceptions.label: Treating 10¹ as 10 × 1 instead of just 10
misconceptions.description: Students misinterpret the exponent 1 and write 10¹ = 10 × 1 = 10 correctly by accident, but don't understand that 10¹ = 10 because the base appears once. This reveals itself when they struggle to explain why 10⁰ should equal 1.

misconceptions.label: Adding the exponent to the base instead of multiplying
misconceptions.description: Students interpret 10³ as 10 + 3 = 13, or confuse powers with repeated addition. This often emerges when students have weak understanding of the difference between powers and products.

misconceptions.label: Reversing the base and exponent when converting from standard form
misconceptions.description: Students write 100 = 2¹⁰ instead of 10² because they invert the relationship, sometimes reading '100' as 'ten squared' but then writing '2 to the ten'. More commonly seen when students conflate different bases.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Converting 10 and 100 (10¹, 10²)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Converting 1000000 or larger, or working with 10⁰ and negative powers (10⁻¹, 10⁻²)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Converting standard form to exponential notation (e.g., 1000 → 10³)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Converting exponential notation to standard form, especially with negative exponents or larger positive exponents (e.g., 10⁵ → 100000)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Counting zeros and matching to exponent in familiar cases
difficultyDimensions.hard: Understanding why 10⁰ = 1, explaining the meaning of negative exponents in decimal contexts, or generalizing the pattern to other bases

transferContexts.context: Scientific notation and very large numbers
transferContexts.example: The population of Earth is approximately 8 × 10⁹. Write out what 10⁹ means in standard form and explain why we use this notation.

transferContexts.context: Decimal place value and small numbers
transferContexts.example: Write 0.001 as a power of 10 (recognizing it as 10⁻³) and explain what the negative exponent tells you about the position of the digit.
A kilogram is 10³ grams and a milligram is 10⁻³ grams. Use this to explain the relationship between these units and convert between them.

generativeContext: This is a foundational power-of-10 skill that bridges arithmetic and algebra. Students must recognize that powers of 10 are a special case where the exponent directly encodes place value. The core insight is that 10ⁿ means '10 multiplied by itself n times' (for positive n), which equals 1 followed by n zeros. Students should internalize the 'one-to-one' mapping: the exponent equals the number of zeros in standard form for powers of 10 only—this does not generalize to other bases. Conversely, 10⁻ⁿ represents a decimal with the first significant digit in the nth place after the decimal point. Avoid conflating this with counting digits; instead emphasize repeated multiplication and factors. When generating questions, vary the conversion direction, include edge cases (10⁰, 10⁻¹), and use realistic contexts (scientific notation, metric prefixes) to deepen transfer. Probe understanding by asking 'why' questions: 'Why is 10² equal to 100, not 20?' and 'What does the 2 in 10² actually mean?' Students who confuse the exponent with zeros or misinterpret it as addition need explicit scaffolding using base-10 blocks or repeated-multiplication diagrams before moving to larger exponents or negative powers.

name: Write positive integers in the form A × 10^n
masteryDefinition: Students can express any positive integer as a product of a number between 1 and 10 (or sometimes 1 and 100) and a power of 10, understanding that this representation preserves the value of the original number. They can identify both the coefficient A and the exponent n correctly, and verify their answer by expanding back to the original integer.

misconceptions.label: Coefficient outside the range [1, 10)
misconceptions.description: Students write the coefficient A as a number larger than 10 or smaller than 1. For example, writing 3500 as 35 × 10² instead of 3.5 × 10³, or writing 420 as 0.42 × 10³ instead of 4.2 × 10².

misconceptions.label: Incorrect exponent due to misplacing decimal point
misconceptions.description: Students count digit positions incorrectly when determining the exponent. For example, writing 7000 as 7 × 10⁴ instead of 7 × 10³, or confusing how many places the decimal point moves with the exponent value.

misconceptions.label: Using negative or zero exponent for whole numbers
misconceptions.description: Students apply rules from standard form with decimals inappropriately to whole numbers, writing 450 as 4.5 × 10⁻¹ or 450 as 45 × 10⁰, misunderstanding that positive integers always require positive exponents.

misconceptions.label: Not recognizing that A must be a single digit followed by decimal places
misconceptions.description: Students fail to move the decimal point far enough or treat multi-digit numbers as the coefficient. For example, writing 5600 as 56 × 10² instead of 5.6 × 10³, or writing 89000 as 89 × 10³ instead of 8.9 × 10⁴.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Four-digit numbers like 2500 or 6400
difficultyDimensions.hard: Seven or eight-digit numbers like 45000000 or 123000000

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Presence of trailing zeros vs non-zero digits
difficultyDimensions.easy: Numbers with all trailing zeros (e.g. 3000, 50000)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Numbers with mixed non-zero and zero digits (e.g. 4305, 62040)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Coefficients that are whole numbers or simple decimals (e.g. 5 × 10⁴, 3.2 × 10³)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Coefficients with multiple decimal places or that require careful rounding (e.g. 9.87 × 10⁵)

transferContexts.context: Scientific notation in physics and astronomy contexts
transferContexts.example: The mass of Earth is approximately 5,972,000,000,000,000,000,000,000 kg. Express this in the form A × 10^n.

transferContexts.context: Large quantities in real-world data (populations, distances, digital storage)
transferContexts.example: The population of India is approximately 1,400,000,000. Write this in the form A × 10^n and identify how many complete millions this represents.

transferContexts.context: Comparing and ordering very large numbers
transferContexts.example: Write 45,000,000 and 4,500,000 in the form A × 10^n, then explain which is larger and by how much.

generativeContext: N1.18 is a foundational skill for scientific notation at KS3. Students must internalize that A × 10^n is a compressed representation of place value: the exponent n tells you how many places the decimal point has moved from A to produce the original integer. The coefficient A must always satisfy 1 ≤ A < 10 (standard convention). Key misconceptions stem from: (1) imprecise decimal-point counting (off-by-one errors in exponent), (2) treating the coefficient as if it can be any non-zero number, and (3) confusing this with rules for negative exponents used in decimals. When generating questions, vary the number of digits and the distribution of zeros. Scaffold from 3–4 digit numbers with obvious place value (like 5000) to 5+ digit numbers with mixed zeros and non-zero digits. Require students to verify by expanding A × 10^n back to the original integer. Stretch tasks should include: (a) finding A and n when given a word problem requiring conversion to large-number form, (b) comparing or operating on two numbers given in A × 10^n form, or (c) identifying which representation is correct from a set of alternatives with intentional distractors matching common misconceptions.

name: Understand negative powers of 10
masteryDefinition: Students can evaluate negative powers of 10 (10^-1, 10^-2, 10^-3, etc.) as decimal fractions, understand the relationship between the exponent and the denominator of the equivalent fraction, interpret 10^-n as 1/(10^n), and use this understanding to convert between exponential and decimal notation. They recognise that negative powers produce values between 0 and 1, and can apply this to contexts involving place value and scientific notation.

misconceptions.label: Treating negative exponent as negating the base
misconceptions.description: Students evaluate 10^-2 as -100 instead of 0.01, treating the negative sign as applying to the base value rather than inverting it. This reflects confusion between the operation of negation and the operation of taking a reciprocal.

misconceptions.label: Counting decimal places incorrectly from the decimal point
misconceptions.description: Students write 10^-3 as 0.001 correctly but miscount when interpreting the relationship, thinking the exponent tells them how many digits appear after the decimal point rather than understanding it as the denominator in a fraction (1/1000). This leads to errors when extending to non-base-10 negative powers.

misconceptions.label: Assuming negative powers always produce negative numbers
misconceptions.description: Students believe that any negative exponent must result in a negative answer, not recognising that 10^-n = 1/(10^n) is always positive. They may write 10^-2 = -0.01 or confuse sign with magnitude.

misconceptions.label: Confusing negative exponent with moving the decimal point in the wrong direction
misconceptions.description: Students understand that negative powers involve decimals but apply the decimal point shift in the opposite direction, e.g. evaluating 10^-2 as 100 (moving right instead of left), or treating it as equivalent to multiplying by 10 rather than dividing.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Small negative exponents (10^-1, 10^-2) where the decimal representation is accessible via place value understanding
difficultyDimensions.hard: Larger negative exponents (10^-5, 10^-6) where counting and visualising decimal places becomes cognitively demanding and fraction equivalence must be more abstract

difficultyDimensions.easy: Converting from exponential notation to decimal form (evaluate 10^-3)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Converting from decimal or fraction form back to exponential notation, or comparing magnitudes across multiple representations

difficultyDimensions.easy: Pure number questions with explicit focus on the power notation and its decimal equivalent
difficultyDimensions.hard: Scientific notation contexts (e.g. 2.5 × 10^-4) or real-world scales (particle sizes, wavelengths) where negative powers represent very small quantities

transferContexts.example: Express the diameter of a red blood cell, approximately 0.000007 metres, in scientific notation using a negative power of 10.

transferContexts.context: Understanding place value and decimal ordering
