In a trapezium with parallel sides 6 cm and 10 cm, and equal slant sides 4 cm each, they might calculate 2 × 6 + 2 × 4 = 20, ignoring the second parallel side, or 2 × 10 + 4 + 4 = 28, missing that one parallel side is already counted.

misconceptions.label: Confusing perimeter with area or using incomplete diagrams
misconceptions.description: Students add only the sides shown with measurements on a diagram, ignoring that an unlabelled equal side must be the same length as its pair. For example, if an isosceles triangle diagram shows only the base and one slant side labelled, students might add just those two sides instead of recognising and including the second equal slant side.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Isosceles shapes where two sides are labelled (students can visually identify the pair); triangle with base and one slant side marked
difficultyDimensions.hard: Isosceles shapes where only one side length is labelled and students must infer which sides are equal; trapezium where slant sides are unlabelled but stated to be equal

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single-digit or small two-digit side lengths (e.g. 5 cm, 7 cm, 12 cm) with no regrouping in addition
difficultyDimensions.hard: Larger or decimal side lengths (e.g. 15.8 cm, 24 cm) requiring careful column addition or decimal place alignment

difficultyDimensions.easy: Fully labelled diagrams with all sides clearly marked with measurements
difficultyDimensions.hard: Partially labelled diagrams, word problems describing measurements, or abstract descriptions requiring students to construct their own mental model of the shape

transferContexts.context: Perimeter of composite shapes made from triangles and trapeziums
transferContexts.example: A shape is formed by joining an isosceles triangle (base 8 cm, equal sides 6 cm each) to an isosceles trapezium (parallel sides 8 cm and 12 cm, equal slant sides 5 cm each) along the triangle's base. What is the perimeter of the composite shape? (Students must recognise that the shared base is internal and not counted in the outer perimeter.)

transferContexts.context: Finding missing side lengths given the perimeter
transferContexts.example: An isosceles triangle has a perimeter of 24 cm and a base of 8 cm. What is the length of each of the equal sides? (Students must work backwards, subtracting the base from the perimeter and dividing by 2.)

transferContexts.context: Comparing perimeters of similar or scaled shapes
transferContexts.example: An isosceles trapezium has parallel sides of 6 cm and 10 cm with equal slant sides of 4 cm. A second isosceles trapezium has all side lengths doubled. What is the difference between their perimeters? (Students apply perimeter calculation and scaling reasoning.)

generativeContext: N2.12 focuses on the perimeter of two-dimensional figures with symmetry: isosceles triangles (three sides, two equal) and isosceles trapeziums (four sides, two parallel of different lengths, two non-parallel and equal). The core skill is identifying which sides are equal, summing all boundary lengths, and handling unlabelled or partially described figures. Key discriminators: (1) students must count every side, not just the equal pair; (2) they must distinguish isosceles from equilateral; (3) they must interpret both fully labelled diagrams and word problems that require inference of equal sides. Perimeter here is purely additive arithmetic; no area concepts or angle properties are involved. Misconceptions cluster around incomplete enumeration (forgetting the base or a parallel side), over-generalisation to regularity, and misinterpretation of sparse labelling. Difficulty scales with: number of distinct edge lengths to track, size and type of numerical values (decimals, larger numbers), and sparsity of labelling in the presented figure. Transfer scenarios include composite shapes (where internal edges must be excluded), inverse problems (perimeter given, side unknown), and scaling contexts (proportional doubling of all sides). Generation should produce authentic KS3 items: diagrams with strategic label placement (some sides shown, equal pairs inferred), word-based descriptions with or without visual aid, and mixed-format problems combining perimeter calculation with geometric reasoning.

name: Perimeter of a compound shape
masteryDefinition: Students can accurately find the perimeter of compound (rectilinear) shapes by identifying all outer edges, handling both given and missing dimensions, and summing lengths correctly. They understand that perimeter is the total distance around a shape's boundary and can decompose compound shapes to find unlabelled sides using properties of rectangles.

misconceptions.label: Including interior edges in perimeter
misconceptions.description: Students add the lengths of interior lines or indentations that are not part of the outer boundary. For example, in an L-shaped figure, they may count internal horizontal or vertical lines as part of the perimeter rather than recognizing these are interior divisions.

misconceptions.label: Failing to find missing side lengths
misconceptions.description: When a compound shape has unlabelled sides, students either ignore them or attempt to measure rather than using logical deduction. For example, in a rectilinear shape where one horizontal side is missing, they do not recognize that opposite sides of rectangles must be equal or that parallel sides must sum correctly.

misconceptions.label: Confusing perimeter with area
misconceptions.description: Students incorrectly attempt to multiply dimensions or calculate area when asked for perimeter, or mix the two concepts by adding dimensions in ways that are appropriate for area but not for finding the boundary length.

misconceptions.label: Misaligning compound shape decomposition
misconceptions.description: When decomposing a compound shape into rectangles to find missing sides, students incorrectly assume which sides are parallel or equal, leading to wrong calculations for unknown edges. For instance, treating a T-shape or inverted L-shape incorrectly by not properly aligning the sub-rectangles.

difficultyDimensions.easy: All sides labelled; student only sums given lengths
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple missing dimensions requiring use of parallel-side equality and rectangular properties to deduce lengths

difficultyDimensions.easy: Simple L-shape or T-shape with clear rectangular structure
difficultyDimensions.hard: Highly irregular rectilinear shapes with multiple indentations or step-like patterns requiring careful edge tracing

difficultyDimensions.easy: Grid-based compound shapes where students can count or clearly see all boundaries
difficultyDimensions.hard: Unlabelled diagram where students must infer scale and decompose the shape mentally without grid support

transferContexts.context: Real-world boundary problems such as finding fencing needed for an irregular garden or path
transferContexts.example: A garden shaped like an inverted L is 10 m long on one side and 6 m long on another, with a rectangular notch cut out (4 m × 2 m). How much fencing is needed to enclose the garden?

transferContexts.context: Compound shapes in architectural or design contexts where perimeter determines material costs
transferContexts.example: A floor plan shows a rectangular room with a rectangular alcove jutting inward. The outer rectangle is 8 m × 5 m and the alcove is 2 m × 3 m. Calculate the perimeter of the usable floor boundary.

transferContexts.context: Comparing perimeters of different compound shapes to evaluate efficiency
transferContexts.example: Two differently shaped play areas both have the same area but different perimeters. Which shape requires more boundary fencing, and why might designers choose one over the other?

generativeContext: Perimeter of a compound shape (rectilinear polygon) requires students to identify and sum all outer boundary segments. Key conceptual challenges: (1) Distinguishing outer edges from interior divisions or indentations—interior lines do not count; (2) Finding missing dimensions using the property that opposite sides of rectangles are equal and that the shape can be decomposed into constituent rectangles; (3) Careful sequential tracing of the perimeter boundary, especially around indentations. When generating questions, vary: the number of sides (starting with 6 sides for simple L-shapes up to 10+ sides for complex step patterns), the number of labelled vs. unlabelled dimensions (easy: all given; hard: 3–4 missing sides requiring deduction), the orientation and symmetry of the indentation, whether the shape is presented on a grid or as a line diagram, and whether students must also calculate missing dimensions before summing. Avoid questions that conflate perimeter with area or that require 3D thinking. Scaffold by first providing all dimensions, then requiring decomposition to find one missing dimension, then requiring multiple deductions. Use consistent units and avoid ambiguous diagrams where scale is unclear.

name: Solve problems involving tables and timetables
masteryDefinition: Students can extract information accurately from tables and timetables, calculate time intervals between events, identify patterns in schedules, and solve multi-step problems that require synthesising data from different parts of a table or cross-referencing multiple timetables. They can interpret 12-hour and 24-hour time formats, account for time zone or duration calculations, and communicate their reasoning clearly with reference to specific table entries.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect time interval calculation due to ignoring hour boundaries
misconceptions.description: Students calculate the duration between two times by subtracting minutes without regrouping when minutes in the start time exceed those in the end time. For example, calculating the time from 14:35 to 15:10 as 10 − 35 = −25 minutes (yielding a negative result or error) rather than recognising that 10 minutes past the next hour is 35 minutes after 14:35.

misconceptions.label: Confusion between 12-hour and 24-hour time notation when reading timetables
misconceptions.description: Students misinterpret times in 24-hour format, treating 13:45 as 1:45 p.m. in the afternoon without converting, or confusing am/pm labels with 24-hour times. They may also incorrectly assume that all timetables use the same format and fail to check which notation is being used.

misconceptions.label: Selecting wrong row or column due to careless row/column tracking
misconceptions.description: Students locate an entry in a table but read across the wrong row or down the wrong column, especially in large or densely formatted timetables. This occurs when no column/row headers are clearly highlighted or when the student fails to use a ruler or finger to trace a path across the table.

misconceptions.label: Failure to account for the structure of timetables when solving multi-step problems
misconceptions.description: Students extract a single data point correctly but do not recognise that a problem requires cross-referencing multiple entries (e.g. finding the earliest connection between two services) or understanding that some rows represent different days or routes, leading to incomplete or incorrect solutions.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of table entries and columns to consult
difficultyDimensions.easy: Single small table with ≤5 rows and ≤4 columns; answer found by locating one cell
difficultyDimensions.hard: Large timetable with ≥10 rows and ≥6 columns; answer requires cross-referencing multiple cells or combining data from two separate tables

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Time interval complexity and hour/minute regrouping required
difficultyDimensions.easy: Time intervals within a single hour (e.g. 14:10 to 14:55) or intervals that do not require borrowing
difficultyDimensions.hard: Intervals spanning multiple hours with minute subtraction requiring regrouping (e.g. 14:35 to 16:12), or problems involving overnight durations or time zone changes

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Clarity of table structure and labelling
difficultyDimensions.easy: Clearly labelled rows and columns with distinct headers and minimal visual clutter; single time format used throughout
difficultyDimensions.hard: Mixed time formats (12-hour and 24-hour in same table); weak or ambiguous headers; dense formatting with multiple timetables presented side-by-side

transferContexts.context: Planning a journey involving multiple transport modes using combined timetables
transferContexts.example: A bus timetable shows arrivals at different stops, and a train timetable shows departures. The student must find a bus service that arrives at the interchange with enough time to catch a specific train. How long after the bus arrives does the train depart?

transferContexts.context: Interpreting work schedules or staff rotas in employment contexts
transferContexts.example: A shop staff rota shows that three employees are scheduled across a week, each with different shift times. Calculate the total overlap time when all three staff are present on Thursday, or identify gaps when only one staff member is scheduled.

transferContexts.context: Analysing sports fixture or event schedules with constraints
transferContexts.example: A tournament fixture table shows match times and venues. A team needs to know if they have enough time to travel from one venue to another between consecutive matches. Use the table to determine if a 45-minute travel time is sufficient.

generativeContext: N2.14 requires students to treat tables and timetables as structured data sources from which they must extract, compare, and synthesise information to solve contextual problems. The skill spans four overlapping competencies: (1) accurate location and reading of entries in tables of varying sizes and clarity; (2) fluent calculation of elapsed time, including regrouping across hour boundaries; (3) interpretation of time notation (12-hour, 24-hour, am/pm) with confidence switching between formats; (4) problem decomposition—recognising when a single question requires multiple table lookups, cross-referencing, or filtering by constraints. Misconceptions cluster around arithmetic (subtraction without regrouping), notation confusion (12-hour vs 24-hour), and careless reading (row/column misalignment). Difficulty scales with table size, complexity of time arithmetic, visual clarity, and the number of independent table consultations needed. Real-world contexts—transport planning, work schedules, event timetables—are essential both to motivate the skill and to highlight why precision in reading and calculation matters. Problems should scaffold from single-lookup tasks (find the 10:30 train) through two-step location tasks (find the 10:30 train and check its arrival time) to multi-constraint problems (find the earliest train that arrives before 11:00 and departs at a station with a café). Encourage systematic annotation (circling headers, drawing lines) and explicit statement of which row/column is being consulted. Error diagnosis should focus on identifying whether mistakes stem from reading, arithmetic, notation confusion, or incomplete problem analysis.

name: Solve problems with frequency trees
masteryDefinition: Students can construct frequency trees from conditional probability scenarios, correctly interpret and extract data from existing trees, and use them to solve multi-step probability problems involving dependent events and conditional outcomes. They understand that branches represent outcomes with frequencies, can identify sample spaces accurately, and can calculate probabilities using tree information.

misconceptions.label: Confusing branch frequencies with probabilities
misconceptions.description: Students treat the numbers on branches as if they are probabilities (decimals/fractions out of 1) when they are actually frequencies or counts. For example, a branch labelled '15' is treated as 0.15 or 15% rather than 15 actual outcomes. This leads to incorrect calculations when converting between frequencies and probabilities.

misconceptions.label: Incorrectly multiplying frequencies at branches
misconceptions.description: Students multiply frequencies along paths as if performing probability multiplication, rather than recognising that path frequencies should be calculated by multiplying the fraction of the parent branch. For instance, if 100 people split into 60/40, and then 60 split into 35/25, students write '60 × 35' instead of finding 35 out of the 60.

misconceptions.label: Adding all final frequencies instead of selecting relevant outcomes
misconceptions.description: When asked to find the frequency of a specific outcome (e.g. 'how many chose option A and then option B?'), students add all endpoint frequencies rather than following the correct path through the tree and identifying the single relevant endpoint frequency.

misconceptions.label: Misinterpreting conditional splits based on preceding outcomes
misconceptions.description: Students fail to recognise that the second layer of branches must represent a different sample that depends on the first outcome. For example, they treat the second split as if it applies to the original total rather than only to the subset from the first branch, leading to impossible or inconsistent totals.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single split (one layer of branches)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Three or more sequential branches with multiple conditional splits

difficultyDimensions.easy: Small whole numbers (10–50 total outcomes) with simple splits like 50/50 or round ratios
difficultyDimensions.hard: Large frequencies (500+), non-obvious ratios (e.g. 37 out of 150), or percentages requiring conversion to counts

difficultyDimensions.easy: Direct reading: 'How many chose X?' where X is a single endpoint
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multi-step inference: 'Find the probability of X given Y' or 'compare frequencies across different branches'; requires summing or combining non-adjacent paths

transferContexts.example: In a school of 800 students, 480 chose sport and 320 chose music. Of those who chose sport, 288 also participate in a club; of those who chose music, 224 do. Draw a frequency tree and find how many students chose both sport and a club.

A doctor tests 1000 patients. 150 test positive for a condition. Of those who test positive, 120 actually have it (true positive). Of those who test negative, 830 do not have it. Use a frequency tree to find the total number who actually have the condition.

A factory produces 5000 items. 4500 pass initial inspection, 500 fail. Of those that pass, 4410 pass final quality check. Of those that fail initial inspection, 50 pass final check. Construct a frequency tree and determine the number of items that pass both stages.

generativeContext: Frequency trees are visual tools for organising conditional frequency data across sequential events. Each branch emanates from a node and is labelled with a frequency count (not a probability), representing how many items/people follow that path. The key conceptual challenge is that frequencies along paths multiply conditionally: if 200 people enter a first branch splitting into thirds, one third is roughly 67, not 200 × (1/3) treated as a decimal operation. Students must: (1) read endpoint frequencies correctly by following a single path from root to leaf; (2) understand that secondary branches split only the count from their parent node, not the total; (3) calculate probabilities post-hoc by dividing endpoint frequencies by the total sample. Common generation targets include constructing trees from given data, extracting endpoint frequencies, combining frequencies across multiple paths for a composite event, and converting tree data to conditional probability statements (e.g. P(outcome X | event Y)). Difficulty scales with tree depth, frequency magnitude, the presence of non-obvious splits, and demand for aggregation across multiple pathways rather than simple endpoint lookup.

name: Add and subtract numbers given in standard form
masteryDefinition: Students can fluently add and subtract numbers expressed in standard form (a × 10^n where 1 ≤ a < 10 and n is an integer), including those with different exponents. They understand the requirement to convert to the same power of 10 before operating, can handle negative exponents, and can express their final answer in standard form. They can justify their method and recognise when standard form arithmetic is appropriate.

misconceptions.label: Adding coefficients and exponents separately
misconceptions.description: Students treat standard form addition like multiplication: (3 × 10²) + (2 × 10³) = 5 × 10⁵. They add the mantissas and add the exponents rather than recognising they must first convert to the same power of 10.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring the need to standardise exponents
misconceptions.description: Students attempt to add or subtract without converting to a common exponent: (3 × 10²) + (2 × 10³) = 5 × 10³, adding mantissas directly without recognising the place-value mismatch.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect re-standardisation after operation
misconceptions.description: Students correctly convert to the same exponent and operate, but then fail to re-express in standard form: they write (5.2 × 10³) + (3.8 × 10³) = 9 × 10³ or = 90 × 10² instead of 9 × 10³, or they write 15 × 10⁴ without adjusting to 1.5 × 10⁵.

misconceptions.label: Mishandling negative exponents in subtraction
misconceptions.description: Students struggle with subtraction involving negative exponents, particularly when the minuend has a smaller (more negative) exponent than the subtrahend, or when the result requires careful place-value reasoning: (4 × 10⁻²) − (1.5 × 10⁻³).

difficultyDimensions.easy: Both numbers have the same exponent (e.g. (3.2 × 10⁴) + (1.8 × 10⁴))
difficultyDimensions.hard: Large difference in exponents requiring significant place-value shifts (e.g. (2 × 10⁵) + (3 × 10⁻²))

difficultyDimensions.easy: Positive exponents only with intuitive place values (e.g. 10², 10³)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Mix of positive and negative exponents requiring mental conversion between scales (e.g. (5 × 10⁻³) − (2 × 10⁻⁴))

difficultyDimensions.easy: Operation yields a mantissa between 1 and 10 naturally (e.g. (3 × 10⁴) + (2 × 10⁴) = 5 × 10⁴)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Result requires carrying or regrouping that forces re-standardisation (e.g. (6.8 × 10³) + (3.5 × 10³) = 10.3 × 10³ = 1.03 × 10⁴)

transferContexts.example: A planet has a mass of 6.4 × 10²³ kg and a moon has a mass of 7.3 × 10²² kg. What is the total mass of the planet and moon? Express your answer in standard form.
A hard drive contains 2.5 × 10¹¹ bytes of data. After backing up 1.8 × 10¹¹ bytes, how many bytes remain? Give your answer in standard form.
Two stars are separated by a distance of 3.2 × 10¹⁶ m. If a spacecraft travels 1.5 × 10¹⁵ m toward the first star, how far from the spacecraft to the first star is now? Express in standard form.

generativeContext: N2.16 focuses on arithmetic operations with numbers already in standard form. Mastery requires understanding that standard form is a positional notation system: 3 × 10² means 3 hundreds, and 2 × 10³ means 2 thousands—they cannot be combined directly. Students must convert both to a common exponent (choosing the larger one for clarity, or either for correctness), perform the arithmetic on mantissas, then verify the result is in standard form (1 ≤ a < 10). Subtraction demands careful attention to sign and borrowing. Negative exponents represent fractional place values (10⁻² = 1/100), so (5 × 10⁻²) − (3 × 10⁻³) requires converting to (50 × 10⁻³) − (3 × 10⁻³) = 47 × 10⁻³ = 4.7 × 10⁻². The skill is a stretch because it combines place-value reasoning, exponent laws, and arithmetic precision; errors cluster around exponent handling and re-standardisation. Generate questions that vary exponent alignment, force re-standardisation, and include at least one negative exponent to probe misconceptions n2-16-m1 through n2-16-m4.

name: Properties of multiplication and division
masteryDefinition: Students can identify and apply the commutative, associative, and distributive properties of multiplication; understand that division is not commutative or associative; and use these properties to simplify calculations and justify their reasoning.

misconceptions.label: Division is commutative
misconceptions.description: Student incorrectly believes that a ÷ b = b ÷ a. For example, claims 12 ÷ 3 = 3 ÷ 12, or that dividing by 2 is the same as multiplying by 2. Often stems from overgeneralisation of commutativity in addition and multiplication.

misconceptions.label: Misapplying distributive property with division
misconceptions.description: Student incorrectly distributes division across a sum or difference: e.g., (12 + 8) ÷ 4 = 12 ÷ 4 + 8 ÷ 4 is correct, but student also believes (12 + 8) ÷ (2 + 2) = 12 ÷ 2 + 8 ÷ 2, or attempts to 'distribute' division on the right: 20 ÷ (4 + 1) ≠ 20 ÷ 4 + 20 ÷ 1.

misconceptions.label: Confusing inverse operations with commutativity
misconceptions.description: Student conflates the idea that multiplication and division are inverses with the idea that they are commutative. For example, thinks 6 × 2 ÷ 2 = 2 ÷ 2 × 6 because 'you can do them in any order', rather than understanding left-to-right evaluation or that only the inverse property guarantees equivalence.

misconceptions.label: Incorrectly applying associativity to division chains
misconceptions.description: Student assumes (a ÷ b) ÷ c = a ÷ (b ÷ c). For example, (24 ÷ 4) ÷ 2 = 6 ÷ 2 = 3, but student calculates a ÷ (4 ÷ 2) = a ÷ 2, leading to 24 ÷ 2 = 12 instead. Stems from seeing division 'grouped with brackets' and assuming the same rule as multiplication holds.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of properties involved in single calculation
difficultyDimensions.easy: Single property (e.g. 7 × 4 = 4 × 7, using commutativity alone)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple properties chained (e.g. 15 × 8 = (5 × 3) × 8 = 5 × (3 × 8), requiring commutativity and associativity together)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Presence of division in the expression
difficultyDimensions.easy: Pure multiplication only (e.g. 6 × 5 × 2)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Mixed multiplication and division (e.g. 36 ÷ 6 × 2 or distributive property with division: (24 + 12) ÷ 4)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single-digit whole numbers (e.g. 3 × 2 × 5)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Variables or expressions (e.g. justify why 2(x + 3) = 2x + 6; or simplify 48 ÷ (8 ÷ 2))

transferContexts.context: Simplifying calculations using properties to find products and quotients efficiently
transferContexts.example: Use commutativity and associativity to work out 25 × 13 × 4 by rearranging as (25 × 4) × 13 = 100 × 13.

transferContexts.context: Algebra: justifying why expressions are equivalent using multiplicative properties
transferContexts.example: Show that 3(2x + 5) = 6x + 15 by applying the distributive property, and explain why 3(2x + 5) ≠ 6x + 5.

transferContexts.context: Problem-solving in area, scaling, and ratio contexts where reordering factors aids mental arithmetic
transferContexts.example: A rectangular garden is 8 m long and 25 m wide. Use properties of multiplication to find the area, then show how you could break this into a simpler calculation (e.g. 25 × 8 = 25 × 4 × 2).

generativeContext: This skill focuses on understanding and applying the four key properties of multiplication: commutativity (a × b = b × a), associativity ((a × b) × c = a × (b × c)), distributivity (a(b + c) = ab + ac), and the existence of multiplicative identity (a × 1 = a). Students must also grasp that division does not satisfy commutativity or associativity, and that the distributive property applies to division on the left side only (a ÷ (b + c) ≠ a ÷ b + a ÷ c in general). Key Year 7 competencies include: recognising when and why to rearrange factors to simplify mental arithmetic; using distributivity to expand brackets and factorise expressions; explaining equivalence of expressions using property notation; and identifying non-examples (e.g. why division chains are evaluated strictly left-to-right). High-frequency misconceptions stem from overgeneralising properties of addition/multiplication to division, or mechanically applying properties without understanding their constraints. Questions should probe both procedural fluency (e.g. simplify 24 ÷ 6 ÷ 2) and conceptual depth (e.g. 'Is (a ÷ b) ÷ c ever equal to a ÷ (b ÷ c)? Explain with an example'). Difficulty increases by introducing more operations per expression, moving from whole numbers to variables, and requiring justification rather than mere computation.

name: Mental strategies for multiplication and division
masteryDefinition: Students can fluently apply a range of mental strategies for multiplication and division, including partitioning, doubling/halving, using known facts, and the distributive property. They can select appropriate strategies based on the numbers involved, explain their method clearly, and apply these strategies to solve problems and missing number puzzles.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect application of the distributive property
misconceptions.description: Students partition one number correctly but then apply the operation incorrectly to one part. For example, calculating 23 × 4 as (20 × 4) + 3 = 83 instead of (20 × 4) + (3 × 4) = 92. They forget to multiply the second part by the multiplier.

misconceptions.label: Confusing halving and doubling in division
misconceptions.description: When using halving strategies for division, students halve the divisor instead of the dividend, or apply the operation inconsistently. For example, 48 ÷ 6 attempted as 48 ÷ 3 ÷ 2 without understanding why both dividend and divisor must be adjusted proportionally.

misconceptions.label: Mixing up factors and products in known facts
misconceptions.description: Students recall multiplication facts but apply them to the wrong part of the calculation. For example, knowing 6 × 7 = 42 but using it to calculate 60 × 7 as 42 × 10 instead of recognising 42 × 10 = 420, or confusing which numbers are being multiplied.

misconceptions.label: Not adjusting for multiples of 10 or powers of 10
misconceptions.description: Students apply mental strategies to single-digit or two-digit numbers but fail to adjust for place value. For example, calculating 34 × 20 as if it were 34 × 2, getting 68 instead of 680, or dividing by 30 without recognising the need to account for the zero.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single-digit multipliers or divisors with two-digit numbers (e.g. 23 × 4, 56 ÷ 7)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Two-digit multipliers, three-digit numbers, or numbers requiring multiple strategy steps (e.g. 47 × 12, 288 ÷ 12)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Using well-known facts (times tables up to 12 × 12) or simple halving/doubling (e.g. 25 × 4 via doubling twice)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Selecting from multiple viable strategies for the same problem, or combining strategies (e.g. 34 × 11 via partitioning and adjustment; 144 ÷ 6 via repeated halving and doubling)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Partitioning into tens and ones with single-digit multipliers (e.g. 34 × 5 = (30 × 5) + (4 × 5))
difficultyDimensions.hard: Partitioning two factors simultaneously or using non-standard partitions (e.g. 47 × 6 via (50 × 6) − (3 × 6); 125 × 8 via (100 × 8) + (25 × 8))

transferContexts.example: A shop sells pencils in packs of 6. How many pencils in 15 packs? (Students apply multiplication strategy 6 × 15 mentally using partitioning or known facts.)

transferContexts.context: Area and perimeter calculations with whole numbers
transferContexts.example: A rectangular garden measures 23m by 4m. What is the area? (Students use partitioning: 23 × 4 = (20 × 4) + (3 × 4) to find 92 m².)

transferContexts.context: Fair sharing and grouping in problem contexts
transferContexts.example: 156 sweets shared equally among 12 children. How many each? (Students apply mental division strategies, such as halving repeatedly or using known facts, to find 13.)

generativeContext: N3.2 focuses on developing flexible, efficient mental calculation for multiplication and division, underpinned by deep understanding of the distributive property, place value, and inverse relationships. Students should move beyond rote procedure toward strategy selection: recognising when to partition, when to use doubling/halving, when to apply known facts, and when to use compensation (adjusting for rounding). Key areas include: (1) Partitioning in multiplication—both partitioning the multiplicand (e.g. 34 × 5) and the multiplier (e.g. 25 × 12 via 25 × 10 + 25 × 2), and understanding that all parts must be multiplied by the full multiplier. (2) Doubling and halving for multiplication—e.g. 24 × 5 as 12 × 10, or 15 × 8 as 30 × 4, recognising the inverse relationship. (3) Division as inverse multiplication and via repeated halving/grouping—e.g. 64 ÷ 8 by knowing 8 × 8, or 48 ÷ 6 by halving to 24 ÷ 3, then 12 ÷ 1.5. (4) Place value awareness—ensuring strategies work for 23 × 4 and transfer to 230 × 4 and 23 × 40. Misconceptions arise when students: apply distributive property incompletely (forgetting to multiply all parts), confuse doubling/halving adjustments, misremember or misapply known facts, or ignore place value cues. Difficulty increases with number size, the need to select from multiple strategies, and the complexity of partitioning. Contextual applications include scaling, area, and fair-share problems where mental fluency frees cognitive load for problem-solving.

name: Multiply and divide integers and decimals by powers of 10
masteryDefinition: Students can fluently multiply and divide both integers and decimals by powers of 10 (10, 100, 1000, 0.1, 0.01, 0.001) by shifting the decimal point the correct number of places in the correct direction, and can explain why this works in terms of place value. They recognise that dividing by a power of 10 is equivalent to multiplying by the reciprocal power and vice versa.

misconceptions.label: Decimal point moves in wrong direction
misconceptions.description: Students move the decimal point left when multiplying (e.g., 3.5 × 100 = 0.035 instead of 350) or right when dividing (e.g., 24 ÷ 10 = 240 instead of 2.4). Often stems from confusion about which direction represents 'bigger' or 'smaller' numbers.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect number of place shifts
misconceptions.description: Students move the decimal point by the wrong number of places, often moving it by the number itself rather than by the number of zeros (e.g., 5.2 × 1000 = 5200 instead of 5200, or 45 ÷ 100 = 0.45 by moving 1 place instead of 2). Sometimes conflate the multiplier with the shift distance.

misconceptions.label: Treating decimal division as 'making numbers smaller always'
misconceptions.description: Students believe dividing always produces a smaller result and apply this rigidly, even when dividing by decimals less than 1 (e.g., 8 ÷ 0.1 produces a smaller number than 8). Struggle to see that dividing by 0.1 is the same as multiplying by 10.

misconceptions.label: Adding zeros instead of shifting decimal point
misconceptions.description: Students append or prepend zeros mechanically without moving the decimal point (e.g., 3.5 × 100 = 3.5100 or 0003.5) or add the correct number of zeros but don't adjust the decimal position, producing incorrect values. Confuses the procedure with the mechanism of place value.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Multiply or divide by 10 or 100
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiply or divide by 0.001 or 10000

difficultyDimensions.easy: Multiply or divide whole integers (e.g., 24 × 10)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiply or divide decimals with multiple decimal places or very small/large starting values (e.g., 0.0045 × 1000 or 567.89 ÷ 0.01)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Powers of 10 written as whole numbers (10, 100, 1000)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Powers of 10 written as decimals or unit fractions (0.1, 0.01, 0.001, or equivalently 1/10, 1/100, 1/1000)

transferContexts.example: Convert 4.5 kg to grams (multiply by 1000) or 300 mm to metres (divide by 1000). Students apply the same decimal-shift logic to real-world measurement conversions.
Convert 2500 pence to pounds (divide by 100) or express £3.40 in pence (multiply by 100). Requires understanding that moving the decimal point scales monetary value.

transferContexts.context: Scientific notation and very large/small numbers
transferContexts.example: Express 0.0000567 in standard form or convert 3.2 × 10⁴ to ordinary notation. Multiplying/dividing by powers of 10 is foundational to handling exponent notation.

generativeContext: N3.3 requires students to internalise the place-value mechanism underlying decimal notation and extend it to the operation of scaling. The core insight is that multiplying by 10ⁿ shifts every digit left by n places (making each digit represent ten times its previous place value), while dividing by 10ⁿ shifts digits right by n places. This is NOT a rule to memorise but a consequence of how place value works: in 35, the 3 represents 3 tens and the 5 represents 5 ones; in 350, the 3 still represents 3 tens but now it sits in the hundreds place because it is followed by a tens digit and a ones digit. Conversely, decimals below 1 (0.1, 0.01, 0.001) represent tenths, hundredths, thousandths—so dividing by 10 is equivalent to moving to the next smaller place-value column. A robust generative model should vary: (1) whether the operation is multiplication or division; (2) the power of 10 (including decimal powers like 0.01); (3) the starting value (integers, simple decimals, complex decimals, very small numbers); (4) whether students must explain or perform the operation; and (5) whether the context is abstract (pure number) or applied (measurement, money, or scientific). Misconceptions n3-3-m1 and n3-3-m2 are by far most common and should be probed frequently. Questions should avoid trivial cases (e.g., multiplying single-digit integers by 10) and include deliberately tricky negatives (e.g., 0.7 × 0.01, or 0.005 ÷ 100) to force precision. Robust mastery involves students confidently handling decimals smaller than 1 as divisors and being able to articulate why the decimal point moves, not just that it does.

name: Multiplication (without carrying)
masteryDefinition: Students can multiply two single-digit numbers or a single-digit number by a two-digit number (where no carrying is required in any column) using the standard column method or mental strategies. They understand that the result is the total of equal groups and can explain their method.

misconceptions.label: Multiplying by only the ones digit
misconceptions.description: When multiplying a single-digit number by a two-digit number (e.g., 3 × 24), students multiply only by the ones digit (3 × 4 = 12) and ignore the tens digit, giving an incorrect partial answer or forgetting to account for the tens place value.

misconceptions.label: Confusing multiplication with repeated addition of single digits
misconceptions.description: Students treat 3 × 24 as 3 + 2 + 4 or add the digits together (3 + 24) rather than understanding that 3 × 24 means '3 groups of 24'. They lose sight of the multiplicative structure.

misconceptions.label: Misaligning partial products in column multiplication
misconceptions.description: When using the column method for 24 × 3, students write the partial product (e.g., 12 from 3 × 4) without correctly positioning it under the ones place, or they shift it incorrectly, leading to addition errors even if individual multiplications are correct.

misconceptions.label: Treating multi-digit multipliers as if only their face value matters
misconceptions.description: When multiplying by a two-digit number written as a single-digit in a specific column (e.g., multiplying 12 × 23 but only multiplying 12 × 3 in the ones column and completely ignoring the 2 in the tens column), students fail to compute the tens partial product.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single-digit × single-digit (e.g., 4 × 7)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Single-digit × two-digit (e.g., 6 × 34) or two-digit × two-digit (e.g., 23 × 12) without carrying

difficultyDimensions.easy: Multiplying by single-digit only (1–9), all products ≤ 9 in each column
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiplying by two-digit numbers where the tens and ones digits each generate separate partial products (e.g., 15 × 12)

difficultyDimensions.easy: No zeros in either operand (e.g., 3 × 24)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Operands containing zero (e.g., 20 × 3 or 102 × 4), requiring careful place-value tracking

transferContexts.example: A shop sells packs of 12 pencils. How many pencils in 4 packs? (4 × 12, no carrying)
A rectangle is 3 units wide and 24 units long. What is its area? (3 × 24)
If one basket holds 23 apples, how many apples in 2 baskets? (2 × 23)

generativeContext: N3.4 targets multiplication of single-digit and two-digit numbers without carrying. Students should understand that multiplication represents equal groups and can compute products using column method or mental strategies (e.g., distributive property: 3 × 24 = 3 × 20 + 3 × 4 = 60 + 12 = 72). Carrying is not required because all intermediate products (digit × digit) are single-digit numbers that do not exceed 9 in any column. Key pedagogical focus: correct place-value alignment, especially when multiplying two-digit numbers by single digits or when two-digit multipliers generate separate partial products. Avoid problems where students must add 10 or more in any column (that requires carrying, which is beyond scope). Use concrete representations (arrays, area models, repeated groups) to anchor understanding. Common errors include ignoring the tens digit entirely, confusing multiplication with addition, misaligning partial products in the column method, or treating zeros incorrectly. Diagnostic questions should isolate whether the error is conceptual (multiplication not understood as equal groups) or procedural (column alignment or computation of intermediate products).

name: Multiplication (with carrying)
masteryDefinition: Students can multiply multi-digit whole numbers using the standard algorithm with regrouping (carrying). They understand place value underpins the process, can correctly manage tens and hundreds carries, and verify answers using estimation or alternative methods. Mastery includes multiplying up to 4-digit by 2-digit numbers fluently with minimal errors.

misconceptions.label: Forgetting to carry or recording the carry in the wrong place
misconceptions.description: Students perform the multiplication of digits correctly but fail to record carried amounts, or write them in the wrong column. For example, 24 × 3 = 612 instead of 72 (forgetting to add the carried 2 to the tens column). This reflects incomplete understanding of place value in the multiplication algorithm.

misconceptions.label: Adding the carry to the wrong digit before multiplying
misconceptions.description: Students add the carry amount to the digit being multiplied rather than to the product. For example, in 34 × 7, they calculate (3+2)×7 instead of (3×7)+2. This confuses the order of operations within the carrying process.

misconceptions.label: Misaligning partial products in multi-digit multiplication
misconceptions.description: When multiplying by numbers with multiple digits (e.g., 24 × 35), students fail to shift partial products correctly. They write the second partial product starting in the ones column instead of the tens column, leading to incorrect final sums. This stems from misunderstanding that each digit represents a different place value.

misconceptions.label: Carrying across place value boundaries incorrectly
misconceptions.description: Students mismanage carries when they span multiple columns, such as carrying from tens to hundreds. They may forget to carry again from a column that already received a carry, or lose track of multiple carries in sequence. Example: 37 × 8 = 256 instead of 296.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of digits in the multiplier
difficultyDimensions.easy: Single-digit multiplier (e.g., 34 × 6)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Three or four-digit multiplier (e.g., 123 × 456)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Frequency and magnitude of carries required
difficultyDimensions.easy: Minimal or no carries (e.g., 12 × 3)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple consecutive carries across columns (e.g., 99 × 8 = 792)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Place value complexity of the multiplicands
difficultyDimensions.easy: Multiplying numbers with few zeros and regular digit patterns (e.g., 23 × 4)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiplying numbers with zeros or repeated digits requiring careful tracking (e.g., 307 × 25)

transferContexts.example: A supermarket has 27 boxes of apples, each containing 18 apples. How many apples are there altogether? (Requires 27 × 18)

transferContexts.context: Area and array models in geometry
transferContexts.example: A rectangular garden is 34 metres long and 16 metres wide. What is its area in square metres? (Requires 34 × 16)
A ticket costs £23. If 42 people buy tickets, what is the total revenue? (Requires 23 × 42)

generativeContext: N3.5 targets the standard multiplication algorithm with carrying for multi-digit whole numbers. Students must internalise that multiplication is repeated addition and that carrying emerges from regrouping: when a digit-by-digit product exceeds 9, the tens digit becomes a carry. The algorithm scales across place value boundaries; carrying in the ones column feeds into the tens column, and so on. Multi-digit multipliers introduce partial products that must align by place value before summing. Misconceptions cluster around: (1) failure to execute the carry action (forgetting to record or add it), (2) sequencing errors (when to carry, what to carry to), and (3) spatial/alignment errors in multi-digit contexts. Difficulty is driven by carry frequency, number of digits, and whether zeros or complex digit patterns demand sustained attention. Questions should vary systematically: start with 2-digit × 1-digit requiring simple carries, progress to 3-digit × 2-digit with multiple partial products, include both high-carry cases (99 × 8) and low-carry cases (123 × 2) to isolate skill from arithmetic burden. Transfer contexts anchor the abstract algorithm in area models, grouping, and money, reinforcing that carrying is not arbitrary but a consequence of place value structure.

name: Area of rectangles, parallelograms, triangles and compound shapes
masteryDefinition: Students can calculate the area of rectangles, parallelograms, and triangles using appropriate formulae (length × width, base × height, ½ × base × height). They can decompose compound shapes into simpler constituent shapes, find the area of each, and combine to find total area. They understand why formulae work geometrically and can select the correct formula based on shape properties. They can work with measurements in different units and convert where necessary.

misconceptions.label: Using perimeter formula instead of area formula
misconceptions.description: Students calculate perimeter (adding all sides) when asked for area, or confuse the two concepts. Often seen as adding all four sides of a rectangle instead of multiplying length by width.

misconceptions.label: Forgetting to halve in triangle area formula
misconceptions.description: Students apply base × height for triangles without dividing by 2, treating triangles as if they were parallelograms. May stem from incomplete memorisation or not understanding that a triangle is half a parallelogram.

misconceptions.label: Using slant height instead of perpendicular height in parallelograms or triangles
misconceptions.description: Students measure or use the slanted side length as the height rather than the perpendicular distance from base to opposite side. Common when diagrams show slanted edges or when students confuse side length with height.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect decomposition of compound shapes
misconceptions.description: Students decompose compound shapes incorrectly by overlapping regions, missing regions, or using shapes that don't fit together properly. May add or subtract areas illogically without visualising the decomposition clearly.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Rectangle with integer dimensions given; students apply length × width only
difficultyDimensions.hard: Compound shape requiring decomposition into three or more triangles and parallelograms with some perpendicular heights needing to be inferred from a diagram

difficultyDimensions.easy: All dimensions clearly labelled in the same unit; all measurements are integers
difficultyDimensions.hard: Mixed units (cm and m); some measurements are decimals or fractions; students must convert before calculating or express answer in a specified unit

difficultyDimensions.easy: Standard orientation (rectangle horizontal, triangle with base horizontal); all dimensions directly labelled
difficultyDimensions.hard: Rotated or irregular orientation; compound shape with no dimension labels; student must use a ruler or identify which edges correspond to base and height

transferContexts.example: A rectangular room is 5 m × 4 m. Tiles cost £2.50 per square metre. How much will it cost to tile the entire floor? (Requires area of rectangle and application to cost calculation.)
A triangular field has a base of 120 m and a perpendicular height of 80 m. What is its area in hectares? (1 hectare = 10,000 m².) (Requires triangle area formula and unit conversion.)

transferContexts.context: Design and composite materials (L-shaped or T-shaped structures)
transferContexts.example: An L-shaped garden is made from a 6 m × 8 m rectangle with a 2 m × 3 m rectangular section removed from one corner. What is the area of the garden? (Requires compound shape decomposition and subtraction.)

generativeContext: This skill bridges visual geometry and algebraic formula application. Students must connect three distinct processes: (1) memorising and selecting the correct formula (rectangle: l × w; parallelogram: b × h; triangle: ½ × b × h); (2) identifying base and perpendicular height correctly in diagrams—especially critical for parallelograms and triangles where slant edges and rotations cause confusion; (3) decomposing irregular compound shapes by sketching sub-regions, calculating each, and combining methodically. The most common error is confusion between perimeter and area, followed by omitting the ÷2 factor in triangles or measuring slant height instead of perpendicular height. Scaffold by linking parallelograms to rectangles ("base × height is the same idea") and triangles to parallelograms ("half the area"). Use grid paper or dynamic software to help students visualise perpendicular height. When generating questions, vary: (a) whether the shape is isolated vs. compound; (b) whether height is directly labelled vs. must be inferred; (c) whether units are uniform or mixed; (d) whether the shape is standard orientation or rotated. Avoid ambiguity in what counts as base and height.

name: Short division (without carrying)
masteryDefinition: Students can divide a multi-digit number by a single-digit divisor using short division (bus-stop method) when there is no remainder or carrying required. They can correctly identify when each digit divides evenly, record the quotient above the dividend, and interpret the result as a whole number.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring remainders or dropping digits
misconceptions.description: Students write down only the whole number part of a division when a digit doesn't divide evenly, effectively ignoring the remainder. For example, 84 ÷ 4 is computed as 2 (from 8÷4) then 1 (from 4÷4), written as 21 instead of 21, but they ignore what happens if they try 85 ÷ 4 and skip the remainder. Commonly occurs when a digit divides with a remainder and students don't know how to proceed.

misconceptions.label: Left-to-right concatenation without place value
misconceptions.description: Students treat each digit independently and concatenate answers without considering place value. For example, dividing 96 ÷ 3, they write 3 (from 9÷3) then 2 (from 6÷3) to get 32, but if they made an error they might write 32 as if 9÷3=3 and 6÷3=2 are separate answers rather than a single 32 (three tens and two ones).

misconceptions.label: Dividing in wrong order or reversing digits
misconceptions.description: Students attempt to divide the smaller leading digit first or divide from right to left instead of left to right. For example, with 48 ÷ 2, they might try 8÷2 first (getting 4) then 4÷2 (getting 2), and write 42 instead of 24, reversing the correct answer.

misconceptions.label: Confusing division with subtraction
misconceptions.description: Students repeatedly subtract the divisor from each digit instead of dividing. For example, when dividing 84 ÷ 4, they write down 4 after subtracting 4 from 8, but then subtract 4 from 4 to get 0 and write that, resulting in 40 instead of 21. Shows confusion between the short division algorithm and repeated subtraction.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Two-digit numbers (e.g. 48 ÷ 2, 63 ÷ 3)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Four or five-digit numbers (e.g. 2468 ÷ 2, 54321 ÷ 3)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Dividing by 2, 3, or 5
difficultyDimensions.hard: Dividing by 7, 8, or 9 (requires stronger multiplication tables fluency)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Evenness of division across all digits
difficultyDimensions.easy: All digits divide evenly with no remainders (e.g. 88 ÷ 4 = 22)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Some digits divide with remainders but the problem is posed as 'no carrying' (requiring careful digit selection, e.g. 84 ÷ 2 where 8÷2=4 evenly but 4÷2=2 evenly, vs. scenarios designed to avoid remainder notation)

transferContexts.example: There are 96 sweets to share equally among 4 friends. How many sweets does each friend get? Use short division to find the answer.
A recipe uses 168 grams of flour and makes 4 cakes. How many grams of flour are in each cake? Divide 168 by 4 using short division.
£824 is divided equally among 4 charities. How much does each charity receive? Use short division to solve.

generativeContext: Short division (bus-stop method) is the formal written algorithm for dividing larger numbers by single-digit divisors. Students have already mastered multiplication tables and understand division as the inverse of multiplication. In N3.7, we constrain problems to cases where no carrying is required—meaning each digit of the dividend divides evenly by the divisor, producing a whole digit in the quotient. The algorithm proceeds left-to-right: position the divisor in a bus-stop bracket, divide the first digit (or first two digits if the first is smaller), write the quotient above the dividend, and repeat for each subsequent digit. The critical procedural error points are: (1) processing digits in wrong order, (2) forgetting a digit in the quotient, (3) mishandling place value when concatenating results, (4) confusing the operation when a remainder arises (which should not happen in no-carrying contexts, but students often construct these cases incorrectly). Reinforce that short division is a left-to-right process tied directly to place value—dividing tens separately from units—and that the quotient digits stack vertically above corresponding dividend digits. Avoid problems involving remainders, decimals, or carrying (bridging) until later skills. Use concrete examples where divisibility is guaranteed (e.g., 48÷2, 369÷3, 8008÷4) and gradually increase dividend length and divisor magnitude. Connect to inverse checking: multiply the quotient by the divisor to verify the original dividend.

name: Short division (with carrying)
masteryDefinition: Students can divide a multi-digit number by a single-digit divisor using short division (bus-stop method), correctly managing remainders that must be carried forward as tens to the next column. They can interpret remainders as whole numbers, fractions, or decimals depending on context, and verify their answers by multiplication.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring the remainder when carrying
misconceptions.description: Student divides each digit independently without carrying the remainder to the next column. For example, when dividing 456 by 3, they might write 1, 5, 2 (from 4÷3=1r1, 5÷3=1r2, 6÷3=2) but fail to combine the remainder 1 with the 5 to make 15 before the next division step.

misconceptions.label: Writing remainder as a separate digit instead of carrying
misconceptions.description: Student writes remainders as additional digits in the quotient rather than carrying them. For example, dividing 372 by 4 yields '932' (written as 9, 3, 2) instead of recognising that the remainder after 37÷4 should be carried to make 32÷4.

misconceptions.label: Misplacing the carried remainder
misconceptions.description: Student carries the remainder but adds it to the wrong position or adds it to the quotient instead of the next digit of the dividend. For example, when dividing 285 by 3, after 28÷3=9r1, the student carries the 1 but adds it to the 9 instead of combining it with the 5 to make 15.

misconceptions.label: Confusion with notation when remainder exists
misconceptions.description: Student calculates correctly but cannot express the final answer appropriately when a remainder exists. They may write '24r5' as '245' or struggle to convert the remainder 5÷6 into the fraction or decimal form. The division process works but the communication of the answer fails.

difficultyDimensions.easy: 2-digit dividend (e.g. 56 ÷ 4)
difficultyDimensions.hard: 4- or 5-digit dividend (e.g. 3847 ÷ 6)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Divisor size and frequency of carrying
difficultyDimensions.easy: Dividing by 2 or 3 with infrequent remainders (e.g. 68 ÷ 2)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Dividing by 7 or 8 with multiple carrying steps (e.g. 529 ÷ 7)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Answer expected as whole number with remainder (e.g. '24r5')
difficultyDimensions.hard: Answer expected as decimal (e.g. 78.5) or mixed number (e.g. 5⅗) or simple fraction

transferContexts.example: If 5 friends share £63 equally, how much does each person get? Express your answer in pounds and pence.
A baker has 185 cakes to pack into boxes of 6. How many full boxes can be made, and how many cakes are left over?
If 8 identical books cost £96, what is the cost of one book?

generativeContext: Short division (bus-stop method) is the standard written algorithm for dividing multi-digit numbers by single-digit divisors at KS3. Mastery requires secure understanding of place value and the mechanics of carrying remainders. The algorithm works left-to-right: divide the first digit(s) to get a quotient digit and remainder, then append the remainder to the next digit to form the new working number. Key errors cluster around three points: (1) forgetting to carry remainders forward, leading to independent division of each digit; (2) miswriting remainders as quotient digits instead of as tens to carry; (3) arithmetic errors in the carrying process itself. Remainder handling varies by context: whole number remainders, fractions, decimals, or in some word problems, a need to round up. Secure students verify answers via multiplication. Questions should progress from 2-digit ÷ single-digit (few carrying steps) to 4–5 digit ÷ challenging single-digit divisors (multiple carries). Word problems should require students to interpret remainders meaningfully (e.g. 'boxes left over' vs 'cost per item'). Avoid division by 1 or 10; focus on genuine division requiring the carrying mechanism.

name: Order of Operations (DM before AS, L→R, indices/roots, brackets, inserting brackets)
masteryDefinition: Students can correctly evaluate multi-step numerical expressions by applying the complete order of operations: brackets first, then indices and roots, then multiplication and division left-to-right, then addition and subtraction left-to-right. They can insert brackets into expressions to change the order of evaluation and verify their effect on the result.

misconceptions.label: Performing addition/subtraction before multiplication/division
misconceptions.description: Student evaluates 2 + 3 × 4 as (2 + 3) × 4 = 20 instead of 2 + (3 × 4) = 14. They process operations left-to-right regardless of hierarchy, or believe that addition and subtraction have equal priority to multiplication and division.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring or misapplying left-to-right rule within same priority level
misconceptions.description: Student evaluates 20 ÷ 4 × 2 as 20 ÷ (4 × 2) = 2.5 instead of (20 ÷ 4) × 2 = 10. They group operations by type rather than evaluating left-to-right when operations have equal priority.

misconceptions.label: Treating indices/roots as lower priority than multiplication/division
misconceptions.description: Student evaluates 2 + 3² as (2 + 3)² = 25 instead of 2 + 9 = 11, or evaluates 12 ÷ 2² as (12 ÷ 2)² = 36 instead of 12 ÷ 4 = 3. They do not recognise that indices and roots must be evaluated before multiplication and division.

misconceptions.label: Incorrectly interpreting the scope of brackets or indices
misconceptions.description: Student evaluates 3 × (2 + 4)² as 3 × 2 + 4² = 6 + 16 = 22 instead of 3 × 36 = 108, failing to recognise that the bracket must be fully evaluated first, including the index applied to the bracket result. Or they evaluate 2 × 3² as (2 × 3)² = 36 instead of 2 × 9 = 18.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of operations in the expression
difficultyDimensions.easy: Two operations: 5 + 2 × 3
difficultyDimensions.hard: Five or more operations: 2 + 3 × 4 - 6 ÷ 2 + 1

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Presence and complexity of brackets and indices
difficultyDimensions.easy: No brackets or indices: 10 - 4 + 2
difficultyDimensions.hard: Nested brackets with indices: (2 + 3²) × (4 - 1) or 2 × (3 + √9)²

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of operations at same priority level
difficultyDimensions.easy: Single operation at each level: 2 + 3 × 4
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple operations at same level requiring consistent L→R application: 20 ÷ 5 × 2 ÷ 4 + 3 - 1

transferContexts.context: Evaluating algebraic expressions with given numerical values
transferContexts.example: If a = 2 and b = 3, find the value of 5a + b². [Tests whether student applies order of operations when substituting and evaluating, not just in pure number expressions.]

transferContexts.context: Rearranging and solving simple equations using inverse operations
transferContexts.example: Solve 2x + 5 = 17. [Requires understanding that multiplication applies before addition, so when inverting, subtraction must reverse the addition first.]

transferContexts.context: Converting between worded problems and numerical expressions
transferContexts.example: Write an expression for 'three times the sum of four and two' and evaluate it. Then compare with 'three times four plus two'. [Tests ability to use brackets to represent correct order and evaluate accordingly.]

generativeContext: Order of operations is a fundamental convention for unambiguous interpretation of mathematical expressions. The hierarchy is: Brackets → Indices/Roots → Multiplication and Division (left-to-right) → Addition and Subtraction (left-to-right). Students must apply this hierarchy consistently and understand why it matters: 2 + 3 × 4 = 14 not 20; 20 ÷ 4 × 2 = 10 not 2.5. Common errors centre on treating all operations as equally prioritised and evaluating strictly left-to-right, or misapplying the left-to-right rule within a priority level. Indices and roots are often forgotten or deprioritised. Brackets and indices interact subtly: (3 + 2)² = 25, but 3 + 2² = 7. Use concrete worked examples with clear annotation of step-by-step evaluation. Scaffold by presenting expressions with one priority level before layering. Ask students to insert brackets to change results, then predict and verify the effect. Use number lines and area models for some expressions to reinforce meaning beyond procedural order.

name: Multiples
masteryDefinition: Students can identify and list multiples of any given positive integer, understand that multiples are products of a number and positive integers, recognise the infinite nature of multiples, and apply multiple knowledge to solve problems involving common multiples and patterns in sequences.

misconceptions.label: Confusing multiples with factors
misconceptions.description: Students incorrectly list factors of a number when asked for multiples, or vice versa. For example, when asked for multiples of 4, they write 1, 2, 4 instead of 4, 8, 12, 16, ... This stems from conflating 'numbers that relate to 4' with 'numbers that 4 divides into' rather than understanding multiples as products of 4 × n.

misconceptions.label: Including 1 as a multiple (other than when multiplying by 1)
misconceptions.description: Students write 1 as a multiple of numbers like 3 or 5, confusing the additive identity with multiplicative iteration. Multiples of 5 should be 5, 10, 15, 20, ... not 1, 5, 10, 15, ... This reflects incomplete understanding that multiples result from 5 × 1, 5 × 2, 5 × 3, etc., where the multiplier starts at 1, not the result.

misconceptions.label: Believing every number has a finite set of multiples
misconceptions.description: Students treat multiples as a closed list and believe that after reaching a certain large number, there are no more multiples. For instance, they might state that multiples of 6 end at 60 or 100, failing to grasp the infinite generative property of the definition (n × 6 for all positive integers n).

misconceptions.label: Treating zero as a multiple in contexts where it causes confusion
misconceptions.description: Students inconsistently handle zero: sometimes including it as a multiple (0 is technically a multiple of every number), sometimes excluding it. This causes issues in real-world contexts (e.g. 'How many multiples of 4 fit in 20?') where 0 may or may not be relevant. Confusion arises from mixing formal mathematical definition with contextual appropriateness.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Multiples of 2, 5, or 10 (familiar skip-counting patterns)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiples of 7, 11, 13, or other primes less commonly encountered in KS2 arithmetic

difficultyDimensions.easy: List the first 5 multiples of 3 (small, finite task with immediate results)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Identify all multiples of 8 between 150 and 250 (requires search within range and checking validity)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Direct listing: 'Write down the first four multiples of 6'
difficultyDimensions.hard: Problem-solving: 'Find the smallest multiple of 6 and 8 that is greater than 100' (requires linking to LCM and reasoning about two sequences simultaneously)

transferContexts.context: Common multiples and finding LCM in real-world scheduling or pattern problems
transferContexts.example: Buses run every 4 minutes and every 6 minutes. When will they next depart at the same time? (Identify common multiples of 4 and 6.)
Explain why any multiple of 6 must also be a multiple of 2 and 3. (Recognise that 6 = 2 × 3 implies all multiples of 6 inherit divisibility by 2 and 3.)

transferContexts.context: Modular arithmetic and remainders in division
transferContexts.example: A number leaves a remainder of 3 when divided by 5. Is it a multiple of 5? Why or why not? (Consolidate that multiples of n have remainder 0 when divided by n.)

generativeContext: Multiples are the set of products obtained by multiplying a given positive integer by successive positive integers: the multiples of n are n×1, n×2, n×3, ... forming an infinite, ascending sequence with common difference n. Key properties: every number is a multiple of 1 and itself; multiples are always ≥ the base number (in the positive direction); every multiple is divisible by its base with remainder 0; multiples appear in arithmetic sequences (e.g. multiples of 7 are 7, 14, 21, 28, ... with constant difference 7). Common confusions arise from: (1) blending factors (divisors) with multiples (inverse operations), (2) misinterpreting the starting point (n×0 vs n×1), (3) underestimating or denying infinity, (4) inconsistent treatment of zero. Diagnostic questions should test whether students can list multiples fluently, predict whether a given number is a multiple (by divisibility), identify patterns in sequences of multiples, and reason about common multiples of two or more numbers. Scaffold by connecting to skip-counting (familiar from KS1–2), building to symbolic definition, then to applications like timetables, patterns, and GCD/LCM contexts.

name: Factors
masteryDefinition: Students can identify all factors of a given positive integer, understand that factors are whole numbers that divide evenly into another number with no remainder, and recognise the relationship between factors and divisibility. They can list factors systematically, understand that 1 and the number itself are always factors, and apply factor knowledge to solve problems involving sharing, grouping, and finding common factors.

misconceptions.label: Confusing factors with multiples
misconceptions.description: Students state that 24 is a factor of 6, or list multiples of a number when asked for its factors. This arises from unclear understanding of the direction of the divisibility relationship: factors divide into a number, while the number is a multiple of its factors.

misconceptions.label: Omitting 1 or the number itself as factors
misconceptions.description: Students list only 'proper factors' and exclude 1 or the number itself (e.g. for 12, listing 2, 3, 4, 6 but not 1 or 12). They may believe factors must be 'smaller' or 'in the middle' of the number.

misconceptions.label: Listing only prime factors
misconceptions.description: When asked for all factors, students list only prime factors (e.g. for 12, writing 2 and 3 instead of 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12). They conflate 'factors' with 'prime factors' or believe only primes qualify as 'real' factors.

misconceptions.label: Incomplete systematic listing
misconceptions.description: Students list some factors correctly but miss others through unsystematic checking (e.g. testing only even divisors, or stopping early). For 20, they might list 1, 2, 4, 5, 10 but omit 20, or list 1, 2, 4, 10, 20 but miss 5.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Size and magnitude of the number
difficultyDimensions.easy: Finding all factors of single-digit or small two-digit numbers (e.g. 6, 12, 20)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Finding all factors of larger numbers, especially three-digit numbers or those with many factors (e.g. 60, 120, 100)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Numbers with few factors, especially primes or numbers with obvious divisors (e.g. 7, 8, 15)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Highly composite numbers with many factors requiring systematic enumeration (e.g. 36, 48, 60)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Identifying factors when dividing by consecutive integers from 1 is explicitly scaffolded
difficultyDimensions.hard: Independently discovering and applying a systematic method (testing 1, then 2, 3, 4... up to √n) without guidance

transferContexts.example: A teacher has 24 students and wants to divide them into equal groups with no students left over. How many different group sizes are possible? (Answer requires finding all factors of 24.)

transferContexts.context: Finding common factors and greatest common factor
transferContexts.example: Find all the common factors of 18 and 24, then identify the greatest common factor. (Requires listing factors of each number first, then comparing.)

A baker has 36 cupcakes and wants to arrange them in a rectangular display with equal rows and columns. What are all the possible dimensions of the rectangle? (Answer requires finding factor pairs of 36.)

generativeContext: Factors are positive integers that divide evenly into a given number with remainder zero. For a positive integer n, factor d satisfies n ÷ d = integer. Key conceptual anchors: (1) factors are bidirectional pairs—if d is a factor of n, then n/d is also a factor; (2) 1 and n are always factors of n; (3) factors can be found by testing divisibility from 1 upward, or by identifying factor pairs systematically. Common pedagogical sequence: start with concrete sharing/grouping contexts (12 items arranged in rows), move to divisibility testing, then teach factor-pair enumeration. Prime factorisation is distinct from factor listing. When generating questions, vary: (a) size of target number (single-digit through three-digit), (b) factor density (primes vs. highly composite), (c) whether question asks for listing all factors, identifying specific factors, or comparing factors across two numbers. Use realistic contexts involving division, arrangement, and common factors. Misconception traps: students often conflate factors with multiples, omit trivial factors (1, n), list only primes, or stop listing prematurely due to unsystematic searching. Assess mastery through complete, correctly-ordered factor lists and application to concrete sharing/grouping scenarios.

name: Lowest Common Multiple
masteryDefinition: Students can find the LCM of two or more numbers using listing multiples, prime factorisation, or systematic methods; apply LCM to solve problems involving cycles, patterns, or synchronisation; and recognise when LCM is the appropriate tool for problems stated in context.

misconceptions.label: Confusing LCM with GCD
misconceptions.description: Students find the Greatest Common Divisor instead of the Lowest Common Multiple, particularly when asked to 'find the common multiple' without emphasis on 'least'. Often occurs when students rush or conflate similar-sounding concepts.

misconceptions.label: Multiplying all numbers together
misconceptions.description: Students assume the LCM is always the product of the numbers given (e.g., LCM of 4 and 6 is 24 instead of 12). This shows misunderstanding that LCM must be a multiple of each number but need not be their product.

misconceptions.label: Stopping at first common multiple found
misconceptions.description: When listing multiples, students identify a common multiple but fail to verify it is the *lowest*. For example, identifying 24 as a common multiple of 4 and 6 but not checking that 12 is smaller.

misconceptions.label: Misapplying prime factorisation method
misconceptions.description: Students add prime factors instead of taking the highest power of each prime (e.g., for LCM of 12 and 18: adding 2² × 3 + 2 × 3² instead of selecting 2² × 3²). Shows procedural error in exponent selection.

difficultyDimensions.easy: LCM of two small single-digit numbers (e.g., 3 and 5)
difficultyDimensions.hard: LCM of three or more numbers with repeated prime factors (e.g., 12, 18, and 30)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Size and complexity of prime factors
difficultyDimensions.easy: Numbers with only small prime factors and low powers (e.g., 6 = 2 × 3)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Numbers with larger primes or higher powers (e.g., 72 = 2³ × 3², 60 = 2² × 3 × 5)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Direct instruction: 'Find the LCM of 4 and 10'
difficultyDimensions.hard: Word problem requiring recognition that LCM applies: 'Buses A and B depart every 12 and 18 minutes. When do they depart together again?'

transferContexts.example: To add 5/12 + 7/18, students must find LCM(12, 18) = 36 to create equivalent fractions. LCM is the common denominator.
Traffic lights at an intersection turn green every 20 seconds and 30 seconds respectively. At what times (in seconds) will they both be green simultaneously starting from time 0?
A gym class meets every 4 days and a swimming lesson every 6 days. If both start on the same day, in how many days will they fall on the same day again?

generativeContext: LCM (Lowest Common Multiple) is the smallest positive integer divisible by each of a set of given numbers. Key conceptual anchors: (1) LCM must be a multiple of *each* input number; (2) there are multiple valid common multiples, but LCM is the smallest; (3) for coprime numbers (GCD = 1), LCM = product; otherwise LCM < product. Primary solution methods: listing multiples (systematic enumeration, accessible but slow for large numbers), prime factorisation (efficient: express each number as product of prime powers, then LCM = product of each prime to its highest power occurring), and Venn diagrams (visual, supports understanding). Pedagogical sequences: start with concrete context (events repeating), move to listing, then prime factorisation. Common contextual applications: finding common denominators in fraction addition/subtraction, synchronisation problems (buses, lights, rotating schedules), and tiling/pattern problems. Link LCM to GCD via identity: LCM(a,b) × GCD(a,b) = a × b. Distinguish carefully from HCF/GCD: LCM is about 'smallest box that fits both', GCD is about 'largest piece both fit into'. Misconceptions often arise from procedural confusion (multiplying instead of selecting max powers) and insufficient verification (claiming first common multiple found is the least).

name: Highest Common Factor
masteryDefinition: Students can identify all factors of two or more positive integers and determine their highest common factor (HCF) using systematic listing, prime factorisation, or the Euclidean algorithm. They can apply HCF to solve problems involving sharing, grouping, and simplification of fractions. They recognise that HCF of coprime numbers is 1 and understand why common factors divide all given numbers.

misconceptions.label: Confusing HCF with LCM
misconceptions.description: Students find the Least Common Multiple instead of the Highest Common Factor. For example, when asked for HCF(12, 18), they calculate 36 instead of 6. This often occurs because students conflate the procedures or misremember which operation finds the 'largest' shared value.

misconceptions.label: Including non-common factors in HCF
misconceptions.description: Students list all factors of both numbers but fail to identify only the common ones. For example, for HCF(12, 18), they might say 12 is a common factor (listing 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12, 18 as a combined set) rather than correctly identifying {1, 2, 3, 6} as common.

misconceptions.label: Incorrectly applying prime factorisation rules
misconceptions.description: Students use prime factorisation to attempt HCF but take the product of ALL prime factors instead of only the common ones with their lowest powers. For example, HCF(12, 18): 12 = 2² × 3, 18 = 2 × 3². They calculate 2² × 3² = 36 instead of 2 × 3 = 6.

misconceptions.label: Assuming HCF must be less than the smallest number
misconceptions.description: Students fail to recognise that HCF can equal one of the given numbers if that number is a factor of all others. For example, they claim HCF(6, 12) cannot be 6, or that HCF(7, 14, 21) cannot be 7, showing incomplete understanding of divisibility.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Size and quantity of input numbers
difficultyDimensions.easy: Two single-digit numbers (e.g. HCF(4, 6))
difficultyDimensions.hard: Three or more two-digit numbers with multiple prime factors (e.g. HCF(60, 84, 132))

difficultyDimensions.easy: One number is a multiple of the other (e.g. HCF(5, 15)) or numbers share obvious common factors
difficultyDimensions.hard: Coprime or near-coprime numbers (e.g. HCF(35, 48)) requiring systematic enumeration or prime factorisation
