difficultyDimensions.easy: Simple listing of factors or obvious mental recall (e.g. HCF(10, 15))
difficultyDimensions.hard: Systematic prime factorisation or Euclidean algorithm on larger numbers (e.g. HCF(144, 180))

transferContexts.context: Simplifying fractions by cancelling to lowest terms
transferContexts.example: Express 24/36 in simplest form by finding HCF(24, 36) and dividing both numerator and denominator by it.

transferContexts.context: Sharing or grouping problems requiring equal distribution
transferContexts.example: A teacher has 48 blue pens and 36 red pens. What is the greatest number of identical pencil cases she can fill so that each case contains the same number of blue and red pens with none left over?

transferContexts.context: Finding the largest square tile that fits exactly into a rectangular floor
transferContexts.example: A floor measures 120 cm by 90 cm. What is the side length of the largest square tile that will fit exactly with no cutting or gaps?

generativeContext: HCF (Highest Common Factor, also called Greatest Common Divisor or GCD) is the largest positive integer that divides all given numbers with no remainder. Mastery requires students to move beyond memorisation to systematic procedures: (1) Factor listing: enumerate all factors of each number, then identify the largest that appears in all lists. (2) Prime factorisation: express each number as a product of primes, then multiply the common primes using the lowest power of each. (3) Euclidean algorithm (extension): repeatedly apply division and remainder until remainder is zero. Key conceptual anchors: HCF(a, b) ≤ min(a, b); HCF is always at least 1; if one number divides another, HCF equals the smaller; coprime numbers have HCF = 1. Common confusions: conflating with LCM (students often compute the product of all primes instead of only common ones), treating non-common factors as part of the answer, and failing to recognise when HCF equals one of the input numbers. Misconceptions typically emerge in prime factorisation contexts and when numbers are large or coprime. Assessment should probe both procedural fluency and conceptual understanding via real-world sharing/grouping problems and fraction simplification. Enrich by introducing the connection between HCF and GCD notation, and motivate the Euclidean algorithm as an efficient procedure for large numbers.

name: Convert metric units
masteryDefinition: Students can fluently convert between metric units of length (mm, cm, m, km), mass (g, kg), and capacity (ml, l) using multiplicative relationships. They understand and apply the correct conversion factors (×10, ×100, ×1000 or ÷10, ÷100, ÷1000) and can justify their choice of operation. They can convert in both directions and work with decimal and fractional amounts.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect direction of scaling
misconceptions.description: Students apply the wrong operation when converting units, e.g. converting 5 km to m by dividing by 1000 instead of multiplying by 1000, or converting 500 g to kg by multiplying by 1000 instead of dividing by 1000. Often caused by confusion about whether the number gets larger or smaller when moving between units.

misconceptions.label: Wrong conversion factor applied
misconceptions.description: Students use incorrect multipliers between units, e.g. converting cm to mm by multiplying by 100 instead of 10, or converting litres to ml by multiplying by 100 instead of 1000. Often reflects misremembering or confusing the relationships between different metric units.

misconceptions.label: Treating metric conversion like imperial conversion
misconceptions.description: Students apply non-base-10 conversion factors (e.g. trying to use 16 or 14) or treat metric conversions as irregular/inconsistent like imperial units. May reflect prior knowledge of imperial units interfering with metric understanding.

misconceptions.label: Decimal point error when multiplying/dividing by powers of 10
misconceptions.description: Students correctly identify the operation and factor but make systematic errors moving the decimal point, e.g. converting 3.5 m to cm and writing 35 cm instead of 350 cm, or converting 240 ml to l and writing 2.40 l instead of 0.24 l.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Converting between adjacent units with factor of ×10 (e.g. cm to mm, g to cg)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Converting between non-adjacent units with factor of ×1000 (e.g. m to mm, kg to g, l to ml)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Presence of decimals in the starting value
difficultyDimensions.easy: Converting whole numbers (e.g. 5 m to cm)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Converting decimals or mixed numbers (e.g. 3.7 km to m, 2.5 litres to ml)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Direction and number of conversion steps
difficultyDimensions.easy: Converting from larger to smaller unit (one step, multiplication only, e.g. kg to g)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Converting from smaller to larger unit (one step, division only, e.g. mm to m) or multi-step conversions requiring both operations

transferContexts.example: A recipe calls for 250 ml of milk but you only have a litre measuring jug. Convert 250 ml to litres to check if you have enough.
On a map, 1 cm represents 5 km. A distance on the map measures 8 cm. Convert 40 km (the actual distance) back to cm to verify the map scale.
A student measures the length of a cell as 0.05 mm under a microscope. Convert this to micrometres (μm) where 1 mm = 1000 μm, or to metres for a science report.

generativeContext: Students are converting between metric units in the MEA (measurement and estimation) strand. The metric system is base-10 and fully regular: moving between adjacent units always involves multiplying or dividing by 10, 100, or 1000. Mastery requires understanding the hierarchical structure (mm–cm–m–km; ml–l; g–kg) and automaticity with the correct scaling direction. Key misconceptions are: reversing the operation (dividing when should multiply); applying wrong factors (confusing ×100 with ×1000); and decimal arithmetic errors especially when working with decimals in the source value. Difficulty scales with conversion factor size, presence of decimals, and whether the conversion is 'shrinking' (larger to smaller unit = multiply) or 'enlarging' (smaller to larger unit = divide, which often feels counterintuitive). Contextualise with practical scenarios: recipes, maps, scientific measurements, sports data (e.g. 100 m sprint = 0.1 km), packaging labels (grams on metric/imperial dual labels). Use bar models and place-value thinking to reinforce why multiplying by 1000 when converting m to mm means the number grows; drawing or physically showing 1 m = 100 cm = 1000 mm clarifies the scaling. Avoid pure algorithmic 'move the decimal' language; ground it in 'how many smaller units fit in a larger one'.

name: Decimal multiplication (decimal × integer)
masteryDefinition: Students can multiply a decimal number by a positive integer, correctly placing the decimal point in the answer by counting decimal places in the original decimal. They understand that multiplying by an integer scales the decimal value up and can apply this to both abstract calculations and contextual problems involving money, measurement, and quantities.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring or losing the decimal point
misconceptions.description: Students multiply the digits correctly but forget to include the decimal point in the answer, or place it in the wrong position. For example, 2.5 × 4 = 100 instead of 10.0, treating it as 25 × 4.

misconceptions.label: Counting decimal places incorrectly
misconceptions.description: Students recount decimal places or apply an incorrect rule, such as moving the decimal point the wrong number of places. For example, 3.2 × 5 = 1.60 instead of 16.0, moving the decimal point left instead of right.

misconceptions.label: Confusing decimal multiplication with decimal addition
misconceptions.description: Students add the decimal parts instead of multiplying them. For example, 1.3 × 2 = 1.6 (thinking 1 + 0.3 + 0.3 visually as adding decimals) instead of 2.6.

misconceptions.label: Assuming the decimal point stays in the same position
misconceptions.description: Students believe the decimal point does not move during multiplication and place the result with the decimal in the original position. For example, 4.7 × 3 = 4.21 instead of 14.1, keeping the decimal after the first digit.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Multiplying by single-digit integers (2–9)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiplying by two-digit or larger integers (10+)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of decimal places in the decimal number
difficultyDimensions.easy: One decimal place (e.g. 2.3 × 4)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Two or more decimal places (e.g. 3.25 × 6 or 1.456 × 7)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Magnitude of the decimal and whether zeros appear in the answer
difficultyDimensions.easy: Multiplying decimals less than 1 or small whole parts with no trailing zeros (e.g. 2.5 × 4 = 10)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiplying to produce answers with trailing zeros or very small results (e.g. 0.04 × 3 = 0.12, or 2.02 × 5 = 10.10)

transferContexts.example: A notebook costs £2.50. How much will 6 notebooks cost? (2.50 × 6)

transferContexts.context: Measurement and scaling lengths or quantities
transferContexts.example: A piece of ribbon is 1.8 metres long. How long will 5 pieces be when laid end-to-end? (1.8 × 5)
A recipe uses 0.75 kg of flour. How much flour is needed to make 4 batches of the recipe? (0.75 × 4)

generativeContext: Decimal multiplication by an integer builds on students' understanding of whole-number multiplication and place value. The core challenge is managing the decimal point: students must recognise that multiplying a decimal by an integer shifts the decimal point, but only by a specific count determined by the original number of decimal places. Reinforce the 'counting decimal places' rule explicitly: if 2.5 has one decimal place, then 2.5 × 3 = 7.5 also has one decimal place (count the places in the original decimal, not the result). Use area models and grid diagrams to visualise why the decimal moves. Connect to prior knowledge of multiplying whole numbers and the distributive property (2.5 × 3 = (2 + 0.5) × 3 = 6 + 1.5 = 7.5). Common errors cluster around: losing or misplacing the decimal, treating decimals as separate whole and fractional parts without proper multiplication, and confusing the direction or magnitude of decimal movement. Contextual problems (money, length) often reveal understanding quickly because students can estimate reasonably. Use concrete representations (tenths and units grids, number lines) before moving to purely symbolic work.

name: Decimal multiplication (decimal × decimal)
masteryDefinition: Students can multiply two decimal numbers by converting to fractions or using place value understanding, correctly placing the decimal point in the product by counting total decimal places. They understand why the result is smaller than both factors when both are less than 1, and can verify answers using estimation.

misconceptions.label: Decimal point placement by alignment rather than counting
misconceptions.description: Students align decimal points vertically (as in addition/subtraction) when multiplying decimals, rather than counting total decimal places in both factors. For example, 0.3 × 0.4 is calculated as 0.12 instead of 0.12 (correct by accident here), but 2.5 × 1.3 becomes 3.25 instead of 3.25 (correct again), masking the error until non-aligned problems appear.

misconceptions.label: Treating decimals as separate integer and fractional parts
misconceptions.description: Students multiply the integer parts and decimal parts independently, then combine them. For example, 2.3 × 1.5 is computed as (2 × 1) + (0.3 × 0.5) = 2 + 0.15 = 2.15 instead of 3.45, fundamentally misunderstanding place value in multiplication.

misconceptions.label: Incorrectly counting decimal places when one factor is a whole number
misconceptions.description: When one factor has no decimal places (written as an integer or with .0), students either forget to count it or double-count it. For example, 0.25 × 4 is calculated with only 2 decimal places (product: 1.00) when it should be 1, or 0.5 × 3 is written as 0.15 instead of 1.5 due to confusion about whether integers contribute to the decimal place count.

misconceptions.label: Assuming the product is always smaller when multiplying two decimals
misconceptions.description: Students generalise that multiplying always makes numbers smaller (true for decimals × decimals when both < 1) and fail to check reasonableness. For example, they might accept 0.5 × 0.5 = 0.25 without estimating, or when encountering 1.2 × 1.5, assume the product must be less than both factors and reject 1.8 as impossible.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of decimal places in factors
difficultyDimensions.easy: One factor is a whole number or 1 d.p.; total d.p. ≤ 2 (e.g. 0.3 × 4, 0.5 × 0.2)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Both factors have 2+ d.p. each; total d.p. ≥ 3 (e.g. 0.25 × 0.36, 1.23 × 2.05)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Both factors are less than 1 (product obviously smaller than both); e.g. 0.4 × 0.5
difficultyDimensions.hard: At least one factor > 1, or both factors close to 1; product may be larger or smaller than a factor (e.g. 2.5 × 0.3, 0.8 × 1.2)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Computational complexity of underlying integer multiplication
difficultyDimensions.easy: Factors are simple multiples (e.g. 0.2 × 0.3, 0.4 × 0.5) requiring only single-digit or two-digit integer multiplication
difficultyDimensions.hard: Factors require multi-digit integer multiplication (e.g. 2.15 × 3.24 requires 215 × 324)

transferContexts.context: Area of rectangles with decimal side lengths
transferContexts.example: A rectangular garden has length 2.5 m and width 1.8 m. What is its area in m²?

transferContexts.context: Money and currency calculations (prices, costs, exchange rates)
transferContexts.example: If one item costs £1.25 and you buy 0.4 of it (by weight or bulk discount), how much do you pay?

transferContexts.context: Scaling and proportional reasoning (enlargements, unit conversions)
transferContexts.example: A recipe uses 0.75 kg of flour. If you make 1.5 times the recipe, how much flour do you need?

generativeContext: Decimal × decimal multiplication requires students to (1) perform integer multiplication on the digits, ignoring decimal points; (2) count the total number of decimal places in both factors; (3) place the decimal point in the product so it has that total count of decimal places. This is distinct from addition/subtraction (where decimal points align) and from integer multiplication (where the decimal point position is entirely new). Key conceptual anchors include: converting to fractions (0.3 × 0.4 = 3/10 × 4/10 = 12/100 = 0.12), using place value (3 tenths × 4 tenths = 12 hundredths), and estimation (0.3 × 0.4 ≈ 0.3 × 0 = 0, so answer < 0.3). The most dangerous misconception is decimal-point alignment (inherited from addition), which students may not detect if both factors have the same number of decimal places. Watch also for the cognitive slip that products are always smaller: when both factors < 1, yes, but 1.2 × 1.5 = 1.8 (larger than 1.2), breaking the pattern. Sequencing: ensure students can multiply integers confidently, convert simple decimals to fractions, and estimate products before expecting fluent decimal × decimal work. Use concrete contexts (area, money, scaling) early to anchor the operation before moving to pure calculation.

name: Multiply by 0.1 and 0.01
masteryDefinition: Students can fluently multiply any whole number or decimal by 0.1 and 0.01, understanding these operations as shifting the decimal point one or two places to the left respectively. They can explain why multiplying by 0.1 is equivalent to dividing by 10, and apply this to solve problems involving scaling, unit conversion, and proportional reasoning. They recognise the pattern and can extend it to multiplying by 0.001, 0.0001, etc.

misconceptions.label: Decimal point direction confusion
misconceptions.description: Students shift the decimal point to the right instead of left when multiplying by 0.1 or 0.01. For example, 35 × 0.1 = 350 instead of 3.5. This stems from confusing multiplication by decimals less than 1 with multiplication by whole numbers greater than 1.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect number of place shifts
misconceptions.description: Students shift the decimal point by the wrong number of places. For example, multiplying by 0.1 but shifting 2 places left (treating it as 0.01), or shifting only 1 place when multiplying by 0.01. Often results from not carefully counting the number of decimal places in the multiplier.

misconceptions.label: Treating 0.1 and 0.01 as whole number multipliers
misconceptions.description: Students multiply by 1 and ignore the 0, calculating 45 × 0.1 as 45 × 1 = 45. This reflects a failure to recognise that the 0 before the decimal point is significant and that these are fractional multipliers.

misconceptions.label: Adding zeros instead of shifting decimal
misconceptions.description: Students add zeros to the right of a number instead of moving the decimal point. For example, 23 × 0.1 = 230 or 23 × 0.01 = 2300. This reflects confusion between multiplication by powers of 10 (where zeros are added) and multiplication by decimals (where the decimal point shifts).

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single-digit whole numbers (e.g. 7 × 0.1)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Large whole numbers or decimals with multiple decimal places (e.g. 456.78 × 0.01)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Whole numbers with no decimal part (e.g. 42 × 0.1)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Decimal numbers as input (e.g. 3.5 × 0.1 or 12.6 × 0.01)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Direct application of the shift rule (e.g. multiply 80 × 0.1)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Explaining why the operation works, connecting to division by powers of 10, or extending the pattern to 0.001

transferContexts.example: A shop reduces all prices by multiplying by 0.1 (a 90% discount). If an item costs £34, what is the new price?
Convert 250 millimetres to metres by multiplying by 0.001 (extending the pattern). Alternatively: A recipe calls for 180g of flour, but you want to make 0.1 of the recipe—how much flour do you need?
A rectangle has dimensions 20 cm × 15 cm. If both dimensions are multiplied by 0.1 to create a scale model, what is the area of the scale model in cm²?

generativeContext: N3.17 concerns multiplication by 0.1 and 0.01—operations that halve and then halve again relative to division by 10 and 100. This is a conceptual bottleneck for many KS3 students because it reverses intuition: multiplying makes smaller, not larger. Mastery requires secure understanding that 0.1 = 1/10 and 0.01 = 1/100, and that multiplying by a fraction smaller than 1 reduces magnitude. The most reliable strategy is the decimal-shift rule: multiply by 0.1 shifts the decimal one place left; multiply by 0.01 shifts it two places left. This can be visualised with place-value grids or number lines. Key misconceptions: confusing left/right direction (n3-17-m1), getting the count of shifts wrong (n3-17-m2), ignoring the decimal entirely (n3-17-m3), or adding zeros like in × 10 operations (n3-17-m4). The skill is a stretch because it requires fluency with decimals, understanding of place value, and ability to reverse the × 10 pattern mentally. It also generalises to 0.001, 0.0001, etc., preparing students for scientific notation and metric conversions. Questions should scaffold from whole numbers to decimals, and eventually ask students to explain the mechanism (why does it work?) and extend the rule. Avoid ambiguity in notation; always write 0.1, never .1.

name: Short division (remainders)
masteryDefinition: Students can perform short division (bus-stop method) on numbers with up to 4 digits by 1-digit divisors, correctly identify and write remainders, and interpret remainders in context (e.g. as whole number remainders, fractions, or rounded answers depending on the problem).

misconceptions.label: Ignoring the remainder
misconceptions.description: Student performs short division correctly but omits the remainder from the final answer, writing only the quotient. For example, 47 ÷ 5 = 9 instead of 9 r2.

misconceptions.label: Treating remainder as part of the quotient digit
misconceptions.description: Student writes the remainder as a digit alongside the quotient rather than as a separate remainder. For example, 47 ÷ 5 = 92 (confusing the carried 2 with a digit in the answer).

misconceptions.label: Not carrying the remainder correctly
misconceptions.description: Student fails to carry the remainder from one step to the next in the short division process. For example, dividing 145 ÷ 3, the student correctly gets 4 r1 for 14 ÷ 3, but then divides 5 by 3 independently instead of 15 ÷ 3.

misconceptions.label: Misinterpreting remainder in context
misconceptions.description: Student calculates the correct remainder but misapplies it to the context (e.g. in a grouping problem 'How many groups of 4 from 23 people?' student answers '5 r3' without recognising that 3 people can form a partial group or stating the answer as 5 complete groups with 3 left over).

difficultyDimensions.easy: Dividing by 2, 3, 5
difficultyDimensions.hard: Dividing by 7, 8, 9 (less familiar times tables)

difficultyDimensions.easy: 2-digit numbers (up to 99)
difficultyDimensions.hard: 4-digit numbers (up to 9999)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Simple recognition and notation of remainder (e.g. 'write your remainder')
difficultyDimensions.hard: Interpreting and applying remainder to real contexts (e.g. 'How many minibuses of 8 seats needed for 50 people?')

transferContexts.example: 38 sweets are shared equally among 5 children. How many does each child get, and how many are left over?

transferContexts.context: Real-world division with remainders requiring rounding
transferContexts.example: A baker needs 150 eggs to make 12 cakes. How many eggs per cake? If she only has 145 eggs, how many complete cakes can she make?

transferContexts.context: Expressing remainders as fractions or decimals
transferContexts.example: Divide 29 by 4 using short division. Express the answer as a mixed number (7¼) and as a decimal (7.25).

generativeContext: Short division (also called the bus-stop or long division abbreviated method) is a formal written algorithm for dividing larger numbers by single-digit divisors. The process involves repeatedly dividing, multiplying, and subtracting (or carrying remainders) from left to right through the digits of the dividend. Mastery requires fluency with the times tables of the divisor, accurate carrying of remainders to the next column, and contextual interpretation. Common errors include: omitting remainders entirely; confusing the carried remainder with a quotient digit; failing to carry remainders forward in multi-step divisions; and misapplying remainders to word problems (e.g. rounding up when sharing discrete objects, vs. rounding down when determining complete groups). Difficulty scales with divisor size (familiarity of times tables), dividend magnitude (cognitive load of tracking carries), and the cognitive demand of the context (bare calculation vs. interpretation in sharing/grouping scenarios). Link to mental division strategies for small dividends and to long division for division by 2-digit numbers. Emphasise that the remainder is always smaller than the divisor.

name: Short division (decimal answers)
masteryDefinition: Students can perform short division (bus-stop method) on integers and decimals to produce decimal answers, correctly placing the decimal point in the quotient, understanding when remainders become decimal places, and verifying answers by multiplication. They can divide by single-digit and two-digit divisors, handle cases where the dividend is smaller than the divisor (producing answers less than 1), and express remainders as decimals rather than whole number remainders.

misconceptions.label: Decimal point misplacement in quotient
misconceptions.description: Student places the decimal point in the quotient at the wrong position, often directly above the decimal point in the dividend rather than determining its true position by the magnitude of the answer. For example, 7.5 ÷ 3 gives 2.5 instead of 2.5, or 15 ÷ 4 gives 3.75 with decimal inserted incorrectly mid-calculation.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring the remainder and not converting to decimal
misconceptions.description: Student performs short division correctly up to a remainder but stops, writing only the whole number part of the answer (e.g., 17 ÷ 5 = 3 remainder 2, then stops) rather than continuing to convert the remainder into decimal places by adding zeros and continuing the division.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect handling of decimal dividend during short division
misconceptions.description: Student struggles to manage decimals in the dividend during the short division process, either moving the decimal point incorrectly, losing track of it mid-calculation, or not aligning it properly with place values. For example, 2.4 ÷ 3 is calculated as 24 ÷ 3 = 8 instead of 0.8.

misconceptions.label: Carrying remainders incorrectly when converting to decimals
misconceptions.description: Student appends zeros to continue division but makes arithmetic errors in the short division process, such as incorrectly multiplying or subtracting when working with the carried remainder. For example, in 10 ÷ 3, after getting 3 remainder 1, adding a zero to make 10 and dividing again yields an incorrect digit.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Dividing two-digit whole numbers by single-digit divisors (e.g., 24 ÷ 3)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Dividing decimals with tenths/hundredths places by two-digit divisors, or where dividend < divisor (e.g., 3 ÷ 8)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Whole number dividends with no decimal point (e.g., 15 ÷ 4)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Dividends with multiple decimal places (e.g., 2.45 ÷ 7) or mixed whole and decimal parts

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of decimal places required in quotient
difficultyDimensions.easy: Division that terminates after 1–2 decimal places (e.g., 10 ÷ 4 = 2.5)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Division requiring 3+ decimal places or repeating decimals (e.g., 10 ÷ 3 = 3.333...)

transferContexts.example: Express 7/8 as a decimal using short division (divide 7 by 8)
£45 is shared equally among 8 people. How much does each person get? (45 ÷ 8)
If 6 identical items cost £9.60, what is the cost of 1 item? (9.60 ÷ 6)

generativeContext: Short division with decimal answers builds on students' mastery of whole-number short division (N3.18) by extending the method to produce quotients with decimal places. The core challenge is understanding that remainders become decimal places by appending zeros and continuing the division process. Key procedural steps: (1) set up short division layout with divisor outside, dividend inside; (2) divide each digit left to right, writing quotient above; (3) when a remainder occurs, append a zero to it and continue dividing, inserting a decimal point in the quotient at the appropriate position (after the units digit of the dividend); (4) repeat step 3 until the remainder is zero or the required precision is reached. Critical conceptual requirement: students must recognize that the decimal point's position in the quotient is determined by the magnitude of the number, not by mechanically placing it above the dividend's decimal point. When the dividend is a whole number, students often forget that zeros can be appended indefinitely to create decimal places. When the dividend is already a decimal, tracking the decimal point position is vital—it should be placed in the quotient once the units digit of the integer part has been divided. Verification by multiplication (quotient × divisor ≈ dividend) is an essential check. Common remediation involves explicit comparison of decimal positions in dividend vs. quotient using place-value language and estimation to check reasonableness (e.g., 15 ÷ 4 is close to 16 ÷ 4 = 4, so answer near 4, not 0.4).

name: Divide decimals (by an integer / by a decimal; incl ÷0.1, ÷0.2, ÷0.5 etc.)
masteryDefinition: Students can divide decimal numbers by integers and by decimals with confidence and accuracy. They understand that dividing by a decimal less than 1 produces a larger result, can apply the equivalence principle (multiplying both dividend and divisor by the same power of 10), and can handle special cases like ÷0.1, ÷0.2, ÷0.5 efficiently. They can also articulate the conceptual reason why dividing by 0.5 is equivalent to multiplying by 2, and apply this reasoning to other unit fractions expressed as decimals.

misconceptions.label: Dividing by a decimal always makes the answer smaller
misconceptions.description: Students believe that division always produces a smaller result, regardless of the divisor. They fail to recognise that dividing by a number between 0 and 1 (e.g. 0.5, 0.2) actually makes the result larger. For example, 4 ÷ 0.5 = 8, not 2.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect movement of decimal point when dividing
misconceptions.description: Students mechanically move the decimal point in the wrong direction or by the wrong number of places when dividing by decimals. For example, when dividing 6.4 ÷ 0.2, they move the decimal point left instead of right, or move it inconsistently. They may also fail to adjust the dividend correctly when converting to whole-number division (e.g., not multiplying both by the same factor).

misconceptions.label: Treating ÷0.1, ÷0.2, ÷0.5 as standard long division without pattern recognition
misconceptions.description: Students perform lengthy long division for ÷0.1 or ÷0.2 rather than recognising efficient shortcuts (÷0.1 = ×10, ÷0.2 = ×5, ÷0.5 = ×2). They do not see the inverse relationship between these operations and multiplication, leading to inefficiency and increased error risk.

misconceptions.label: Confusion between place value and decimal scaling when adjusting divisor
misconceptions.description: When converting division by a decimal to division by an integer (e.g., 7.5 ÷ 0.3 → 75 ÷ 3), students multiply only the divisor by 10 or only the dividend, rather than both by the same power of 10. This breaks the equivalence and produces incorrect answers. For example: 7.5 ÷ 0.3, they compute 7.5 ÷ 3 = 2.5 instead of 75 ÷ 3 = 25.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Dividing by integers or decimals ≥1 (e.g. 5.6 ÷ 2, 3.2 ÷ 1.6)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Dividing by decimals < 0.5 or by unit fractions expressed decimally (e.g. 2 ÷ 0.1, 3.6 ÷ 0.25)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of decimal places in divisor
difficultyDimensions.easy: Divisors with single decimal place (e.g. 4.8 ÷ 0.2, 8 ÷ 0.5)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Divisors with two or more decimal places (e.g. 6.25 ÷ 0.125, 3.456 ÷ 0.008)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Dividing by named decimals with obvious fraction equivalents (÷0.5, ÷0.25, ÷0.2)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Dividing by arbitrary decimals with no intuitive shortcut (÷0.37, ÷0.48)

transferContexts.context: Scaling and rate problems in science or engineering contexts
transferContexts.example: A liquid is diluted by a factor of 0.5. If the final concentration is 3.2 g/L, what was the original concentration?
If £1 = €1.2, how many pounds is €36? (Requires dividing by 1.2)
A distance of 4.5 km is divided equally among 0.3 km segments. How many segments are there?

generativeContext: This is a stretch skill for N3 (Year 7 decimals). Students have secure understanding of dividing decimals by whole numbers. The conceptual leap here is understanding that dividing by a number less than 1 multiplies the result—this directly contradicts intuition from whole-number division and must be explicitly modelled using area models, number lines, or bar models showing how many 0.5s fit into 4 (answer: 8, not 2). The equivalence strategy—multiply both numerator and denominator by a power of 10 to convert to whole-number division—is the primary procedural tool, but it only works if students understand why multiplying both by the same factor preserves the quotient. Pattern recognition with special divisors (0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 0.25) should be encouraged but not made mandatory; efficiency gains are a bonus outcome, not the core skill. Word problems involving scaling (enlargement/reduction), dilution, currency conversion, and unit scaling are rich contexts. Be alert to students who move decimal points mechanically without understanding equivalence, and those who assume division always reduces magnitude.

name: Find missing lengths given area (rectangles, parallelograms, triangles and compound shapes)
masteryDefinition: Students can fluently rearrange area formulae to find missing dimensions (length, width, base, height) for rectangles, parallelograms, and triangles. They can identify which dimension is missing, select the correct formula, and solve linear equations to find the unknown length. They can also decompose compound shapes into simpler components, calculate missing lengths of parts, and verify answers by checking that the calculated dimensions produce the given area.

misconceptions.label: Dividing by 2 for all shapes
misconceptions.description: Students apply the triangle area formula (÷2) to parallelograms and rectangles. For example, given a rectangle with area 24 cm² and base 6 cm, they calculate height as 24 ÷ 6 ÷ 2 = 2 cm instead of 24 ÷ 6 = 4 cm. This occurs because they memorise 'divide by 2' as a generic area rule without understanding that the factor of 2 is specific to triangles.

misconceptions.label: Confusing height with slant length in triangles and parallelograms
misconceptions.description: Students use the slant side length or hypotenuse instead of perpendicular height in the formula. For instance, given a triangle with area 20 cm² and hypotenuse 8 cm, they treat 8 as the height rather than recognising they need the perpendicular height, leading to incorrect calculations or inability to solve the problem.

misconceptions.label: Incorrectly rearranging multiplication to subtraction
misconceptions.description: When solving Area = base × height for a missing dimension, students subtract instead of divide. For example, given area 30 and base 5, they write height = 30 − 5 = 25 instead of height = 30 ÷ 5 = 6. This reflects weak understanding of inverse operations and equation solving.

misconceptions.label: Not decomposing compound shapes correctly
misconceptions.description: Students fail to identify how to split a compound shape into rectangles and triangles, or they count overlapping regions twice. For example, an L-shaped figure is treated as a single rectangle rather than two rectangles, preventing them from setting up equations to find missing dimensions of component parts.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single rectangle or simple triangle with all dimensions clearly labelled except one
difficultyDimensions.hard: Compound shape (L-shape, trapezoid-like composite) requiring decomposition and solving for an interior dimension using area relationships between parts

difficultyDimensions.easy: One-step division: Area ÷ known dimension = missing dimension
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multi-step equation requiring rearrangement, fractions, or solving when the missing dimension appears in a product (e.g., base × (height + 2) = Area)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Shape type explicitly stated; student only applies one formula
difficultyDimensions.hard: Mixed problem set or compound shape where student must select which formula(e) to apply and interpret which dimension is 'height' or 'base'

transferContexts.example: A rectangular garden has area 48 m². If its length is 12 m, find its width. Then extend: a triangular flower bed has the same base as the garden's width but area 15 m². Find the perpendicular height of the flower bed.

A parallelogram-shaped floor tile has area 60 cm² and base 8 cm. Find its perpendicular height. Now a designer tiles a room with a compound shape (two such tiles and a triangular section). Given total area, find the missing height of the triangle.

A poster is a rectangle with area 0.5 m². Its width is 0.5 m. Find its height. The poster is then cropped: a triangular section with base equal to the poster's height is removed. If that triangle had area 0.1 m², find its perpendicular height.

generativeContext: This skill requires students to reverse-engineer shape dimensions from area. The core challenge is understanding that area formulae are multiplicative relationships (area = base × height for rectangles/parallelograms; area = 0.5 × base × height for triangles) and that finding a missing dimension means dividing area by the known dimension. Crucially, students must correctly identify perpendicular height, not side length or slant. For compound shapes, decomposition is essential: students must split the figure into constituent rectangles/triangles, use partial areas (or total area minus parts), and set up equations linking dimensions. Common errors include: applying ÷2 universally; confusing height with slant or side; using subtraction instead of division; misidentifying which segments are bases/heights; and failing to correctly decompose or measure perpendicular distances in tilted shapes. Question design should progress from single shapes with single unknowns, to shapes where height must be inferred from a diagram, to compound shapes requiring decomposition and multi-step algebra. Emphasise the role of perpendicularity, use grid backgrounds to clarify height measurement, and provide practice in recognising height in different orientations (vertical, diagonal).

name: Solve problems using the mean
masteryDefinition: Students can calculate the mean of a dataset, use the mean to solve problems involving missing values, and interpret the mean in context. They understand that the mean is the sum of all values divided by the count, can find missing values when the mean is known, and can apply mean calculations to real-world scenarios involving averages.

misconceptions.label: Counting the wrong number of values
misconceptions.description: Students include zero or exclude zero when counting the number of values in a dataset. For example, when finding the mean of 5, 8, and 12, they might count only 2 values instead of 3, or in a dataset with a zero value (e.g., 0, 4, 6) they skip counting the zero.

misconceptions.label: Using the median or mode instead of mean
misconceptions.description: When asked to find the mean, students calculate the median (middle value) or mode (most frequent value) instead. This often occurs when the terminology is unfamiliar or when they confuse the three measures of central tendency.

misconceptions.label: Forgetting to divide when calculating mean
misconceptions.description: Students correctly sum all values but forget to divide by the number of values, reporting the total as the mean. For example, when finding the mean of 10, 20, and 30, they report 60 instead of 20.

misconceptions.label: Incorrectly rearranging the mean formula
misconceptions.description: When given the mean and asked to find a missing value, students misapply the formula. They might multiply instead of using division, or add when they should subtract, leading to incorrect missing values. For example, if the mean of three numbers is 8 and two numbers are 5 and 9, they might calculate 8 × 3 + 5 + 9 instead of 8 × 3 − 5 − 9.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Mean of 2–3 values (e.g., 10, 20)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Mean of 5+ values with larger numbers or decimals (e.g., 12.5, 18.3, 24.6, 31.2, 29.8)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Presence of zero or negative values
difficultyDimensions.easy: All values are positive integers
difficultyDimensions.hard: Dataset includes zero, negative numbers, or both (e.g., −5, 0, 8, 12)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Direct calculation: find the mean of a given list
difficultyDimensions.hard: Reverse problem: find missing value(s) given the mean, or interpret mean in context requiring comparison or decision-making

transferContexts.example: A student's test scores are 75, 82, and 88. What is their average score? If they want an average of 85 across four tests, what must they score on the fourth test?

Two classes took the same maths test. Class A's mean score is 72; Class B's mean score is 78. There are 30 students in each class. Which class performed better overall, and how many more marks did Class B accumulate in total?
The mean height of five students is 160 cm. Four of the students have heights 152, 158, 165, and 163 cm. What is the height of the fifth student?

generativeContext: Mean (average) is a core measure of central tendency in KS3 statistics. Students must master two complementary skills: (1) computing the mean directly using sum ÷ count, and (2) rearranging the relationship (mean × count = sum) to solve inverse problems where the mean is known and a value is missing. Common errors cluster around careless counting, formula confusion, and conflation with median/mode. Difficulty scales with dataset size, presence of zero/negative values, and problem complexity (direct vs. inverse). Context matters: students should see means applied to test scores, measurements, survey data, and comparisons. Use concrete step-by-step language ('add them all up, then divide by how many numbers') initially, then move to symbolic work. Emphasise that the mean is not always a value in the dataset, and that outliers (very large or very small values) strongly affect it.

name: Square and cube numbers, roots
masteryDefinition: Students can identify and calculate square numbers (1², 2², 3², ..., 12²) and cube numbers (1³, 2³, 3³, ..., 5³), recognise perfect squares and cubes, and find square roots and cube roots of numbers, including understanding that non-perfect squares/cubes have irrational roots. They can interpret and use the notation √ and ³√ correctly, and apply these concepts in context.

misconceptions.label: Confusing square root with halving
misconceptions.description: Students incorrectly believe √16 = 8 (i.e. divide by 2) rather than √16 = 4. This stems from conflating 'square root' with 'half' or misremembering the operation as division rather than the inverse of squaring.

misconceptions.label: Distributing the square operation over addition
misconceptions.description: Students claim (a + b)² = a² + b² rather than a² + 2ab + b². For example, they think (3 + 2)² = 9 + 4 = 13 instead of 25. This reflects a failure to recognise that squaring is non-linear.

misconceptions.label: Treating cube root as halving a cube root operation
misconceptions.description: Students confuse ³√8 with √8 ÷ 2 or ³√8 = 8 ÷ 3, rather than understanding ³√8 = 2 (the number which when cubed gives 8). Often stems from weak understanding of inverse operations.

misconceptions.label: Assuming all square roots are whole numbers
misconceptions.description: Students believe √7 must equal a whole number (rounding to 2 or 3) or attempting to simplify √7 as if it were a perfect square. Difficulty accepting irrational numbers or approximations as valid answers.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Squaring or cubing single-digit numbers (e.g. 2², 3³)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Squaring or cubing larger numbers (e.g. 11², 15², 5³) requiring multi-step calculation or mental effort

difficultyDimensions.easy: Perfect squares and cubes with integer roots (√36, ³√27)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Non-perfect squares/cubes requiring estimation or approximation (√50, ³√100), or involving negative numbers (e.g. ³√-8)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single operation: given base, find square/cube, or given perfect square, find root
difficultyDimensions.hard: Chained operations or problem-solving: e.g. finding the side length of a square garden given area, or evaluating 2 + √9 × 3

transferContexts.context: Geometry: finding side lengths from area or volume
transferContexts.example: A square tile has an area of 144 cm². What is the side length? (Requires understanding √144 = 12 in a spatial context.)
A cube-shaped storage box has a volume of 500 cm³. Estimate the side length. (Requires approximating ³√500 ≈ 7.9 cm.)
The nth square number is n². Write down the 10th square number, then find which square number equals 144. (Requires recognising the pattern and using inverse reasoning.)

generativeContext: Square and cube numbers are fundamental building blocks for algebraic and geometric reasoning in KS3. Mastery requires fluency with the 12 principal square numbers (1–144) and the first 5 cube numbers (1–125), plus confident handling of radical notation and inverse operations. Students must distinguish between squaring as a multiplicative operation (inherently non-linear) and addition; recognise perfect powers versus non-perfect; and develop intuition for approximating roots of non-perfect powers. Cube roots are less intuitive than square roots; explicitly anchor them via multiplication ('what × what × what = 8?'). Non-perfect squares/cubes introduce irrationals; frame these as 'numbers between integers' rather than as defective answers. Common errors centre on procedural confusion (square root as halving), algebraic distribution myths, and treating roots as always rational. Generative tasks should scaffold from recognition and recall (Is 49 a perfect square?) through calculation (√225 = ?) to estimation (place √30 on a number line between integers) and contextual application (side length from area). Avoid purely procedural drilling; embed roots in geometric and real-world contexts where they have meaning. Use error diagnosis to catch distribution errors early, as they resurface in binomial expansion.

name: Introduction to primes
masteryDefinition: Students can identify prime numbers and non-prime (composite) numbers within a given range, explain why 1 is not prime, and use the definition of a prime as a number with exactly two distinct factors (1 and itself) to justify their classifications.

misconceptions.label: 1 is prime
misconceptions.description: Students believe 1 is a prime number because it is only divisible by itself. They have not internalized that a prime must have exactly two distinct factors, and 1 has only one factor (itself).

misconceptions.label: All odd numbers are prime
misconceptions.description: Students assume that because prime numbers like 3, 5, 7 are odd, all odd numbers must be prime. They fail to test composite odd numbers like 9, 15, or 21 by dividing.

misconceptions.label: 2 is not prime because it is even
misconceptions.description: Students overgeneralize the pattern that odd numbers tend to be prime and incorrectly classify 2 as composite or non-prime, missing that 2 has exactly two factors (1 and 2).

misconceptions.label: A number is prime if it is not divisible by small numbers (2, 3, 5)
misconceptions.description: Students use incomplete divisibility tests and conclude a number is prime without checking all factors up to its square root. For example, they might claim 91 is prime because it is not divisible by 2, 3, or 5, missing that 91 = 7 × 13.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Identifying primes in the range 1–20 (2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Identifying primes in the range 1–100 and determining primality of two-digit numbers like 97, 91, or 87 by systematic factor testing

difficultyDimensions.easy: Simply labeling a number as prime or not prime
difficultyDimensions.hard: Explaining why a number is prime by listing its factors or proving a composite number has more than two factors

difficultyDimensions.easy: Separating primes from even numbers or numbers ending in 5
difficultyDimensions.hard: Distinguishing primes from factors, multiples, and composite numbers in mixed or word-problem contexts

transferContexts.example: List all the factor pairs of 36. Which factors are prime numbers?
Express 24 as a product of prime numbers.
A farmer wants to arrange 17 plants in rows with equal numbers in each row, with no plants left over. Why is this difficult to do?

generativeContext: N3.24 introduces the concept of prime numbers as natural numbers with exactly two distinct factors: 1 and the number itself. This is foundational to number theory and factorization work. Students must distinguish primes from composite numbers (which have more than two factors) and recognize that 1 is neither prime nor composite by definition. Key teaching points: (1) primes are defined by the count of factors, not by being odd or small; (2) systematic checking by division or listing factors is the correct method; (3) 2 is the only even prime; (4) no universal divisibility rule eliminates all testing—primality often requires checking divisibility up to the square root. Common errors include misclassifying 1 as prime, assuming all odd numbers are prime, or using incomplete divisibility checks. Difficulty scales with number size and the need for explicit justification. Primes connect directly to factorization, multiples, and GCD/LCM, and appear in cryptography and patterns. Generate questions with explicit factor-listing, justification demands, and carefully chosen distractors (e.g., 91, 87, composite odds).

name: Understand a fraction as part of a whole and locate simple fractions on a number line
masteryDefinition: Students can identify and name fractions (including halves, quarters, thirds, fifths, eighths, and tenths) as equal parts of a whole shape or quantity, recognise that the denominator tells you how many equal parts the whole is divided into and the numerator tells you how many of those parts are shaded or selected, and accurately place simple fractions on a number line between 0 and 1 by dividing the interval into equal divisions corresponding to the denominator.

misconceptions.label: Counting shaded regions instead of parts
misconceptions.description: Students count the total number of shaded and unshaded regions rather than identifying equal parts. For example, in a shape divided into 4 equal parts with 2 shaded, they may say the fraction is 1/2 if there are 2 shaded regions out of 4 total, but fail to recognise that only 2 equal parts were shaded when the shape could have been divided unequally.

misconceptions.label: Misunderstanding denominator as a label rather than divisor
misconceptions.description: Students treat the denominator as a label for the shape type rather than understanding it represents the number of equal parts. They may correctly identify a quarter but cannot explain that a quarter means the whole has been divided into 4 equal parts, or fail to see that the same denominator must represent the same size part regardless of the whole's shape or size.

misconceptions.label: Unequal parts treated as fractions
misconceptions.description: Students accept and work with shapes divided into unequal parts as if they represent valid fractions. For example, they may say a circle divided into 3 unequal regions with 1 shaded is 1/3, without recognising that the parts must be equal for the notation to be meaningful.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect placement on number line due to ignoring equal intervals
misconceptions.description: Students place fractions on a number line by guessing or using incorrect spacing, not dividing the interval into equal parts. For instance, they may place 1/3 and 2/3 on a 0–1 number line without first dividing it into three equal sections, resulting in positions that don't correspond to the correct values.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Fractions with denominators 2, 3, or 4 (halves, thirds, quarters)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Fractions with denominators 5, 8, 10 or mixed sets requiring comparison of different denominators

difficultyDimensions.easy: Rectangular or circular shapes already divided into equal parts with shading clearly marked
difficultyDimensions.hard: Irregular shapes, composite wholes (e.g. groups of objects), or abstract number lines without pre-marked divisions

difficultyDimensions.easy: Number line from 0 to 1 with pre-marked equal intervals or tick marks indicating the required divisions
difficultyDimensions.hard: Unlabelled number line requiring students to independently identify and construct equal intervals for a given denominator

transferContexts.context: Sharing and division in everyday contexts
transferContexts.example: If a pizza is cut into 8 equal slices and you eat 3, what fraction have you eaten? Where would 3/8 sit on a number line from 0 to 1?
A recipe calls for 3/4 of a cup of flour. Mark 3/4 on a number line showing cups from 0 to 2, and identify what 1/4 and 2/4 represent on the same scale.
Place 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, and 3/4 on the same 0–1 number line and explain which is larger and why, using the concept of equal parts.

generativeContext: N4.1 is the foundational unit fraction and simple fraction concept. Mastery requires students to internalise three linked ideas: (1) a fraction represents a whole divided into n equal parts, with m of those parts selected, (2) equality of parts is non-negotiable—unequal divisions are not fractions, and (3) the denominator defines the unit size, not the shape. Diagnostic tasks should include: identifying fractions in shapes divided unequally (to catch m1 and m3), explaining what a denominator means in words (to catch m2), and placing fractions on blank number lines (to catch m4). Common procedural errors: counting all regions (m1), ignoring unequal divisions (m3), spacing fractions arbitrarily on number lines (m4). Avoid: assuming students see 'fourths' as a shape property rather than a divisor. Emphasise: denominator always tells you 'how many equal parts'; numerator tells you 'how many of those'; parts must be visually equal and equal in area/quantity. Use consistent language: 'one quarter' = 'one out of four equal parts', not 'a piece of something called a quarter'. Number line work must begin with pre-marked intervals, then progress to student-constructed intervals. Misconceptions are stable from KS2 and can persist if fraction language remains decoupled from division and equal-sharing.

name: Generate equivalent fractions
masteryDefinition: Pupils can identify and generate fractions that are equal in value by multiplying or dividing both numerator and denominator by the same non-zero number. They understand the multiplicative relationship underpinning equivalence and can apply this to simplify fractions to lowest terms and to create equivalent fractions with specified denominators.

misconceptions.label: Adding the same number to numerator and denominator
misconceptions.description: Pupils incorrectly believe that adding the same number to both numerator and denominator creates an equivalent fraction (e.g., 1/2 + 1 = 2/3). They confuse the additive strategy with the correct multiplicative rule.

misconceptions.label: Operating on numerator and denominator independently
misconceptions.description: Pupils multiply or divide only the numerator or only the denominator, losing the multiplicative relationship (e.g., claiming 2/4 = 2/8 or 3/6 = 6/6). They fail to recognise that the same operation must apply to both parts.

misconceptions.label: Confusing simplification with equivalence
misconceptions.description: Pupils believe that a simplified fraction and its unsimplified form are different values, or they assume all fractions must be simplified to be 'correct'. They lack understanding that 2/4 and 1/2 represent the same quantity despite different notation.

misconceptions.label: Multiplying by different numbers
misconceptions.description: Pupils multiply the numerator and denominator by different numbers (e.g., 1/2 × 3/4 = 3/8), misunderstanding that the scaling factor must be identical for both parts to preserve value.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Multiplying by 2 or 3 (e.g., 1/2 × 2 = 2/4)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiplying by larger numbers or by multiples of 10 (e.g., 3/5 × 20 = 60/100)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Generating equivalent fractions by multiplying (expanding to larger numerator/denominator)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Simplifying by dividing both parts (requires identifying common factors; e.g., reducing 12/18 to 2/3)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Unit fractions (1/n) and fractions with small whole numbers (e.g., 2/3, 3/4)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Fractions with larger numerators or those requiring multiple equivalent representations (e.g., 7/12, 15/20)

transferContexts.example: Which is larger: 3/5 or 5/8? Generate equivalent fractions with a common denominator to compare them.
A recipe calls for 2/3 cup of flour, but you want to make half the recipe. Generate an equivalent fraction to find the correct amount.
Express 3/20 as an equivalent fraction with denominator 100, then convert to a percentage.

generativeContext: N4.2 targets the core multiplicative principle of fraction equivalence. Students must move beyond additive thinking and internalise that multiplying (or dividing) both numerator and denominator by the same factor preserves the fraction's value. Mastery requires fluency with:  (1) Generating equivalent fractions using multiplication (2/3 = 4/6 = 6/9); (2) Simplifying by dividing out common factors (12/18 = 2/3); (3) Finding a specific equivalent fraction with a given denominator (2/5 = ?/20); (4) Recognising that infinite equivalent forms exist for any fraction. Misconception n4-2-m1 (adding instead of multiplying) is very common and stems from confusion with additive models taught earlier. Misconception n4-2-m2 (operating independently on numerator/denominator) reveals incomplete understanding of the 'both parts together' rule. When generating questions, vary the multiplier size, mix expansion and simplification tasks, and use contexts where equivalence matters (comparing, ordering, recipes, percentages). Avoid implying that one form is 'more correct'; emphasise that 2/4 and 1/2 are identical in value but different in representation. For stretch, introduce more complex simplifications (e.g., 24/36), links to ratio, or three-fraction chains (1/2 = 2/4 = 4/8).

name: Simplify a fraction using factors/HCF
masteryDefinition: Students can identify the highest common factor (HCF) of the numerator and denominator of a fraction, and use it to reduce the fraction to its simplest form in one step. They understand that simplifying does not change the value of the fraction, and can verify their answer by checking that the numerator and denominator share no common factors other than 1.

misconceptions.label: Subtracting instead of dividing by HCF
misconceptions.description: Students subtract the HCF from both numerator and denominator instead of dividing. For example, simplifying 12/18 by subtracting 6 to get 6/12, rather than dividing both by 6 to get 2/3.

misconceptions.label: Only dividing the numerator or denominator
misconceptions.description: Students divide only the numerator by a factor, or only the denominator, but not both. For example, simplifying 12/18 to 6/18 or 12/9 instead of 2/3.

misconceptions.label: Confusing HCF with a non-common factor
misconceptions.description: Students identify a factor of one part but not both, or choose an arbitrary common factor instead of the highest one. For example, using 2 to simplify 12/18 to get 6/9, rather than recognising 6 is the HCF and simplifying to 2/3.

misconceptions.label: Cancelling digits rather than finding factors
misconceptions.description: Students cancel out digits that appear in both numerator and denominator without understanding factors. For example, with 15/51, crossing out the 1's to get 5/5 = 1, instead of finding HCF of 3 to get 5/17.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Small integers (numerator and denominator both under 20)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Larger integers (numerator or denominator over 50) requiring systematic factor listing

difficultyDimensions.easy: One number is a multiple of the other (e.g. 5/15, where 5 divides 15)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Neither is a multiple of the other; HCF must be found via prime factorisation or listing (e.g. 24/36)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of steps to reach simplest form
difficultyDimensions.easy: Fraction simplifies in one division by the HCF
difficultyDimensions.hard: Fraction is already partially simplified, requiring recognition that further simplification is possible or impossible

transferContexts.context: Ordering fractions by converting to a common denominator
transferContexts.example: Simplify 8/12 and 9/15 before finding the LCD to compare them; recognising 8/12 = 2/3 and 9/15 = 3/5 makes finding LCD faster.
A bag contains 6 red marbles and 9 blue marbles. Express the probability of drawing red as a simplified fraction.
Simplify (3x)/(6x²) by cancelling common factors; reinforces the principle that both numerator and denominator must be divided by the same value.

generativeContext: N4.3 focuses on reducing fractions to lowest terms via HCF. This is foundational for all fraction arithmetic and algebra. At KS3, students move from listing factors by trial to systematic methods (prime factorisation, repeated division). Key conceptual anchors: (1) simplifying preserves the fraction's value; (2) HCF is the largest factor common to both parts; (3) the simplified form has no common factors except 1 (coprime). Generator should vary numerator/denominator sizes, include fractions already simplified and those needing multi-step simplification if preceded by easier content, and avoid purely mechanical repetition. Common error patterns: arithmetic (subtracting HCF, dividing only one part), conceptual (choosing a non-maximal common factor, confusing the process with digit cancelling), and procedural (losing track of whether a fraction is fully simplified). Assess via showing working and asking students to verify their answer.

name: Convert a fraction to a decimal (terminating decimals)
masteryDefinition: A student can reliably convert any fraction with a denominator that is a factor of a power of 10 into its terminating decimal equivalent, using either long division or denominator manipulation (scaling to 10, 100, 1000, etc.). They understand when a fraction will terminate as a decimal and can verify their answer.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring the denominator when converting
misconceptions.description: Student writes the numerator as the decimal without considering the denominator. For example, 3/10 becomes 3 instead of 0.3, or 7/100 becomes 7 instead of 0.07. The student treats the fraction bar as insignificant or forgets to divide.

misconceptions.label: Placing the decimal point in the wrong position
misconceptions.description: Student performs the division correctly but misplaces the decimal point in the result. For example, 1/4 = 0.25 but student writes 2.5 or 25, confusing the magnitude. Often caused by not counting decimal places from the denominator correctly.

misconceptions.label: Confusion between numerator and denominator in long division
misconceptions.description: When using long division, student divides the denominator by the numerator (inverse), or missets up the division layout. For example, to convert 3/8, they attempt 8 ÷ 3 instead of 3 ÷ 8, giving 0.375 as 2.666... or similar.

misconceptions.label: Assuming all fractions terminate as decimals
misconceptions.description: Student attempts to convert fractions like 1/3 or 2/7 to terminating decimals and either records a rounded result as exact, or believes the conversion is possible when it isn't. Does not recognise that only denominators with factors of 2 and 5 (in powers of 10) will terminate.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Denominator is a factor of 10 or 100 (e.g. 2, 4, 5, 8, 10, 20, 25, 50, 100)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Denominator is a factor of 1000 or requires multiple steps to scale (e.g. 16, 125, 200, 250)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Denominator can be directly scaled to a power of 10 without long division (e.g. 3/10, 7/100, 1/4 = 25/100)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Long division is the most efficient method, especially when scaling is cumbersome (e.g. 3/8, 7/20, 11/16)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Numerator is smaller and simpler than denominator (e.g. 1/4, 2/5, 3/8)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Numerator is large or close to the denominator, requiring careful long division and decimal place tracking (e.g. 17/20, 23/25, 99/125)

transferContexts.context: Comparison of fractions and decimals in real-world measurement
transferContexts.example: A recipe requires 3/8 of a cup of flour. Convert this to a decimal so you can use a digital scale that only displays decimal amounts.

transferContexts.context: Converting prices or monetary fractions to standard decimal notation
transferContexts.example: A shop has a sale where items cost 7/20 of the original price. Write this as a decimal so it can be entered into a till system.

transferContexts.context: Data presentation and graphing in science or statistics contexts
transferContexts.example: In an experiment, 9/16 of the sample reacted. Express this as a decimal for a spreadsheet graph.

generativeContext: N4.4 focuses on converting terminating fractions to decimals. A fraction terminates if and only if its denominator (in lowest terms) has only factors of 2 and 5. The two main methods are: (1) scaling the denominator to a power of 10 and applying the same scale to the numerator, then reading off decimal places; (2) long division, which divides the numerator by the denominator, inserting a decimal point after the ones place and bringing down zeros. Key teaching points: emphasise that division by the denominator is essential (not ignoring it); use place-value understanding to position the decimal correctly (tenths, hundredths, thousandths); show that 1/4 = 0.25 because 4 × 25 = 100, so the numerator scales by 25; and model long division explicitly, pausing at each step to verify decimal placement. Common errors cluster around ignoring the denominator, misplacing the decimal, inverting the division order, and assuming all fractions terminate. Diagnostic tasks should distinguish between procedural errors (decimal placement) and conceptual gaps (not knowing which fractions terminate). Ensure students see examples where scaling is natural (e.g. 3/4 → 75/100 → 0.75) and where long division is preferable (e.g. 3/8 → 0.375).

name: Convert a decimal to a fraction (simple/terminating)
masteryDefinition: A student can accurately convert any terminating decimal (to at least 2 decimal places) into its simplest form fraction. This includes: identifying the correct denominator based on place value (tenths, hundredths, thousandths); writing the decimal digits as the numerator; and simplifying by finding and dividing by the greatest common factor. The student can explain and justify their method using place value understanding.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring or forgetting to simplify the fraction
misconceptions.description: Student converts 0.25 to 25/100 and stops, failing to simplify to 1/4. They may write the correct denominator and numerator but treat simplification as optional or forget the step entirely. This is the most common error at KS3.

misconceptions.label: Using the wrong denominator based on place value confusion
misconceptions.description: Student converts 0.4 to 4/10 correctly but converts 0.04 to 4/100 when they write it as 4/10 instead, or confuses which power of 10 to use. For example, treating 0.35 as 35/10 instead of 35/100, or writing 0.5 as 5/10 but then claiming the denominator should be 100 because 'there are two numbers after the decimal'.

misconceptions.label: Inverting the fraction (numerator/denominator swap)
misconceptions.description: Student converts 0.6 to 6/10 but writes it as 10/6 or confuses which value goes 'on top.' This typically stems from weak understanding of fraction notation rather than decimal conversion itself.

misconceptions.label: Treating the decimal as a whole number denominator
misconceptions.description: Student converts 0.25 to 25/0.25 or writes the decimal point into the denominator (e.g., 25/0.100). This reflects a fundamental misunderstanding of how place value relates to denominator selection.

difficultyDimensions.easy: 1 decimal place (e.g., 0.3, 0.7)
difficultyDimensions.hard: 3 or more decimal places (e.g., 0.125, 0.375, 0.625)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Obvious common factors or already in simplest form (e.g., 0.5 = 1/2, 0.2 = 1/5)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Requires finding a less obvious GCF (e.g., 0.45 = 9/20, 0.36 = 9/25, 0.48 = 12/25)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Presence of leading zeros or fractional position
difficultyDimensions.easy: Decimals with non-zero tenths place (e.g., 0.6, 0.75, 0.8)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Decimals with zeros between decimal point and first non-zero digit or less intuitive values (e.g., 0.04, 0.008, 0.125)

transferContexts.context: Ratio and proportion problems requiring conversion of decimal ratios into fraction form
transferContexts.example: A recipe calls for ingredients in the ratio 0.5 : 1. Express 0.5 as a fraction to find equivalent ratios for double batches.

transferContexts.context: Probability expressed as decimals needing conversion to fractions for combinatorial reasoning
transferContexts.example: The probability of an event is 0.75. Write this as a fraction in simplest form to determine how many favourable outcomes occur in 20 trials.

transferContexts.context: Measurement and scaling in practical contexts (cooking, models, maps)
transferContexts.example: A model car is 0.12 metres long. Express this as a fraction of a metre to compare with other scaled models.

generativeContext: Converting terminating decimals to fractions requires secure understanding of place value, especially powers of 10 (tenths = 1/10, hundredths = 1/100, thousandths = 1/1000). The algorithm is: (1) count decimal places to determine denominator; (2) remove the decimal point to create the numerator; (3) simplify by dividing numerator and denominator by their GCF. Students must recognise that 0.6, 0.60, and 0.600 are equivalent, so 6/10, 60/100, and 600/1000 all simplify to 3/5. Common pitfalls include forgetting simplification (treating 25/100 as final), swapping numerator and denominator, misidentifying the place value (confusing 0.4 with 0.04), and weak GCF skills leading to incomplete simplification (stopping at 2/4 instead of 1/2). Diagnostic questions should probe: (a) can the student identify the correct denominator from decimal place count? (b) can they find the GCF of the resulting numerator and denominator? (c) can they verify simplification is complete? Fractions with denominators that are powers of 10 (2, 4, 5, 8, 10, 20, 25, 50, 100, 125, 250, 500, 1000) are the only ones arising from terminating decimals; students should eventually recognise this pattern. Encourage use of factorisation or the Euclidean algorithm for GCF when numerators and denominators are large.

name: Convert a decimal to a percentage and a percentage to a decimal
masteryDefinition: Students can fluently convert between decimal and percentage notation by multiplying or dividing by 100, understanding that percentages represent parts per hundred. They can work in both directions (0.35 ↔ 35%) and apply this skill to real-world contexts without conceptual errors.

misconceptions.label: Decimal point movement confusion
misconceptions.description: Students move the decimal point one place instead of two when converting. For example, converting 0.35 to 35% by moving one decimal place to get 3.5%, or converting 45% to 0.45 by moving one place to get 4.5. This stems from confusing the procedure with other decimal operations.

misconceptions.label: Directional error in percentage-to-decimal conversion
misconceptions.description: Students divide by 10 instead of 100 when converting a percentage to decimal form. For example, 25% becomes 0.25 is correct, but students write 2.5 or 0.025 due to misremembering the operation direction or magnitude.

misconceptions.label: Treating percentage sign as a digit or multiplier
misconceptions.description: Students misinterpret the % symbol as an instruction to do something other than 'divide by 100' or 'multiply by 100'. For example, they might add 100 to a decimal (0.35 + 100), or write 35% as simply 35 without understanding it represents a fraction of 100.

misconceptions.label: Inconsistent handling of decimals in percentages
misconceptions.description: Students can convert simple percentages like 50% or 25% correctly but struggle with decimal percentages like 12.5% or non-terminating conversions. They may incorrectly write 0.125 as 0.1215 or fail to recognise the connection between fractions, decimals, and percentages.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Whole number percentages (50%, 75%, 30%) with familiar decimal equivalents
difficultyDimensions.hard: Decimal percentages (12.5%, 33.3%), percentages greater than 100% (150%), or percentages less than 1% (0.5%)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Direct conversion with no additional context or fractional reasoning required
difficultyDimensions.hard: Converting then comparing, interpreting percentage change, or working backwards from a percentage of a quantity

difficultyDimensions.easy: Decimals with one or two decimal places (0.5, 0.35, 0.08)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Recurring decimals (0.333...), very small decimals (0.005), or decimals with more than two places (0.128)

transferContexts.context: Interpreting discounts and sale prices in shopping
transferContexts.example: A shop offers a 0.2 discount on items. Write this as a percentage. Or: An item is reduced by 15%. What is the decimal multiplier applied to the original price?

transferContexts.context: Calculating and interpreting test scores and grades
transferContexts.example: A student scored 0.85 on a test. Express this as a percentage. Or: A grade of 92% is required for a distinction. What is the decimal equivalent?
The probability of rain is 0.4. Express this as a percentage chance. Or: There is a 35% chance of snow. What is the decimal probability?

generativeContext: N4.6 focuses on the bidirectional conversion between decimal and percentage notation. The core procedure is multiplication or division by 100, rooted in the definition that 'percent' means 'parts per hundred'. When converting decimal to percentage: multiply by 100 and append the % symbol (0.35 × 100 = 35%). When converting percentage to decimal: divide by 100 or remove % and shift the decimal point left two places (45% ÷ 100 = 0.45). Key insight: students must understand that 0.35 and 35% represent the same quantity, just in different notational systems. Common errors include moving the decimal point only once, confusing the direction of operation, or misunderstanding what the % symbol signifies. Misconceptions often arise from weak understanding of place value, decimal multiplication/division by powers of 10, or rote memorisation without conceptual grounding. Students should be able to work fluently in both directions and recognise equivalent representations across fractions, decimals, and percentages. Real-world contexts (discounts, probabilities, test scores) reinforce that percentages are a practical communication tool. Stretch opportunities include non-terminating decimals (0.333... = 33.3%), percentages above 100%, and inverse contexts (e.g., 'if 25% of a number is 10, find the number').

name: Convert a fraction to a percentage (via decimal or equivalence to /100)
masteryDefinition: Student can convert any proper or improper fraction to a percentage using either: (1) converting to a decimal then multiplying by 100, or (2) finding an equivalent fraction with denominator 100. Student understands the relationship between fractions, decimals, and percentages, and can select the most efficient method. Can explain why multiplying a decimal by 100 gives a percentage, and can work backwards from a percentage to express it as a fraction in simplest form.

misconceptions.label: Multiplying numerator and denominator by the same number to reach 100, then reading only the numerator
misconceptions.description: Student correctly identifies that 3/4 × 25/25 = 75/100, but then states the percentage as '25%' or '4%' instead of '75%'. They confuse the multiplier or the denominator with the percentage value. Occurs frequently when the denominator is not a factor of 100.

misconceptions.label: Inverting the fraction before converting
misconceptions.description: Student converts 3/5 by first writing 5/3, then converts to get 166.67% or similar incorrect values. Or converts 1/4 as 4/1 = 400%. This stems from confusion about what the fraction represents or a procedural error when using the decimal method.

misconceptions.label: Forgetting to multiply by 100 when converting from decimal
misconceptions.description: Student correctly converts 3/4 to 0.75 (decimal) but then writes the answer as '0.75%' instead of '75%'. They state the decimal value as the percentage without the final multiplication step. Often accompanied by the belief that decimals and percentages are equivalent.

misconceptions.label: Incorrectly scaling denominators that do not factor 100
misconceptions.description: When converting 2/3, student attempts to find an equivalent fraction with denominator 100 but either rounds (e.g., 66.67/100 written as 67/100 = 67%) or gives up, claiming 'you can't convert 2/3 to a percentage'. Reflects weak understanding of equivalent fractions and the role of 100 as a scaling anchor.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Denominators that are factors of 100 (2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 50)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Denominators that do not factor 100 (3, 6, 7, 8, 9, 11, 12, 13) requiring division or rounding of the decimal

difficultyDimensions.easy: Proper fractions (numerator < denominator) yielding percentages ≤ 100%
difficultyDimensions.hard: Improper fractions (numerator ≥ denominator) or mixed numbers yielding percentages > 100%, requiring students to interpret percentages beyond 100

difficultyDimensions.easy: Direct equivalence to /100 (e.g., 3/4 = 75/100) with no intermediate reasoning required
difficultyDimensions.hard: Decimal method with non-terminating or repeating decimals (e.g., 1/3 = 0.333... = 33.33...%), requiring rounding decisions or exact notation

transferContexts.context: Real-world percentage representation (discounts, test scores, survey data)
transferContexts.example: A shop offers a discount of 3/5 off the original price. Express this discount as a percentage. How much would a customer save on a £40 item?

transferContexts.context: Comparison and ordering of fractions, decimals, and percentages
transferContexts.example: Order these from smallest to largest: 7/8, 0.85, 82%. Convert each to a percentage to compare.

transferContexts.context: Working backwards from percentage to fraction
transferContexts.example: A student scores 85% on a test out of 20 marks. Express 85% as a fraction in simplest form. How many marks did they score?

generativeContext: N4.7 requires fluent conversion between the three representations of rational numbers: fractions, decimals, and percentages. The skill demands procedural competence in two distinct pathways: (1) equivalence method — scaling a fraction so its denominator becomes 100, then reading the numerator as the percentage; (2) decimal method — dividing numerator by denominator to obtain a decimal, then multiplying by 100. Students must understand that a percentage is a statement of 'per 100' and that 100% = 1 = 100/100.
