Misconceptions arise from: (a) confusing which value is the final answer (multiplier vs. numerator vs. denominator); (b) skipping the ×100 step in the decimal method; (c) inverting fractions; (d) struggling when the denominator does not divide 100 cleanly, requiring division of decimals or acceptance of recurring/rounded values. Difficulty scales with denominator 'niceness' (5, 4, 20 are easy; 3, 7, 9 are hard) and whether the fraction is proper or improper. Transfer requires recognizing percentage as a label for proportional reasoning in applied contexts: discounts, marks, survey splits. Remediation should emphasize the conceptual anchor of '/100' and explicit practice comparing the efficiency of both methods across different fractions. Use bar models or area diagrams to reinforce the relationship between the fraction's size, its decimal representation, and its percentage value.

name: Compare and order fractions, decimals and percentages
masteryDefinition: Students can accurately convert between fractions, decimals and percentages, then use these representations to compare and order sets of numbers. They understand that different representations express the same quantity, can identify which is largest or smallest across mixed representations, and can arrange three or more numbers in ascending or descending order correctly. They can justify their ordering by explaining the conversion process or by finding a common representation.

misconceptions.label: Comparing decimals as if they were whole numbers
misconceptions.description: Students treat the decimal part as a whole number when comparing. For example, they believe 0.5 < 0.25 because 5 < 25, ignoring place value. This stems from not recognising that 0.5 = 50/100 and 0.25 = 25/100.

misconceptions.label: Assuming percentages are always out of 100 in the numerator
misconceptions.description: When comparing a fraction like 3/5 with 60%, students struggle because they don't recognise that 3/5 = 60/100. Instead, they may assume the fraction is 'incomplete' or try to compare 3 directly with 60, not understanding that the denominator matters.

misconceptions.label: Incorrectly converting fractions to decimals by dividing numerator by denominator in the wrong order
misconceptions.description: Students divide the denominator by the numerator instead of numerator by denominator. For example, to convert 1/4, they calculate 4 ÷ 1 = 4 instead of 1 ÷ 4 = 0.25. This produces nonsensical results that are not caught because students don't sense-check against the original fraction.

misconceptions.label: Believing that a longer decimal representation means a larger number
misconceptions.description: Students think 0.5 < 0.125 because 0.125 has more digits. They do not recognise that trailing zeros are implicit (0.5 = 0.500) and that the position of digits determines magnitude, not the count of digits.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of representations mixed in one problem
difficultyDimensions.easy: Comparing two numbers in the same representation (e.g. two decimals or two fractions)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Ordering four or more numbers across all three representations (e.g. 3/5, 0.58, 62%, 7/10)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Complexity of fraction and denominator size
difficultyDimensions.easy: Simple fractions with small denominators (1/2, 1/4, 3/4) that convert to terminating decimals and simple percentages
difficultyDimensions.hard: Non-standard fractions with larger denominators (7/8, 5/12) requiring long division or knowledge of less common percentage equivalents

difficultyDimensions.easy: Numbers with clear visual/magnitude separation (0.25, 0.5, 0.75)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Numbers that are very close (0.48, 48%, 12/25) requiring precise conversion and comparison

transferContexts.example: A survey shows 45% of students prefer maths, 9/20 prefer English, and 0.42 prefer science. Which subject is preferred by the fewest students? Rank all three.
A shop offers three deals: 1/3 off, 35% off, or 0.32 off. Which offer saves the customer the most money?
Three solutions have concentrations of 7/10, 72%, and 0.68. Which is most concentrated?

generativeContext: N4.8 requires students to bridge three symbolic systems: fractions (part-whole as ratio), decimals (base-10 positional notation), and percentages (parts per hundred). Mastery demands fluency in conversion in all directions, not just one-way translations. Key insight: students must internalise anchor equivalences (1/2 = 0.5 = 50%, 1/4 = 0.25 = 25%, 3/4 = 0.75 = 75%) and use them as reference points. Common failure mode is proceeding mechanically through conversion without sense-checking: if 3/5 converts to a decimal, it should lie between 0.5 and 1. Ordering problems must include mixed representations (never all three in one representation, as that loses the transfer demand). When generating questions, vary: (1) how many numbers to order (2–5); (2) distribution across representations (avoid 'all decimals'); (3) whether conversions are 'nice' (terminating) or require rounding/recurring decimals; (4) whether answers sit close together (0.47, 48%, 47/100) or far apart. Avoid repeating the same reference fractions; use 1/3, 2/3, 1/5, 2/5, 3/5, 1/6, 5/6, 1/8, 3/8, 5/8, 7/8 to build flexibility. Include misconception traps: place 0.5 and 0.50 in same set; ask students to justify ordering; require written justification of at least one conversion step.

name: Find a percentage of an amount (using non-calculator-friendly methods)
masteryDefinition: Students can find any percentage of a given amount using efficient mental or written methods without a calculator. This includes: finding 10%, 1%, and 5% as building blocks; using multiplicative reasoning to combine these; applying the unitary method (finding 1% then multiplying); and converting percentages to fractions where efficient (e.g. 25% = 1/4, 75% = 3/4). Students demonstrate fluency selecting the most efficient strategy based on the percentage and amount, and can justify their method choice.

misconceptions.label: Percentage interpreted as a simple additive operation
misconceptions.description: Students treat 'find 20% of 50' as 50 + 20 = 70, or subtract the percentage from the amount. This arises from confusing the word 'of' with addition, or misunderstanding percentage as an absolute quantity rather than a proportion. They may also divide by the percentage value directly (50 ÷ 20) without conceptual understanding.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect use of the 10% building block method
misconceptions.description: Students correctly find 10% but then make errors when scaling up or down. For example, to find 30% they may find 10% then add 3 (instead of multiply by 3), or they find 10% correctly but miscount how many tens are in the target percentage. They may also confuse 10% with 1 tenth and incorrectly apply proportional scaling.

misconceptions.label: Misapplication of fraction equivalents to percentages
misconceptions.description: Students know that 50% = 1/2 or 25% = 1/4 but make errors in arithmetic after converting. For example, they correctly identify 25% of £80 as 1/4 of £80, but then divide incorrectly (80 ÷ 4 = 30 instead of 20), or they confuse the conversion and use the wrong fraction (treating 25% as 1/3).

misconceptions.label: Confusion between 'percentage of' and 'percentage increase'
misconceptions.description: Students find a percentage of an amount correctly, but then add it back on as if solving a percentage increase problem. For example, finding 20% of 150, they calculate 30 but then report 150 + 30 = 180 because they conflate 'find 20%' with 'increase by 20%'.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Multiples of 10 (10%, 20%, 30%); percentages with direct fraction equivalents (25%, 50%, 75%)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Non-standard percentages (e.g. 15%, 35%, 17%, 3%); percentages requiring multiple steps in the building-block method

difficultyDimensions.easy: Round whole numbers (100, 50, 200) where division yields whole numbers; amounts that are multiples of 4, 5, or 10
difficultyDimensions.hard: Non-round amounts (e.g. 73, 156, 247); amounts that create fractional intermediate steps (e.g. finding 10% of 35 gives 3.5)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Percentages where 10% or 1% building blocks divide evenly into the target; amounts compatible with fraction methods (e.g. 50% of 48)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Scenarios requiring unitary method or multiple scaling steps; contexts where no single 'natural' method is obvious (e.g. 13% of 54)

transferContexts.context: Real-world discount and sale price contexts
transferContexts.example: A jacket costs £60. It is in a 25% off sale. Calculate the sale price without a calculator. (Requires finding 25% = 1/4 of £60, then subtracting from original; or finding the percentage to pay: 75% of £60.)

transferContexts.context: Proportion and scaling in recipes or measurements
transferContexts.example: A recipe serves 4 people and contains 200g flour. You need to serve 6 people. If you reduce a key ingredient by 20%, how much of that ingredient do you need? (Combines percentage-finding with ratio reasoning.)
A savings account offers 5% annual interest. Calculate the interest earned in the first year on a deposit of £320. (Requires finding 5% of £320 using mental scaling or the unitary method.)

generativeContext: N4.9 focuses on non-calculator mental and written strategies for finding percentages. Students must move beyond rote percent formulas and develop flexible, efficient reasoning. Key strategies include: (1) The 10% building block method: divide by 10, then scale to the target percentage. (2) The 1% unitary method: divide by 100 (or by 10, then by 10 again), then multiply. (3) Fraction equivalence: recognising 50%=1/2, 25%=1/4, 75%=3/4, 20%=1/5, etc., and computing the division instead. (4) Composite methods: combining strategies (e.g. 15% = 10% + 5%). Mastery requires students to: select the most efficient strategy based on the numbers (e.g. use 1/4 for 25% of 84, but 1% method for 17% of 200); perform arithmetic accurately in context; and articulate why one method is better than another for a given problem. Generation should span a range of percentage values (simple multiples, fractions, and non-standard values), vary the starting amounts to control divisibility, and create contexts where strategy choice is meaningful. Avoid repetitive scaffolding; instead, vary the cognitive demand by shifting which operations are 'natural' for the numbers given.

name: Concept of a fraction — shading shapes, bar models, placing on a number line
masteryDefinition: Students can represent fractions accurately using three distinct models: (1) shading the correct proportion of a shape divided into equal parts; (2) identifying or constructing equivalent bar model representations; (3) locating and labelling fractions precisely on a number line between 0 and 1, and beyond. They understand that the denominator describes the number of equal parts and the numerator describes how many parts are selected. They can move fluently between these representations and justify why a shaded region or marked point corresponds to a given fraction.

misconceptions.label: Unequal parts assumption
misconceptions.description: Students shade an arbitrary number of sections without checking that all parts are equal, or accept shading of unequal sections as a valid fraction representation. They may count 3 shaded sections out of 5 sections (regardless of size variation) and call it 3/5, ignoring the equal-parts requirement.

misconceptions.label: Numerator-denominator reversal
misconceptions.description: Students interpret or write the fraction upside-down, treating 3/5 as 5/3, or misidentify which number represents the total parts versus the selected parts. This often emerges when reading bar models where they count shaded sections as the denominator.

misconceptions.label: Number line spacing error
misconceptions.description: Students place fractions on a number line using equal intervals without scaling to the denominator, or assume all fractions fit at the same positions regardless of denominator. For example, placing 1/2, 1/3, and 1/4 at the same point, or spacing 1/5 and 2/5 equally as if they were 1 unit apart.

misconceptions.label: Part-to-part confusion
misconceptions.description: Students interpret a fraction as a ratio or comparison between two parts (e.g., 3 shaded and 2 unshaded means 3/2) rather than a part-to-whole relationship. They may describe shading as 'the ratio of shaded to unshaded' instead of recognising the denominator as the total number of equal parts.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Fractions with small denominators (halves, quarters, fifths) where parts are visually distinct and clearly bounded
difficultyDimensions.hard: Fractions with large denominators (tenths, twelfths, twentieths) where individual parts appear small and are harder to count or shade accurately

difficultyDimensions.easy: Regular shapes (squares, rectangles, circles) divided into congruent parts by parallel or radial lines
difficultyDimensions.hard: Irregular or composite shapes, or shapes divided by non-obvious lines; polygons divided into non-congruent regions requiring careful visual counting

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single static shaded shape in a familiar model (circle or rectangle); explicit visual demarcation of parts
difficultyDimensions.hard: Transitioning between shading, bar models, and number lines; placing improper fractions or mixed numbers; interpreting unlabelled number line intervals

transferContexts.example: A chocolate bar divided into 8 equal pieces; if 3 pieces are eaten, what fraction remains? Represent this on a bar model and locate the remaining fraction on a number line from 0 to 1.

transferContexts.context: Comparing and ordering fractions using visual models
transferContexts.example: Shade 2/5 of one rectangle and 3/7 of another (both same size). Use number lines to determine which is larger and explain why equal parts are needed for fair comparison.
A rope is 10 m long. Mark 3/5 of its length on a diagram and place it on a number line scaled to show 0 m and 10 m. Explain where the mark sits relative to the halfway point.

generativeContext: N5.1 introduces fractions as a part-to-whole relationship. Mastery requires secure understanding that fractions are constructed from equal-sized parts: the denominator fixes the number of equal divisions, and the numerator counts how many of those equal parts are selected. Three modalities are essential: (1) Shading: students must verify equal divisions before shading; common errors include shading arbitrary regions or unequal sections. (2) Bar models: linear strips divided into n equal cells, where m cells are shaded; errors often involve reading the model backwards (confusing filled vs. unfilled) or miscounting due to spacing. (3) Number lines: scaled lines marked 0 and 1, with internal tick marks for equal-part boundaries; errors occur when intervals are unequal, when students assume all fractions occupy the same position, or when they lack fluency in translating between a denominator and the required number of equal intervals. The concept is foundational for equivalence, comparison, and arithmetic operations later. Strengthen by anchoring fractions to repeated measurement (folding, cutting, measuring) and by forcing explicit verification of equal parts before accepting any representation. Address reversal errors through labelling protocols: 'total parts' and 'selected parts' language. For number lines, use premade grids before blank lines, and require students to mark all interval boundaries before locating a fraction.

name: Equivalent fractions: generating, showing equivalence, finding missing numbers
masteryDefinition: Students can generate equivalent fractions by multiplying or dividing both numerator and denominator by the same non-zero number. They can identify when two fractions are equivalent by cross-multiplication, by converting to a common denominator, or by reducing to simplest form. They can find missing numerators or denominators in equivalent fraction pairs and justify their reasoning using the multiplicative relationship between numerator and denominator.

misconceptions.label: Adding the same number to numerator and denominator
misconceptions.description: Students incorrectly believe that adding the same number to both the numerator and denominator creates an equivalent fraction. For example, they think 1/2 + 1 = 2/3 or that 2/4 becomes 3/5 by adding 1 to both parts. This stems from confusion between additive and multiplicative relationships.

misconceptions.label: Operating on only the numerator or only the denominator
misconceptions.description: Students apply the same operation to only one part of the fraction, not both. For example, when finding the equivalent of 2/3 with denominator 12, they multiply only the denominator by 4 to get 2/12, forgetting to multiply the numerator. Or they multiply only the numerator, producing 8/3.

misconceptions.label: Misidentifying equivalent fractions by comparing individual parts
misconceptions.description: Students believe two fractions are equivalent if their numerators are equal OR their denominators are equal, rather than checking the ratio. For example, they think 3/5 and 3/7 are equivalent because the numerators are both 3, or that 2/5 and 4/5 are equivalent because the denominators are both 5.

misconceptions.label: Confusing simplification with finding equivalent fractions
misconceptions.description: Students reduce a fraction to its simplest form when asked to find an equivalent fraction with a specific denominator, without checking whether the target denominator is actually a multiple of the original. For example, given 6/8 and asked for an equivalent with denominator 5, they simplify 6/8 = 3/4 and then incorrectly claim no equivalent exists, or they force an answer like 3.75/5.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Scaling by small whole numbers (×2, ×3, ×5) where numerator and denominator are single digits
difficultyDimensions.hard: Scaling by larger multipliers (×7, ×10, ×12) or by non-obvious multipliers (×11); finding missing numerator/denominator when the multiplier is not immediately obvious

difficultyDimensions.easy: Generating equivalent fractions in the forward direction (given fraction and multiplier, find new fraction)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Working backwards to find missing numbers (given two fractions, identify the multiplier; given starting fraction and target denominator, find the numerator)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number size and starting fraction complexity
difficultyDimensions.easy: Fractions with numerators and denominators ≤ 10, starting from unit fractions like 1/2 or 1/4
difficultyDimensions.hard: Fractions with larger numbers (e.g., 7/13, 12/18); fractions already in non-simplest form; fractions close to 1 (e.g., 9/10)

transferContexts.example: Convert 3/4, 5/6, and 7/12 to a common denominator in order to rank them from smallest to largest.

transferContexts.context: Adding and subtracting fractions with different denominators
transferContexts.example: Calculate 2/5 + 3/8 by first finding equivalent fractions with a common denominator.

transferContexts.context: Scaling quantities in recipes and real-world ratios
transferContexts.example: A recipe uses 2 cups of flour and 3 cups of sugar. If you want to double the recipe, write equivalent fractions to show the new proportions: 4/6 compared to the original 2/3.

generativeContext: N5.2 focuses on the multiplicative structure underlying fraction equivalence. The core principle is that multiplying (or dividing) both numerator and denominator by the same non-zero number preserves the value of the fraction because the ratio remains unchanged. This is fundamentally different from additive operations. Students must develop fluency in three directions: (1) generating new equivalent fractions from a given fraction and a scale factor; (2) finding the scale factor when given two fractions and determining whether they are equivalent; (3) finding missing numbers in equivalent fraction pairs by identifying and applying the multiplier. Emphasis should be placed on the invariance of the ratio and on checking work by cross-multiplication or by reducing both fractions to lowest terms. Common errors include confusing this multiplicative relationship with addition, applying the operation to only one part of the fraction, and misinterpreting equivalence by focusing on individual numerators or denominators rather than their ratio. Scaffold using visual representations (area models, number lines) alongside symbolic notation. Use concrete numbers before introducing variables or missing-number problems. Pay attention to fractions that are already non-unit or non-simplest forms, as these expose whether students understand the ratio or merely follow a procedure.

name: Place fractions on a number line; compare and order fractions
masteryDefinition: Students can accurately place fractions with different denominators on a number line between 0 and 1 (and beyond), compare two or more fractions using reasoning about their size relative to benchmarks (0, 1/2, 1), and order sets of fractions in ascending or descending order. They understand that fractions with the same numerator but different denominators are ordered oppositely to their denominators, and can justify comparisons using equivalent fractions or visual models.

misconceptions.label: Comparing numerators only, ignoring denominators
misconceptions.description: Students claim that 3/5 < 2/5 because 3 > 2, or that 1/2 > 1/4 based only on the numerator without considering the size of the equal parts. They treat fractions like whole numbers where only the numerator determines size.

misconceptions.label: Inverting the relationship when denominators differ with same numerator
misconceptions.description: When comparing fractions with the same numerator such as 1/3 and 1/5, students incorrectly state that 1/5 > 1/3 because 5 > 3, reversing the correct relationship. They confuse 'larger denominator' with 'larger fraction'.

misconceptions.label: Placing fractions on a number line using denominator as the interval divisor without adjusting for scale
misconceptions.description: When placing 2/3 on a number line, students divide the 0–1 interval into 3 equal parts and mark the second division, but if the number line spans 0–2 or has an unlabeled scale, they fail to rescale their thinking. They treat the visual interval rigidly rather than proportionally.

misconceptions.label: Assuming fractions with the same denominator are equal if numerators look similar in magnitude
misconceptions.description: When ordering 4/7, 5/7, and 3/7, students group 4/7 and 5/7 together as 'similar size' without recognising the precise one-seventh difference, or lose track of which is larger when numerators are close (e.g., confusing 6/8 and 7/8).

difficultyDimensions.easy: Fractions with denominators 2, 4, or matching denominators (e.g., 1/4, 2/4, 3/4)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Fractions with unrelated denominators (e.g., 3/7, 2/5, 4/9) requiring common denominators or benchmark reasoning

difficultyDimensions.easy: Number lines explicitly marked 0, 1/2, 1 with visual equal divisions clearly shown
difficultyDimensions.hard: Number lines spanning 0–2 or 0–3 with minimal labels, requiring proportional rescaling and multiple iterations across unit intervals

difficultyDimensions.easy: Comparing or ordering two fractions, both less than 1
difficultyDimensions.hard: Ordering 4+ fractions including improper fractions (e.g., 5/4), fractions close in value (e.g., 7/8 and 8/9), and spanning multiple unit intervals

transferContexts.example: A recipe uses 2/3 cup flour, 3/4 cup sugar, and 1/2 cup butter. Which ingredient uses the least? Place these on a number line to order them, then scale the recipe for double the quantity.
Three tasks take 3/5 hour, 1/2 hour, and 4/5 hour respectively. Order these durations from shortest to longest and place them on a timeline marked in hours.

transferContexts.context: Progress and distance on a journey
transferContexts.example: A walker has completed 2/5 of a trail, another has completed 3/8, and a third has completed 1/3. Who is furthest along? Place their positions on a number line from start (0) to finish (1).

generativeContext: N5.3 develops the critical skill of reasoning about fraction magnitude without relying on procedural equivalence. Students must internalize that fractions measure parts of a whole, and that the denominator determines the size of each part—larger denominators create smaller parts. Number line work is foundational: placing fractions requires students to mentally divide an interval into equal parts and locate the appropriate position, building geometric and proportional reasoning. Comparison and ordering require flexible switching between three strategies: (1) using visual models or number lines to 'see' relative sizes; (2) benchmarking to 0, 1/2, or 1 (e.g., 3/7 is less than 1/2 because 3/7 < 3.5/7); and (3) converting to common denominators or decimals when benchmarking is ambiguous. Special attention should be paid to the inversion of order when comparing fractions with the same numerator—this is a conceptual threshold where students often revert to treating fractions as pairs of whole numbers. Improper fractions and fractions greater than 1 extend the number line beyond unit intervals and require understanding that 5/4 = 1 + 1/4, reinforcing the additive structure of fractions. Generative questions should include explicit number line diagrams, mixed contexts (comparison and placement combined), and deliberate inclusion of distractors (fractions with similar numerators or denominators to invite reversal errors).

name: Simplify fractions
masteryDefinition: Students can reduce a fraction to its simplest form (lowest terms) by identifying and dividing both numerator and denominator by their greatest common divisor (GCD). They recognise when a fraction is already in simplest form and understand that simplification does not change the value of the fraction. They can simplify fractions arising from real contexts (e.g. 15/20 of a class, 8/12 of a pizza) and verify their answer by checking that the GCD of the resulting numerator and denominator is 1.

misconceptions.label: Subtracting instead of dividing by GCD
misconceptions.description: Students subtract the same number from both numerator and denominator instead of dividing. For example, simplifying 8/12 by subtracting 4 to get 4/8, rather than dividing both by 4 to get 2/3. This reflects confusion between operations and often stems from thinking about 'removing' equal parts.

misconceptions.label: Dividing only the numerator or only the denominator
misconceptions.description: Students divide only the top or only the bottom by a factor, not both. For example, simplifying 10/15 to 5/15 or 10/5, rather than 2/3. This shows incomplete understanding that both parts of the fraction must be treated equally.

misconceptions.label: Cancelling non-common factors
misconceptions.description: Students cancel digits that appear in both numerator and denominator but are not true common factors. For example, simplifying 16/64 to 1/4 by 'cancelling the 6s', or 25/52 to 2/2 by cancelling the 2s. This reflects a procedural error where students confuse digit-level cancellation with factor division.

misconceptions.label: Believing a fraction cannot be simplified further when GCD is not obvious
misconceptions.description: Students incorrectly state that fractions like 12/18 or 24/36 are already in simplest form because they don't immediately spot the common factor. They may check only obvious factors (2, 3, 5) and miss larger GCDs. This reflects incomplete factor-finding strategies rather than a misconception about the process itself.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Both numerator and denominator are single-digit numbers (e.g. 2/4, 3/6)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Both numerator and denominator are two-digit or larger numbers with less obvious common factors (e.g. 36/84, 48/120)

difficultyDimensions.easy: GCD is a small prime (2 or 5) and factors are immediately visible (e.g. 6/10, 8/12)
difficultyDimensions.hard: GCD is composite or requires systematic checking (e.g. 18/24 with GCD 6, or 15/35 with GCD 5 requiring careful inspection)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Abstract symbolic fractions presented in standard form with no contextual meaning
difficultyDimensions.hard: Fractions embedded in word problems or diagrams (e.g. 'simplify the fraction of pupils who...' or visual part-whole models) requiring extraction before simplification

transferContexts.context: Simplifying fractions arising from ratio problems
transferContexts.example: A recipe uses 12 cups of flour and 18 cups of sugar. Simplify the ratio flour : sugar as a single fraction in lowest terms.
To decide which is larger, 9/15 or 4/10, first simplify both fractions then compare them.
In a survey, 20 out of 50 students preferred tea. Express this as a simplified fraction.

generativeContext: Simplifying fractions is a procedural skill rooted in understanding multiplicative equivalence and the properties of division. The core mechanism is: identify the greatest common divisor (GCD) of numerator and denominator, then divide both by this number. The result is a fraction with the same value but expressed in simplest form (coprime numerator and denominator). Key prerequisites include: confident multiplication and division facts, ability to list factors or use systematic methods (e.g. Euclidean algorithm or prime factorisation), and understanding that multiplying/dividing by 1 (in the form n/n) preserves value. Common procedural errors cluster around: (1) confusing division with subtraction; (2) applying the operation asymmetrically; (3) cancelling at the digit level rather than factor level; (4) incomplete factor-finding leading to premature stopping. Difficulty scales with the size of numbers involved and the transparency of the GCD. In real-world transfer, students encounter fractions that need simplification in ratio/proportion problems, probability contexts, measurement, and recipe scaling. The skill is foundational for later work with algebraic fractions, where the same GCD process applies to polynomial expressions. When generating problems, vary GCD size, numerator/denominator magnitude, and whether the fraction is embedded in a narrative context or presented abstractly. Always include verification tasks where students confirm their answer by checking GCD(new numerator, new denominator) = 1.

name: Express one quantity as a fraction of another
masteryDefinition: Students can identify and express one quantity as a fraction of another in any form (proper, improper, or mixed), simplify the resulting fraction to lowest terms, and justify their answer by referencing the part–whole relationship. They recognise that the order of quantities matters and can work flexibly across different units (within the same measurement system) and different contexts (discrete objects, length, mass, capacity).

misconceptions.label: Inverts numerator and denominator
misconceptions.description: Student writes the fraction upside-down, placing the 'whole' as the numerator and the 'part' as the denominator. For example, when asked 'express 3 as a fraction of 12', writes 3/12 instead of recognising this as correct, or when asked 'express 12 as a fraction of 3', writes 3/12 instead of 12/3. Stems from confusion about which quantity is the part and which is the whole.

misconceptions.label: Subtracts instead of fractions
misconceptions.description: Student treats 'as a fraction of' as a subtraction problem. For example, when asked 'express 4 as a fraction of 10', calculates 10 − 4 = 6 and writes 6 or 6/10, rather than 4/10. Arises from misinterpreting the language as 'what is left' or 'the difference'.

misconceptions.label: Fails to simplify or over-simplifies
misconceptions.description: Student either leaves the fraction unsimplified when it can be reduced (e.g. writes 4/10 instead of 2/5), or cancels incorrectly by subtracting common digits rather than dividing by the GCD (e.g. simplifies 4/10 as 4/1 by 'removing the 0'). Related to weak understanding of equivalent fractions and the definition of simplification.

misconceptions.label: Confuses units or forgets to convert
misconceptions.description: When quantities are given in different units, student writes the fraction without converting to the same unit first. For example, 'express 30 cm as a fraction of 2 m' is answered as 30/2 instead of 30/200. Indicates poor grasp of measurement relationships and assumption that numerical values alone suffice.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Proper fractions where the part is visibly smaller than the whole (e.g. express 3 out of 10 objects)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Improper fractions or situations where the part exceeds the whole (e.g. express 15 out of 10, or 18 months as a fraction of 1 year); requires comfort with fractions > 1

difficultyDimensions.easy: Fractions that do not simplify or simplify obviously to unit or simple fractions (e.g. 5/10 = 1/2)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Fractions requiring multi-step simplification or GCD discovery with larger or coprime numbers (e.g. express 24 out of 56; GCD = 8 → 3/7)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Same units given or unit-free discrete objects (e.g. 'express 7 marbles out of 20 marbles')
difficultyDimensions.hard: Different but related units requiring conversion (e.g. 'express 45 minutes as a fraction of 2 hours'; 'express 250 g as a fraction of 2 kg')

transferContexts.example: In an experiment, 12 out of 50 coin flips landed heads. Express the number of heads as a fraction of the total number of flips. Simplify your answer.
A rectangle has length 8 cm and width 5 cm. Express the width as a fraction of the perimeter.
A student saves £45 out of a £180 monthly allowance. Express the saved amount as a fraction of the allowance in simplest form.

generativeContext: N5.5 requires students to construct a fraction by identifying a part quantity and a whole quantity, then expressing the part as a numerator over the whole as a denominator. The core mental action is: given two quantities A and B (where A is the 'part' or 'subset' and B is the 'whole' or 'reference'), write A/B and reduce to simplest form. Language is critical—'express X as a fraction of Y' means X goes in the numerator, Y in the denominator; reversing this is a frequent error. Simplification must follow (dividing both by their GCD). In KS3, extend to improper fractions (where A ≥ B, so the fraction ≥ 1) and mixed numbers, and ensure unit consistency (convert units before forming the fraction). Common contexts: discrete objects (e.g. 3 red out of 12 total), length/mass/capacity (often with unit conversion), probability (successful outcomes out of total trials), and ratio-like problems (part of a budget, portion of a time interval). Misconception n5-5-m1 (inverted numerator–denominator) is very frequent and often persists; diagnose by asking 'which number is the part, which is the whole?' Misconception n5-5-m2 (subtraction) signals misinterpretation of language and requires explicit re-reading. Misconception n5-5-m3 (simplification failure) surfaces when students have weak GCD fluency or believe all fractions must simplify. Misconception n5-5-m4 (unit confusion) emerges with measurement questions and requires pre-checking unit conversion. Vary numerator–denominator size relationship (proper vs improper), GCD complexity, and unit pairs to probe depth.

name: Convert between mixed numbers and improper fractions; write integers as fractions
masteryDefinition: Students can fluently convert between mixed numbers and improper fractions in both directions, understand the structural relationship between the two forms, and express integers as fractions with any specified denominator. They can justify their conversions using diagrams or repeated addition, and recognise when each form is most useful.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect application of division algorithm
misconceptions.description: When converting improper fractions to mixed numbers, students divide numerator by denominator but write the quotient as the numerator of the fraction part rather than the whole number part. For example, converting 11/3 as 3 remainder 2, then writing it as 2 3/3 instead of 3 2/3.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring the denominator when converting mixed to improper
misconceptions.description: Students multiply the whole number by the numerator instead of the denominator, or add incorrectly. For example, converting 2 3/5 as (2 × 3) + 3 = 9/5 instead of (2 × 5 + 3)/5 = 13/5.

misconceptions.label: Treating integers as already fractions without adjustment
misconceptions.description: When asked to write an integer as a fraction with a specific denominator, students write the integer as the numerator without multiplying. For example, writing 4 as 4/7 instead of 28/7.

misconceptions.label: Confusing the position of whole number and remainder
misconceptions.description: When converting improper to mixed, students correctly perform the division but write the remainder in the wrong position. For example, 14/5 becomes 2 4/5 initially, but then errors occur when they reverse parts or misplace the remainder as a whole number.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single-digit whole numbers and denominators (e.g., 7/2, 1 3/4)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Double-digit or larger numerators and denominators (e.g., 47/8, 5 12/13)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Familiar denominators like 2, 4, 5, 10 that relate to common fractions
difficultyDimensions.hard: Prime or unfamiliar denominators like 7, 11, 13 where visual models are less intuitive

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single-direction conversions in sequence (all mixed to improper, then all improper to mixed)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Mixed-direction conversions requiring flexible movement between forms and writing integers with multiple different denominators

transferContexts.example: To add 2 3/4 + 1 1/2, convert both to improper fractions first. What is 2 3/4 as an improper fraction?
A recipe requires 5 cups of flour. If you want to make 1/3 of the recipe, express 5 as a fraction with denominator 3 to calculate the amount needed.
A length is 3 2/5 metres. Convert this to an improper fraction to compare with another measurement given as 17/5 metres.

generativeContext: N5.6 builds on secure understanding of fractional parts and the relationship between numerator, denominator, and unit. Students must grasp that a mixed number is a compact notation for a whole plus a fractional part, and that improper fractions represent the same quantity as a single fraction. The key procedural knowledge is: (1) to convert mixed to improper, multiply the whole number by the denominator, add the numerator, and write over the same denominator; (2) to convert improper to mixed, divide the numerator by the denominator to find quotient (whole) and remainder (new numerator); (3) to express an integer as a fraction, multiply the integer by the desired denominator and place the result as the numerator. Misconceptions often stem from procedural slips (wrong operation order, misplaced numbers) or incomplete understanding of place-value in fractions. Visual models (area diagrams, number lines, bar models) are critical scaffolds. Distinguish carefully between the quotient (whole number part) and remainder (numerator) during division, and reinforce the multiplicative relationship between whole number, denominator, and final numerator. Students should develop the habit of checking conversions by reversing the operation or verating the value is sensible.

name: Add fractions (same then different denominators)
masteryDefinition: Students can add two or more fractions with the same denominator by adding numerators and keeping the denominator unchanged. Students can add fractions with different denominators by finding a common denominator (using multiples or LCM), converting both fractions, then adding numerators. Students can simplify the result to lowest terms and convert improper fractions to mixed numbers where appropriate.

misconceptions.label: Adding both numerator and denominator
misconceptions.description: When adding fractions, students add both the numerators and denominators together. For example, 1/3 + 1/4 = 2/7 instead of finding a common denominator first. This stems from treating the fraction as two separate numbers rather than a single quantity.

misconceptions.label: Keeping original denominators when converting to common denominator
misconceptions.description: Students find a common denominator but fail to adjust the numerators proportionally. For example, when converting 1/3 + 1/4 to tenths (incorrect choice), they write 1/10 + 1/10 instead of recognising that the numerator must also scale by the multiplier used on the denominator.

misconceptions.label: Using sum of denominators as common denominator without converting numerators
misconceptions.description: Students assume that 1/2 + 1/3 can be solved by using 5 (2+3) as the denominator, writing 1/5 + 1/5 = 2/5 or 1/2 + 1/3 = 2/5, without scaling the original numerators to match the new denominator.

misconceptions.label: Not simplifying or recognising improper fractions in final answer
misconceptions.description: Students obtain correct intermediate results but leave answers as unsimplified fractions (e.g., 6/9 instead of 2/3) or fail to convert improper fractions to mixed numbers (e.g., 7/4 instead of 1¾), misunderstanding that simplification is part of the complete answer.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Fractions with the same denominator (e.g., 2/5 + 1/5)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Fractions with unrelated denominators requiring LCM (e.g., 3/7 + 2/11)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Small denominators where common denominator is obvious (e.g., 1/2 + 1/4 = 2/4 + 1/4)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Larger denominators where LCM is not immediately obvious and numerators are larger (e.g., 7/12 + 5/18)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Addition resulting in proper fractions in simplest form (e.g., 1/3 + 1/6 = 1/2)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Addition resulting in improper fractions requiring conversion to mixed numbers and simplification (e.g., 5/6 + 7/9 = 19/18 = 1¹/₁₈)

transferContexts.example: A recipe requires 2/3 cup of flour and 1/4 cup of sugar. How much of these two ingredients are needed in total?

transferContexts.context: Time and distance problems combining fractional quantities
transferContexts.example: A runner completes 3/8 of a lap in the morning and 5/12 of a lap in the afternoon. What fraction of a lap has been completed in total?

transferContexts.context: Fraction of a whole in division of resources or areas
transferContexts.example: A garden is 1/3 planted with vegetables and 2/5 planted with flowers. What fraction of the garden is planted with either vegetables or flowers?

generativeContext: N5.7 teaches systematic addition of fractions, moving from the concrete case (same denominator) to the abstract case (different denominators). The core conceptual shift is that fractions represent quantities, not separate numerator and denominator integers. When denominators differ, students must use proportional reasoning to convert fractions to equivalent forms with a common unit (common denominator) before combining. The most reliable common denominator strategy for KS3 is the product of denominators (always valid, though not always minimal) or finding the LCM (more efficient). After addition, students must recognize that results should be simplified (by dividing by the GCD of numerator and denominator) and improper fractions converted to mixed numbers. Key errors cluster around: (1) adding numerators and denominators as separate operations, (2) forgetting to scale numerators when changing denominators, (3) using heuristics like "sum of denominators" without understanding why, (4) leaving answers unsimplified. Scaffold using area models and linear diagrams to show that the denominator represents the size of the unit part being counted. Use colour-coding to track numerator scaling during conversion. Difficulty increases with denominator size, the need to find non-obvious LCMs, and when results require both simplification and mixed-number conversion.

name: Subtract fractions (same then different denominators)
masteryDefinition: Students can reliably subtract fractions with the same denominator by subtracting numerators and keeping the denominator unchanged. They can also subtract fractions with different denominators by finding a common denominator (including the lowest common denominator), converting both fractions, then subtracting. They understand when to simplify the result and can verify their answer by adding the result to the subtrahend to recover the minuend.

misconceptions.label: Subtracting both numerator and denominator
misconceptions.description: Student subtracts denominators as well as numerators, e.g. 5/7 - 2/3 = 3/4. This reflects a failure to understand that the denominator represents the size of the unit being counted, not a quantity to be operated on.

misconceptions.label: Using wrong common denominator or not converting both fractions
misconceptions.description: When subtracting fractions with different denominators, student finds a common denominator (often not the lowest) but then fails to convert one or both fractions before subtracting, or uses a common denominator but applies it inconsistently. For example: 3/4 - 1/6, student identifies 12 as common denominator but only converts 3/4 to 9/12, then calculates 9/12 - 1/6.

misconceptions.label: Forgetting to simplify or simplifying incorrectly
misconceptions.description: Student correctly performs the subtraction but fails to reduce the answer to simplest form, or attempts to simplify by subtracting from numerator and denominator equally (e.g. 6/8 becomes 5/7 instead of 3/4).

misconceptions.label: Confusing the order of subtraction
misconceptions.description: When presented with a problem like 'subtract 3/5 from 7/8', student reverses the order and calculates 3/5 - 7/8 instead, or struggles with 'from' language and positions the larger fraction second regardless of phrasing.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Same denominator (e.g. 5/7 - 2/7)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Denominators with no common factors requiring LCM calculation (e.g. 5/7 - 2/9)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Result is a proper fraction in simplest form (e.g. 4/5 - 1/5 = 3/5)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Result requires simplification or is an improper fraction (e.g. 7/8 - 3/12 = 9/24 → 3/8)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Small whole numbers in numerators and denominators (e.g. 2/3 - 1/4)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Larger numbers requiring fluent LCM and multiplication (e.g. 11/15 - 7/12)

transferContexts.example: A recipe calls for 3/4 cup of flour. You have already added 1/3 cup. How much more flour do you need to add?
A piece of wood is 5/6 metres long. You cut off 1/4 metre. What length remains?
A film is 7/8 hours long. You have watched 1/3 of it. What fraction of the film remains unwatched?

generativeContext: N5.8 requires students to extend same-denominator subtraction (where numerators subtract directly) to different denominators using equivalence. The core mechanism is: identify a common denominator, convert both fractions to that denominator (multiplying numerator and denominator by the same scaling factor for each), then subtract numerators. The denominator remains unchanged after subtraction. Emphasise that denominators represent the 'unit size' and cannot be subtracted like quantities. Common denominator is not unique but LCM minimises complexity. After subtraction, check whether the result can be simplified by finding the GCD of numerator and denominator. Contextualise with measurement (removing part of a length), sharing (removing a portion from a quantity), and time. Key misconceptions: treating denominators as operands (n5-8-m1); inconsistent or incomplete conversion when finding common denominator (n5-8-m2); omitting or mishandling simplification (n5-8-m3); reversing minuend and subtrahend, especially with verbal phrasing (n5-8-m4). Vary numerators, denominators, and degree of simplification required. Link explicitly to addition of fractions (N5.7) to reinforce mechanism transfer.

name: Add fractions with mixed numbers (same then different denominators)
masteryDefinition: Students can fluently add mixed numbers by converting them to improper fractions, finding common denominators when needed, adding the numerators, and expressing the result as a mixed number or improper fraction as appropriate. They understand the structural equivalence between mixed and improper forms and can justify their method.

misconceptions.label: Adding whole parts and fractional parts separately without conversion
misconceptions.description: Student adds the whole numbers together and the fractions together separately, treating them as independent operations. For example, 2¾ + 1⅖ becomes (2+1) + (¾+⅖) = 3 + 11/20 = 3 11/20, which happens to work, but fails when the fractional parts sum to ≥1. Leads to errors like 3⅞ + 2⅔ = 5 + 11/24 instead of converting to improper fractions first.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring the need for common denominators before adding fractions
misconceptions.description: Student adds numerators and denominators directly without finding a common denominator. For example, ⅗ + ¼ becomes 8/9 instead of 12/20 + 5/20 = 17/20. When applied to mixed numbers like 1⅗ + 2¼, student may write 3 8/9 or simply add across: 3 8/9.

misconceptions.label: Incorrectly converting mixed numbers to improper fractions
misconceptions.description: Student makes arithmetic errors when converting, such as multiplying the whole number by the numerator instead of the denominator, or forgetting to add the numerator to the product. For example, 2⅗ becomes 2×5+3 = 13 (correct) but is written as 2×3+5 = 11, or the denominator is dropped. This cascades into incorrect final answers.

misconceptions.label: Failing to simplify or convert improper fractions back to mixed numbers
misconceptions.description: Student correctly adds improper fractions but leaves the answer as an improper fraction when a mixed number is more appropriate, or fails to simplify when both fractions share a common factor. For example, answering 25/8 instead of 3⅛, or leaving 14/28 instead of simplifying to ½.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Denominators are the same or one is a multiple of the other (e.g., 1⅔ + 2⅙, where 6 is a multiple of 3)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Denominators are coprime or require finding LCM through prime factorisation (e.g., 2⅜ + 3 5/12, or 1 2/5 + 2 3/7)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Fractional parts sum to less than 1 (e.g., ⅓ + ¼ = 7/12)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Fractional parts sum to 1 or more, requiring regrouping and simplification (e.g., ⅝ + ⅞ = 1½, which affects the whole number)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Mixed numbers with small whole parts (e.g., 1⅔ + 2¾) or one addend is a pure fraction
difficultyDimensions.hard: Larger whole parts or all addends are mixed numbers with different fractional denominators (e.g., 12 7/9 + 8 5/12)

transferContexts.context: Measurement and recipe scaling in cooking or construction
transferContexts.example: A recipe calls for 2¾ cups of flour and 1⅔ cups of sugar. How much flour and sugar combined are needed? Calculate 2¾ + 1⅔.

transferContexts.context: Distance and journey problems combining legs of different unit fractions
transferContexts.example: On a walk, Jamie travels 1½ miles in the morning and 2⅜ miles in the afternoon. What is the total distance? Calculate 1½ + 2⅜.

transferContexts.context: Combining repeated fractions in algebra contexts and sequences
transferContexts.example: A plant grows 1⅖ cm in week 1 and 2 3/10 cm in week 2. What is the total growth? Calculate 1⅖ + 2 3/10, then extend to multiple weeks.

generativeContext: N5.9 builds on students' fluency with adding fractions with common denominators (N5.7) and their understanding of mixed number notation (N5.8). The core skill is converting mixed numbers to improper fractions, finding common denominators, adding numerators while preserving the denominator, and converting back to mixed form if needed. Emphasise that mixed numbers are shorthand for (whole number + proper fraction); the conversion is reversible: a⅗ = (a×5+3)/5. Common denominators must be found by identifying multiples; LCM methods become important when denominators share few factors. The skill requires secure arithmetic (multiplication, addition) and conceptual understanding of fraction equivalence. When teaching, use diagrams (bar models, number lines) alongside procedural steps. Highlight that adding fractions requires a common unit (denominator); without this, addition is meaningless. Misconception n5-9-m1 arises from students' intuition that mixed numbers are two separate entities; explicitly model why this fails when fractional parts sum to ≥1. Misconception n5-9-m2 reflects a procedural error without conceptual grounding; use visual models to show why denominators cannot be added. Misconception n5-9-m3 arises from weak conversion procedures; scaffold with area models or repeated multiplication tables. Misconception n5-9-m4 reflects incomplete communication of answers; reinforce that improper fractions and mixed numbers represent the same value but mixed form is often preferred for readability. Differentiation: start with same denominators and small whole parts; progress to different denominators where one divides the other; then coprime denominators. Use concrete manipulatives (fraction strips, Cuisenaire rods) before symbolic work.

name: Subtract fractions with mixed numbers (same then different denominators)
masteryDefinition: Students can subtract mixed numbers by converting to improper fractions, finding a common denominator when needed, performing the subtraction, and simplifying the result. They understand when to convert mixed numbers, how to handle borrowing when the fractional part of the minuend is smaller than the subtrahend, and can work fluently with both same and different denominators.

misconceptions.label: Subtracting whole and fractional parts separately without considering borrowing
misconceptions.description: Students subtract the whole numbers and fractions independently, e.g. 3¼ − 1¾ = (3−1)+(¼−¾) = 2−½ = 1½, instead of recognising they need to borrow 1 from the whole number to make the subtraction work. They may also write negative fractions or incorrectly flip numerators and denominators.

misconceptions.label: Forgetting to find a common denominator before subtracting
misconceptions.description: When denominators differ, students subtract numerators and denominators directly, e.g. 2⅓ − 1⅕ = 1(3−5)/(3−5) = 1(−2)/(−2), or they only convert one fraction and leave the other unchanged. They do not recognise that a common denominator is essential for subtraction.

misconceptions.label: Converting mixed numbers to improper fractions incorrectly
misconceptions.description: Students miscalculate the numerator when converting, e.g. 2¾ is written as 9/4 (multiplying only part of the calculation) or 2¾ = 6/4, forgetting to add the fractional part. Some multiply the denominator by the fractional part instead of the whole number.

misconceptions.label: Not simplifying the final answer or simplifying incorrectly
misconceptions.description: Students leave answers in unsimplified form, e.g. 6/8 instead of ¾, or incorrectly 'simplify' by subtracting the same amount from numerator and denominator, e.g. 6/8 = 5/7. Some divide only the numerator or only the denominator by the GCF.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Same denominators (e.g. 3⅖ − 1⅕)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Different denominators with no common factor (e.g. 4⅖ − 2⅞) or larger LCM values (e.g. 5⁷⁄₁₂ − 2⁵⁄₈)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Fractional part of minuend larger than subtrahend (e.g. 3¾ − 1¼)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Fractional part of minuend smaller than subtrahend, requiring borrowing (e.g. 3¼ − 1¾)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Result is a whole number or simple fraction (e.g. 4½ − 2½ = 2)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Result is a mixed number requiring simplification (e.g. 5⁷⁄₁₂ − 3⁵⁄₆ = 1⁹⁄₁₂ → 1¾) or numerator and denominator share a GCF > 1

transferContexts.example: A recipe requires 2¾ cups of flour, but you only have 1⅜ cups. How much more flour do you need?
A piece of ribbon is 5⅖ metres long. You cut off a piece that is 2⁷⁄₁₀ metres long. How much ribbon remains?

transferContexts.context: Time intervals (hours and fractions of hours)
transferContexts.example: A film is 2⅖ hours long. You have watched 1⅘ hours. How much of the film is left?

generativeContext: This skill requires students to subtract mixed numbers fluently, bridging the gap between subtracting like fractions and managing the complexity of different denominators and the whole number components. The core challenge is that subtraction of mixed numbers often demands regrouping (borrowing) when the fractional part of the first number is smaller than the fractional part of the second number—a step that mirrors borrowing in whole number subtraction but operates on fraction equivalents. Students must convert mixed numbers to improper fractions, identify or construct a common denominator, perform the subtraction on equivalent fractions, and reduce to simplest form. Diagnostic questions should probe: (1) whether students spontaneously convert to improper fractions or attempt part-wise subtraction; (2) whether they recognise when borrowing is necessary and execute it correctly; (3) whether they can find the LCM for different denominators or revert to the product method; and (4) whether they simplify using the GCF. Avoid presenting only same-denominator cases, as these mask the misconception that students can ignore denominator alignment. Embed questions where borrowing is mandatory and where the result requires non-trivial simplification. Use visual or step-by-step scaffolds to help students model the borrowing process explicitly before asking them to work abstractly.

name: Fractions with negatives (addition and subtraction)
masteryDefinition: Fluently add and subtract fractions (including mixed numbers) where one or both operands are negative, correctly applying integer rules to fractional quantities, handling sign changes in numerator or denominator, and simplifying results. Mastery includes working with negative fractions in both improper and mixed form, recognising equivalent representations (e.g. -3/4 = 3/-4), and justifying sign rules through visual or algebraic reasoning.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring the negative sign when adding/subtracting fractions
misconceptions.description: Student treats -3/5 + 1/5 as 3/5 + 1/5 = 4/5, or -2/3 - 1/3 as 2/3 + 1/3 = 1. The negative sign is dropped entirely and only the magnitude is processed. Often occurs when procedural focus is on 'find common denominator and add numerators' without attending to sign.

misconceptions.label: Applying integer subtraction rule incorrectly to fraction subtraction
misconceptions.description: When computing 1/4 - (-2/4), student writes 1/4 - 2/4 = -1/4 instead of 1/4 + 2/4 = 3/4. The rule 'subtracting a negative becomes addition' is either forgotten or misapplied to the numerator only rather than the entire fraction. Conversely, -1/3 - 2/3 may be computed as -1/3 + 2/3 when no double negative is present.

misconceptions.label: Misplacing the negative sign in the result
misconceptions.description: Student correctly identifies that the result is negative but places the minus sign inconsistently: writing 3/-5 instead of -3/5, or computing -1/2 + (-1/4) = 3/4 (correct magnitude, wrong sign). May also write answer as -(3/5) when -3/5 is expected, suggesting uncertainty about standard form.

misconceptions.label: Confusing the sign of the fraction with the operation sign
misconceptions.description: Student struggles to distinguish between the negative sign that is part of a number (e.g. -3/5 as a negative fraction) and the operation sign (e.g. the minus in 1/4 - 3/5). Leads to errors like -3/5 - 1/5 being computed as -(3/5 - 1/5) = -2/5 instead of -4/5, or incorrectly reordering terms because the negative is treated as part of the operation rather than the quantity.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of negatives in the expression
difficultyDimensions.easy: Single negative (either one fraction is negative, or one subtraction operation)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple negatives (double negative subtraction, both fractions negative, mixed signs across numerator and denominator)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Whether common denominator is already present
difficultyDimensions.easy: Fractions already have the same denominator; focus is purely on integer sign rules
difficultyDimensions.hard: Fractions have different denominators; student must simultaneously find common denominator and apply negative rules

difficultyDimensions.easy: Proper fractions and single negatives (e.g. -2/5 + 3/5)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Mixed numbers with negatives, improper fractions, or negatives in denominator (e.g. -2 1/3 + 1 2/5, or -7/4 - (-5/2))

transferContexts.example: A thermometer reads -3°C. The temperature drops by 2/5 °C per hour over 1 hour. What is the final temperature? (Model as -3 - 2/5.)

transferContexts.context: Directed line segments or number line movements (left/right, up/down)
transferContexts.example: A point is at position -1/2 on a number line. It moves 3/8 units to the left, then 5/8 units to the right. What is its final position? (Model as -1/2 - 3/8 + 5/8.)

transferContexts.context: Profit and loss or account balance (gain/loss, credit/debit)
transferContexts.example: A shop has a balance of -£2/3 (owing money). A transaction gains £5/6. What is the new balance? (Model as -2/3 + 5/6.)

generativeContext: This is a Year 8+ stretch skill that extends fraction arithmetic into the signed number system. Core difficulty: students must simultaneously manage two layers of reasoning—fractional magnitude (numerator, denominator, common denominator) and integer sign rules (which they typically learned in isolation for whole numbers). The skill requires fluency with: (1) adding fractions with same/different denominators; (2) the convention that -a/b = a/(-b) ≠ -(a/b) in value but all represent the same negative quantity; (3) integer rules (negative + negative, positive + negative, double negatives); (4) recognising that subtraction distributes the negative: a - (b + c) = a - b - c, and a - (-b) = a + b. Stretch quality arises because students must hold multiple representations of negative fractions in mind, apply rules flexibly (not mechanically), and explain why procedures work using properties of integers. Key misconceptions often arise from: dropping negative signs entirely (treating -a/b as a/b), confusing operation signs with number signs, or applying integer rules incompletely (e.g. remembering 'two negatives make a positive' for subtraction but not applying it correctly across fraction forms). Effective generation should layer misconceptions progressively: start with single negatives and common denominators, then combine. Require clear working and sign tracking. Use concrete contexts (temperature, position, money) to anchor abstract sign rules. Challenge stretch students to justify rules algebraically (why does subtracting a negative become addition?) and to predict whether a result is positive or negative before computing.

name: Order of operations with fractions (addition and subtraction, no multiply/divide)
masteryDefinition: Students can correctly evaluate expressions involving multiple additions and subtractions of fractions (including mixed numbers and different denominators) by applying left-to-right evaluation, finding common denominators when required, and simplifying results to lowest terms. They understand that addition and subtraction are evaluated with equal priority from left to right, and can work fluently with expressions requiring up to three operations.

misconceptions.label: Performing operations right-to-left instead of left-to-right
misconceptions.description: Student evaluates 3/4 - 1/3 - 1/6 by calculating 1/3 - 1/6 first (= 1/6) then 3/4 - 1/6, rather than (3/4 - 1/3) first then subtract 1/6. This reveals a fundamental misunderstanding that subtraction is left-associative and that order matters for non-commutative operations.

misconceptions.label: Adding or subtracting numerators and denominators separately without common denominator
misconceptions.description: Student calculates 1/3 + 1/4 as (1+1)/(3+4) = 2/7, or applies this error within a multi-operation expression. This shows confusion about what the denominator represents and failure to recognise the need for a common denominator even when explicitly dealing with fractions that cannot be combined directly.

misconceptions.label: Mishandling the integer part of mixed numbers during subtraction
misconceptions.description: When subtracting mixed numbers such as 2 1/4 - 1 3/5, student subtracts integer parts and fractional parts separately without borrowing (e.g., 2 - 1 = 1 and 1/4 - 3/5 attempted directly, leading to incorrect or abandoned calculation). Student does not recognise that 1/4 < 3/5 requires regrouping the integer part.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring the need to simplify or reduce final answers
misconceptions.description: Student correctly finds a common denominator and performs operations correctly but leaves the answer as an unsimplified fraction (e.g., 6/8 instead of 3/4) or fails to recognise when an improper fraction should be converted to a mixed number. This reflects incomplete mastery of fraction normalisation.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of operations in the expression
difficultyDimensions.easy: Single operation (e.g., 1/2 + 1/3)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Three or more operations chained (e.g., 2/3 + 1/4 - 1/6 + 3/8)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Common or related denominators where LCM is obvious (e.g., 1/2 and 1/4)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Unrelated prime or composite denominators requiring full LCM calculation (e.g., 5/12 - 2/15 + 1/8)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Presence and handling of mixed numbers
difficultyDimensions.easy: Proper fractions only (e.g., 2/5 + 1/3)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Mixed numbers with borrowing/regrouping required (e.g., 3 1/5 - 2 3/4)

transferContexts.example: A recipe calls for 1 2/3 cups of flour, then 3/4 cup is added, then 1/6 cup is removed by accident. How much flour is now in the bowl? Evaluate: 1 2/3 + 3/4 - 1/6.

A runner covers 1/4 of a 10 km course, then an additional 2/5 of the course, then walks back 1/10 of the course. What fraction of the total course has been covered (net)? Express as a mixed number or simplified fraction.

transferContexts.context: Financial and ratio contexts (money, discounts, allocations)
transferContexts.example: A budget of £100 is split: 3/8 for supplies, 1/4 for materials, then 1/6 is returned. What fraction of the original budget remains unallocated?

generativeContext: N5.12 extends students' fraction fluency into multi-step arithmetic expressions where the associativity and commutativity rules differ from those for integer arithmetic. The skill is stretch-level because it requires simultaneous management of (1) left-to-right precedence for operations of equal priority, (2) common denominator reasoning across multiple fractions, (3) possible simplification and mixed number conversion, and (4) conceptual robustness in choosing operation order (not right-to-left shortcuts that work for integers). Question design should systematically vary: the number of operations (2–4), the denominator structure (simple/related vs. coprime/composite requiring detailed LCM), presence of mixed numbers and whether regrouping is required, and the order of operations (ensure some require borrowing during subtraction, some do not, to test genuine understanding rather than routine). Avoid multiplication and division entirely to isolate the precedence and denominator-handling challenge. Use negative intermediate results sparingly at this level—the focus is on operation order and fraction arithmetic mastery, not signed-number reasoning. Provide worked examples showing step-by-step simplification and explicit labeling of 'left-to-right' evaluation for multi-operation cases.

name: Algebraic terminology (e.g. term, expression, coefficient)
masteryDefinition: Students can accurately identify, name and distinguish between algebraic components: terms, expressions, coefficients, variables, constants, and factors. They understand that a term is a single product of numbers and variables, an expression is a collection of terms separated by + or −, and a coefficient is the numerical multiplier of a variable. They can parse complex algebraic statements and correctly label each component.

misconceptions.label: Conflating 'term' with 'variable'
misconceptions.description: Students believe a term is only the variable part (e.g., in 3x + 2, they say x is the term, not 3x). They do not recognise that a term is the entire product including the coefficient and all variable factors.

misconceptions.label: Misidentifying coefficient in expressions with implicit multiplication
misconceptions.description: Students fail to recognise the coefficient in terms like x (coefficient 1), −y (coefficient −1), or 2ab (coefficient 2 applies to the whole product ab). They may say 'there is no coefficient' or identify only the first number encountered.

misconceptions.label: Treating expressions and equations as the same thing
misconceptions.description: Students use the terms 'expression' and 'equation' interchangeably, not recognising that an expression is a collection of terms without an equals sign, while an equation has two sides linked by =. This leads to confusion when asked to 'simplify the expression' versus 'solve the equation'.

misconceptions.label: Confusion between factors and terms in algebraic context
misconceptions.description: Students believe 3 and x are separate 'terms' in 3x, or that in 2(a + b) the factors are separate terms rather than recognising the entire bracketed expression is one factor. They do not distinguish multiplicative decomposition from additive decomposition.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single-variable terms (5x, 2y, −3z)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multi-variable terms with mixed coefficients and powers (−7abc, 2x²y³z)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Explicit positive coefficients (3x, 5ab, 10y²)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Implicit or negative coefficients (x, −y, −a²b) and coefficient 1 or −1

difficultyDimensions.easy: Simple expressions with like terms only (3x + 5x + 2)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Nested expressions with brackets and multiple term types (2(3x + 4) − 5(x − 1) + 7)

transferContexts.example: Simplify 5a + 3b + 2a − b. Students must first identify that 5a and 2a are terms with the same variable, and 3b and −b are terms with the same variable, in order to combine them correctly.

transferContexts.context: Expanding brackets and applying the distributive property
transferContexts.example: Expand 3(2x + 5). Students must recognise that 3 is a factor (multiplier) outside the bracket, and that 2x + 5 is an expression made of two terms, each of which must be multiplied by 3.

transferContexts.context: Factorising expressions and finding common factors
transferContexts.example: Factorise 6a + 9b. Students must identify the greatest common factor and recognise that 6a and 9b are separate terms, and both share a common factor; they then express the result as a product of factors.

generativeContext: Algebraic terminology is foundational for all subsequent algebra work. Students must learn to parse symbolic statements precisely: a term is a product of a coefficient and one or more variables (e.g., −5xy is a single term with coefficient −5); an expression is a sum or difference of terms without an equals sign (3x + 2 − y); a coefficient is the numerical part multiplying the variables; a variable is an unknown represented by a letter; a constant is a term with no variables. Ensure questions progress from single-variable, explicit-coefficient terms through multi-variable and implicit-coefficient terms, then to multi-term expressions and nested structures. Avoid ambiguity: always clarify whether you are asking about a term, the whole expression, a factor, or a coefficient. Common errors: students confuse terms with variables, fail to recognise coefficient 1 or −1, treat expressions as equations, and misidentify factors versus terms in products. Use concrete labelling tasks (e.g., 'Circle the term. Box the coefficient. Underline the variable.') and comparison questions (e.g., 'Which is a term: 3x, or x, or 3x + 2?') to expose and correct these misconceptions early.

name: Algebraic notation and basic collecting like terms (juxtaposition, index, fraction)
masteryDefinition: Students can write algebraic expressions using conventional notation (juxtaposition for multiplication, index notation for powers), identify and collect like terms correctly, and simplify expressions involving linear terms and simple powers. They understand that 2a means 2 × a, that a² is distinct from 2a, and that only terms with identical variable parts can be combined.

misconceptions.label: Collecting unlike terms
misconceptions.description: Students incorrectly combine terms with different variables or different powers, e.g. writing 2a + 3b = 5ab, or 3a + 2a² = 5a³. They treat algebraic terms as purely numerical and ignore the variable part.

misconceptions.label: Misinterpreting juxtaposition as addition
misconceptions.description: Students read 3a as 3 + a rather than 3 × a, leading to errors in simplification and when substituting values. They may also struggle to recognize that ab means a × b.

misconceptions.label: Confusing index notation with multiplication
misconceptions.description: Students write a² as 2a, or conflate a × a with 2a. They may think x³ means 3x or write 4² as 4 × 2 = 8 rather than 4 × 4 = 16. Root cause is conflating the operation indicated by an exponent with repeated multiplication and positional notation.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect simplification of fractions with variables
misconceptions.description: When simplifying expressions like (2a + 4b)/2, students cancel individual terms instead of factoring, writing a + 2b as a + b or leaving 2a/4 as 2a/4 without reducing to a/2. They do not recognize the need to divide all terms in the numerator by the denominator.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single variable: simplify 3a + 2a
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple variables: simplify 2x + 3y + 5x - y and identify which can be combined

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Presence of powers and index notation
difficultyDimensions.easy: Linear terms only: collect 4m + 2m - 3m
difficultyDimensions.hard: Mixed powers: simplify 2a² + 3a + a² - a and correctly group squared and linear terms

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Presence of fractional coefficients and denominators
difficultyDimensions.easy: Integer coefficients: collect 5x + 2x
difficultyDimensions.hard: Fractional and complex forms: simplify (3x + 6)/3 or collect x/2 + x/3

transferContexts.context: Forming and simplifying expressions from word problems
transferContexts.example: A rectangle has length (2a + 3) and width a. Write an expression for the perimeter and simplify it.
Solve: 3x + 2x - 5 = 20. Students must first collect 3x + 2x before isolating x.
Simplify 4b + 2b - 3b, then substitute b = 5 to find the value. Error in simplification (e.g. treating unlike terms as like) leads to wrong final answer.

generativeContext: A1.2 is the foundational skill for algebraic fluency. Students must internalize that juxtaposition denotes multiplication (3a = 3 × a), that index notation represents repeated multiplication of the base (a² = a × a, not 2a), and that only terms with identical variable parts and powers can be combined. Common errors stem from: (1) treating variables as labels rather than quantities, leading to incorrect combination of unlike terms; (2) misreading notation, conflating 3a with 3 + a or a² with 2a; (3) applying arithmetic rules mechanically without attending to algebraic structure. Fractional forms introduce additional difficulty: students must understand that dividing a sum by a number requires distributing the division across all terms. When generating questions, vary the number of variables, introduce different powers (a, a², occasionally a³), include negative terms and coefficients, and progress from collecting single-variable expressions to multi-variable ones. Emphasize notation interpretation: explicitly show 3 × a written as 3a, and reinforce that a² means 'a multiplied by itself', not 'a times 2'. Use substitution tasks to validate correct simplification before calculation errors mask algebra errors. Avoid framing like terms as a purely mechanical rule; connect to the distributive law (3a + 2a = (3 + 2)a = 5a).

name: Substitution into expressions
masteryDefinition: Students can accurately substitute numerical values for variables in algebraic expressions and evaluate them correctly, following the order of operations. They understand that substitution replaces each occurrence of a variable with the given value, and can handle expressions involving multiple variables, negative numbers, and operations with different priorities.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring order of operations after substitution
misconceptions.description: Students substitute values correctly but then perform operations left-to-right without respecting BODMAS/PEMDAS. For example, substituting x=2 into 3 + 2x gives 3 + 2 × 2, but they calculate (3+2) × 2 = 10 instead of 3 + 4 = 7.

misconceptions.label: Multiplying the coefficient by the variable value rather than with it
misconceptions.description: Students treat 2x with x=3 as 2 × x = 2 × 3 = 6 but then don't apply the multiplication correctly, or misinterpret 2x to mean 2+x. Related error: writing 23 instead of 2×3 when substituting.

misconceptions.label: Substituting into only some occurrences of the variable
misconceptions.description: When a variable appears multiple times in an expression, students substitute the value into only the first or some occurrences. For example, x + x + 3 with x=2 becomes 2 + x + 3 = 5 + x, leaving x unsubstituted.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect handling of negative substitutions
misconceptions.description: When substituting negative values, students fail to use brackets or mishandle the sign. For example, x² with x=−2 becomes −2² = −4 instead of (−2)² = 4, or they treat −2² as needing brackets written but still compute it as −4.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single variable (e.g., 3x + 5)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple different variables (e.g., 2a + 3b − c)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Complexity of operations and order of operations required
difficultyDimensions.easy: Addition and subtraction only (e.g., x + 4)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Mixed operations with higher priority (e.g., 2x² + 3x − 5 or 10 ÷ x + 2)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Sign and magnitude of substituted values
difficultyDimensions.easy: Positive integer substitutions (e.g., x=3)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Negative, fractional, or zero substitutions (e.g., x=−2, x=0.5, x=0)

transferContexts.context: Formulas in real-world contexts (speed, area, cost)
