transferContexts.example: The formula for distance is d = 35t. Calculate d when t = 4 (substituting into a linear formula with a coefficient).

transferContexts.context: Evaluating polynomial expressions in algebraic contexts
transferContexts.example: If p(x) = x² − 4x + 3, find p(−1) (evaluating a quadratic with negative substitution).
Check if x=2 is a solution to 2x + 3 = 7 by substituting (confirmation through substitution).

generativeContext: Substitution into expressions is foundational algebraic thinking. Students must replace every occurrence of a variable with a bracketed value and then correctly apply order of operations. Difficulty scales with: variable count (single vs. multiple), operation variety (addition-only vs. mixed with powers/division), and value complexity (positive integers vs. negatives/zero/fractions). Common errors cluster around operation precedence failures post-substitution, coefficient mishandling (treating 3x as 3+x or 3×x differently in evaluation), partial substitution (missing repeated variables), and sign errors with negatives and powers. Effective questions isolate each dimension: simple expressions test substitution accuracy; BODMAS-heavy expressions test procedural knowledge; negative or zero values test conceptual robustness. Transfer appears immediately in formula evaluation and equation checking, making this skill essential for all later algebra.

name: Simplify expressions by collecting like terms
masteryDefinition: Students can identify terms with identical variable parts (same letters and powers), combine their coefficients using addition and subtraction, and write simplified algebraic expressions in standard form. They understand that only like terms can be combined and that the variable part remains unchanged when collecting terms.

misconceptions.label: Combining unlike terms
misconceptions.description: Students incorrectly combine terms with different variables or powers, e.g. simplifying 3x + 2y + x as 6xy instead of 4x + 2y, or 5x² + 3x as 8x³. They do not check that the variable parts are identical before combining.

misconceptions.label: Treating coefficients and variables as separate entities to add
misconceptions.description: Students add all numbers and all letters separately without recognising like terms, e.g. simplifying 4a + 3b + 2a + 5b as 9 + 8ab or (4+3+2+5) + (a+b+a+b). They lose sight of which coefficients belong to which variables.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring sign changes when collecting terms
misconceptions.description: Students drop or misapply negative signs when combining like terms, e.g. simplifying 5x - 3x + 2 as 8x + 2 instead of 2x + 2, or treating subtraction as addition. They may also incorrectly apply the distributive property and add the negative sign to the coefficient rather than treating it as an operation.

misconceptions.label: Changing the power of terms during simplification
misconceptions.description: Students alter exponents when combining like terms, e.g. simplifying 3x² + 2x² as 5x⁴ instead of 5x², or 4ab + 2ab as 6a²b². They may apply operation rules for exponents even when just adding coefficients.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single variable (e.g. 3x + 5x + 2)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Four or more distinct variables and mixed powers (e.g. 2a²b + 3ab² + 4a²b - 5ab + 6)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Presence and complexity of negative/subtraction terms
difficultyDimensions.easy: All positive or one simple subtraction (e.g. 4x + 3x - 2x)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple negative terms with mixed signs throughout (e.g. -3x + 5y - 2x - 4y + 6x - y)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Arrangement and repetition of like terms
difficultyDimensions.easy: Like terms grouped consecutively (e.g. 2x + 3x + 5 + y)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Like terms scattered throughout with repeated variable patterns (e.g. 3a + 2b + 4 - a + 3b - 2 + 5a)

transferContexts.example: Solve 2x + 5 + 3x = 20 by first simplifying the left side to 5x + 5

transferContexts.context: Finding perimeters of polygons with algebraic side lengths
transferContexts.example: A triangle has sides of length (2x + 3), (x + 5), and (3x - 1). Write and simplify an expression for its perimeter.
Expand 2(x + 3) + 4(x - 1) and simplify by collecting like terms to get 6x + 2

generativeContext: A1.4 is the foundational skill for manipulating algebraic expressions. Students must reliably distinguish like terms (identical variable parts with identical exponents) from unlike terms, then combine coefficients using signed arithmetic whilst preserving the variable part unchanged. Mastery requires careful tracking of signs: the term '-3x' contributes -3 to the coefficient of x, not +3. Common errors cluster around mixing variables, mishandling negatives, and accidentally combining terms with different powers. Expressions may range from simple single-variable cases (4a + 2a) to multi-variable polynomials with repeated variables at different powers (3xy + 2x + 5xy). Sequencing: introduce with all positive terms, then introduce subtraction, then mixed arrangements. Link to equation-solving (where simplifying one side is a prerequisite step) and perimeter problems (collecting terms from side lengths). Avoid expressions with parentheses initially; reserve those for composition with A1.5.

name: Multiply a single term over a bracket (expand)
masteryDefinition: Students can correctly expand brackets by multiplying a single term (numerical, algebraic, or both) by each term inside the bracket, applying the distributive property accurately. They can handle positive and negative multipliers, simplify the resulting expression, and explain why the expansion is valid.

misconceptions.label: Only multiplying the first term in the bracket
misconceptions.description: Student multiplies the external term by only the first term inside the bracket and ignores the remaining terms. For example: 3(2x + 5) = 6x + 5 instead of 6x + 15. This stems from incomplete application of the distributive property.

misconceptions.label: Sign error with negative multipliers
misconceptions.description: Student fails to correctly apply the sign when multiplying by a negative term. For example: -2(3x - 4) = -6x - 8 instead of -6x + 8. The student multiplies the coefficient but does not distribute the negative sign correctly to the second term.

misconceptions.label: Multiplying only numbers, ignoring variables
misconceptions.description: Student treats algebraic terms as if they were purely numerical, losing the variable part. For example: 5a(2 + 3b) = 10 + 15 instead of 10a + 15ab. The variable is dropped during multiplication.

misconceptions.label: Adding instead of multiplying terms
misconceptions.description: Student adds the external term to each term in the bracket instead of multiplying. For example: 4(x + 3) = 4x + 4(3) is written as 4x + 7 or 4x + 4 + 3. This reflects confusion between the distributive property and simple addition.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single positive integer multiplier (e.g. 3(x + 2))
difficultyDimensions.hard: Negative or multi-part multiplier (e.g. -2a(3b - 5c) or (x + 1)(2x) expressed as a single term times bracket)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of terms in the bracket
difficultyDimensions.easy: Two terms in the bracket (e.g. 2(x + 3))
difficultyDimensions.hard: Three or more terms in the bracket (e.g. 5(2x - 3y + 4z))

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Presence of like terms after expansion
difficultyDimensions.easy: No simplification needed after expansion (e.g. 3(a + b) = 3a + 3b)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Like terms created during expansion that must be combined (e.g. 2(x + 3) + x requires combining to 3x + 6)

transferContexts.example: Given 6x + 9, factor out the greatest common factor to rewrite as 3(2x + 3). This requires recognizing that expansion and factorisation are inverse operations.
Solve 2(x + 5) = 18. Students must expand 2(x + 5) to 2x + 10, then solve 2x + 10 = 18 to find x = 4.

transferContexts.context: Area and perimeter of rectangles with algebraic dimensions
transferContexts.example: A rectangle has length (2x + 3) and width 4. Find the area. Students must expand 4(2x + 3) to get 8x + 12.

generativeContext: A1.5 focuses on the distributive property in its most basic form: a single term multiplied by a polynomial (typically binomial or trinomial). The skill requires students to systematically multiply the external term by each term inside the bracket separately, then combine results. Key conceptual points: (1) every term in the bracket must be multiplied—this is where the 'only first term' error arises; (2) signs must be carefully tracked, especially when the multiplier or terms inside are negative (distributive property for negative numbers); (3) the result should be simplified if like terms emerge; (4) the operation is commutative (3(x+2) = (x+2)×3, though right-distribution is less common at KS3). Expansion is the gateway to factorisation, solving equations, and polynomial manipulation. Generate questions that vary systematically: positive/negative multipliers, integer/algebraic/mixed multipliers, binomials/trinomials, and contexts requiring simplification. Avoid questions that jump to two-bracket expansion (that is A2.1) or require collecting like terms across multiple expansions in a single step initially.

name: Factorise by taking out a common factor
masteryDefinition: Students can identify the highest common factor (HCF) of all terms in an algebraic expression and write the expression as a product of that factor and a bracket containing the remaining expression. They can verify their factorisation by expanding and should recognise when no common factor exists beyond 1.

misconceptions.label: Takes out only a partial common factor
misconceptions.description: Student identifies a common factor but fails to extract it completely from all terms. For example, factorises 6x + 4y as 2(3x + 4y) correctly, but then factorises 6x² + 9x as 3(2x² + 3x) instead of 3x(2x + 3), leaving a common factor of x still in the bracket.

misconceptions.label: Confuses factorisation with expansion or applies inverse operation incorrectly
misconceptions.description: Student writes the expression with brackets but does not consistently divide each term by the extracted factor. For instance, writes 8a + 12 = 4(8a + 12) or 8a + 12 = 4(2a + 3) without fully dividing the second term, or reverses the process by multiplying instead of dividing.

misconceptions.label: Identifies incorrect or non-existent common factor
misconceptions.description: Student extracts a factor that does not divide evenly into all terms, or picks the smallest factor instead of the highest common factor. For example, factorises 12x + 8y as 2(6x + 8y) when 4(3x + 2y) is the correct answer, or tries to factor out an algebraic term that does not appear in every term.

misconceptions.label: Fails to factorise expressions with algebraic common factors
misconceptions.description: Student can extract numeric common factors but does not recognise or extract common algebraic factors (variables and their powers). For example, leaves 5x² + 10xy as 5(x² + 2xy) instead of 5x(x + 2y), or does not attempt to factorise 3ab + 6a² at all beyond identifying 3.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Numeric common factor only (e.g. 6x + 9 → 3(2x + 3))
difficultyDimensions.hard: Mixed numeric and algebraic common factor with powers (e.g. 12x³y + 8x²y² → 4x²y(3x + 2y))

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of terms and diversity of variables
difficultyDimensions.easy: Two terms, one variable (e.g. 7a + 14)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Four or more terms with multiple variables and mixed powers (e.g. 6a²b + 9ab² + 12a³ + 15ab)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Simple factorisations with straightforward expansion check
difficultyDimensions.hard: Expressions where HCF must be justified; student must explain why no further factorisation is possible or identify when the expression is prime

transferContexts.example: Solve 3x² + 6x = 0 by factorising and finding the roots.
Simplify (8x + 12) / 4 by factorising the numerator first.

transferContexts.context: Working with area or perimeter problems involving factorisation
transferContexts.example: A rectangle has dimensions (5x + 10) by 2. Express the area in factored form and find its value when x = 3.

generativeContext: Factorising by taking out a common factor is a foundational algebraic skill that requires students to: (1) systematically identify the highest common factor (HCF) of numerical coefficients using prime factorisation or listing factors; (2) identify the lowest power of each variable that appears in every term; (3) divide each term by this HCF to obtain the bracket contents; (4) verify by expanding back to the original expression. Students must distinguish between numeric factors (2, 3, 4, 5, ...) and algebraic factors (x, y, x², xy, ...) and understand that the HCF includes both. Common errors include extracting only a partial factor (leaving another factor inside the bracket), dividing only some terms, extracting a factor that doesn't divide all terms evenly, or ignoring algebraic factors entirely. Expressions may have 2–4 terms, single or multiple variables, and powers ranging from 1 to 3+. Difficulty scales with the complexity of the HCF (numeric only vs. mixed) and the number of distinct variables. This skill directly enables solving equations by factorisation, simplifying fractions, and working with polynomial identities.

name: Write expressions and formulae from worded descriptions
masteryDefinition: Students can reliably translate worded mathematical descriptions into algebraic expressions and formulae, correctly identifying variables, operations, and the order in which operations are performed. They can distinguish between expressions (single calculations) and formulae (relationships between variables), and use appropriate algebraic notation including brackets, indices, and fraction notation where needed.

misconceptions.label: Literal translation order (left-to-right reading)
misconceptions.description: Students write operations in the exact order words appear in the sentence rather than applying correct mathematical order of operations. For example, 'multiply 3 by x then add 5' becomes 3x + 5, but 'add 5 to the product of 3 and x' is written as 5 + 3x, treating it as left-to-right instead of recognising the final addition structure.

misconceptions.label: Confusion between expression and equation
misconceptions.description: Students write an equals sign when describing a process or calculation that should be an expression. For example, 'double a number and add 3' becomes 2x = 3 instead of 2x + 3. They treat every worded description as if it contains a 'is' or 'equals' statement.

misconceptions.label: Misinterpretation of 'more than' and 'less than' as operations on the variable
misconceptions.description: Students reverse the operation when translating comparative language. For example, 'x more than 5' is written as 5 - x or x - 5 instead of x + 5. Similarly, '3 less than y' becomes y - 3 instead of y - 3 (this one is correct, but 'less than' applied first triggers the error: writing 3 - y).

misconceptions.label: Omission of the operation symbol or bracket when required
misconceptions.description: Students write numbers and variables adjacently without explicit operators, or fail to use brackets around grouped operations. For example, 'add 2 to x then multiply by 4' is written as 2x4 instead of 4(x + 2), or as 4x + 2 by ignoring the grouping instruction.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Simple, single operation: 'add 5 to x'; 'multiply y by 3'
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multi-step with embedded clauses: 'divide the sum of p and q by 2'; 'subtract 7 from three times the length'

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of variables and operations involved
difficultyDimensions.easy: Single variable, single operation: 3x; y + 4
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple variables and operations with brackets and indices: 3(a + b)² or (m - n)/5

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Presence of explicit grouping cues in the wording
difficultyDimensions.easy: Clear sequential language: 'multiply x by 2, then add 3'
difficultyDimensions.hard: Implicit grouping requiring interpretation: 'the product of a and the sum of b and c'; 'twice as large as the difference between p and q'

transferContexts.context: Writing formulae for geometric properties (perimeter, area, volume)
transferContexts.example: The perimeter of a rectangle with length l and width w is twice the length plus twice the width. Write a formula for the perimeter.

transferContexts.context: Setting up equations from real-world problems before solving
transferContexts.example: A cinema ticket costs £8. Write an expression for the cost of n tickets. Now write an equation if the total cost is £56.
The nth term of a sequence is described as: start with 2 and add 5 each time. Write the formula for the nth term.

generativeContext: A1.7 requires students to act as translators between natural language and symbolic mathematical notation. Key cognitive demands: (1) parsing the grammar of the description to identify the main operation and any sub-operations, (2) assigning variables to unknowns or parameters, (3) applying correct mathematical order of operations (BIDMAS) even when words don't follow that sequence, (4) choosing appropriate notation (brackets, fractions, indices) to encode the structure, and (5) distinguishing when a statement describes a process (expression) versus a relationship (formula/equation). Misconceptions often stem from students treating English left-to-right syntax as identical to mathematical syntax, or conflating the roles of variables and constants. High-frequency errors arise in multi-operation descriptions, especially those involving division or 'reverse' operations (less than, difference). When generating questions, systematically vary: (a) operation count and nesting depth, (b) use of comparative/relational language (more, less, product, quotient, difference), (c) presence of constants vs. parameters, and (d) whether the context is a pure expression task or requires students to identify that an equals sign is needed. Always ask for the formula/expression, not the answer to a specific case, to isolate this skill from arithmetic.

name: Solve one-step linear equations
masteryDefinition: Solve linear equations of the form ax + b = c or ax = b or x + b = c by applying inverse operations correctly, demonstrating understanding that the same operation must be applied to both sides of the equation to maintain equality. Student can identify the operation to undo, apply its inverse, and verify their solution.

misconceptions.label: Applies operation to only one side
misconceptions.description: Student performs the inverse operation on only one side of the equation rather than both sides. For example, solving x + 5 = 12 by writing x + 5 - 5 = 12, leaving 12 unchanged, then concluding x = 12 instead of x = 7.

misconceptions.label: Uses wrong inverse operation
misconceptions.description: Student applies the wrong inverse operation. For example, when solving x - 3 = 8, student adds 3 to get x = 5 instead of recognizing subtraction requires addition as the inverse, yielding x = 11. Or applying multiplication instead of division when solving 4x = 20.

misconceptions.label: Confuses the variable with the constant
misconceptions.description: Student isolates the wrong term or reverses the roles of variable and constant. For example, solving 3x = 12 by writing 3 = 12/x or concluding x = 3/12 instead of x = 12/3, treating the coefficient as if it were the answer.

misconceptions.label: Performs correct operation but arithmetic error follows
misconceptions.description: Student correctly identifies the inverse operation and applies it to both sides but makes a computational error in the resulting arithmetic. For example, solving x + 7 = 15 correctly as x = 15 - 7 but calculating 15 - 7 = 9 instead of 8.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Simple addition/subtraction: x + 5 = 12 or x - 3 = 10
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiplication/division or negative coefficients: -2x = 14 or 3x = -9

difficultyDimensions.easy: Small positive integers: x + 2 = 9
difficultyDimensions.hard: Large numbers, decimals, or negatives: x - 23.5 = -7.2 or 12x = 156

difficultyDimensions.easy: Equation where substitution is straightforward: x + 1 = 6 → x = 5
difficultyDimensions.hard: Equation requiring multi-step verification or with fractions: x/4 = 3 → x = 12 (requires multiplying back)

transferContexts.example: A book costs £5 more than a pen. If the book costs £12, find the cost of the pen. Write and solve: p + 5 = 12.
A rectangle has a perimeter of 24 cm and one side is 5 cm. If all sides are equal except two are unknown, write and solve: 2x + 10 = 24 for the unknown side length.
A recipe requires 3 times as much flour as sugar. If 180g of flour is used, write and solve: 3s = 180 to find the sugar needed.

generativeContext: One-step linear equations are algebraic statements of balance: both sides of the equals sign represent the same quantity. Solving requires undoing the operation applied to the variable by applying its inverse operation to both sides simultaneously. The operation hierarchy matters: addition/subtraction are inverses of each other; multiplication/division are inverses of each other. Students must develop procedural fluency (identify the operation, apply its inverse correctly to both sides) and conceptual understanding (why we do the same thing to both sides—to maintain equality). Key error patterns include applying operations to only one side (breaking balance), using the wrong inverse (e.g., subtracting when adding was needed), arithmetic mistakes in the final calculation, and confusion about which number to isolate. Verification—substituting the answer back into the original equation—is a critical check that students often skip. Extend difficulty by introducing negative numbers, larger quantities, and two-step equations where students must undo multiple operations in reverse order.

name: Solve two-step linear equations
masteryDefinition: Students can solve two-step linear equations of the form ax + b = c or ax - b = c by correctly applying inverse operations in the correct order (subtract/add first, then divide/multiply), and can verify their solution by substitution. They understand that the goal is to isolate the variable and that operations must be performed on both sides of the equation.

misconceptions.label: Performing operations in wrong order (multiplying/dividing before adding/subtracting)
misconceptions.description: Student solves 3x + 5 = 20 by first dividing by 3 to get x + 5/3 = 20/3, then attempting to isolate x, rather than subtracting 5 first. This reflects a misunderstanding of inverse operations and order of operations in equation-solving context.

misconceptions.label: Forgetting to apply operations to both sides of the equation
misconceptions.description: Student solves 2x + 7 = 15 by subtracting 7 from the left side only (getting 2x) then dividing by 2, without subtracting 7 from the right side. Results in incorrect answer like x = 4 instead of x = 4.

misconceptions.label: Using the wrong inverse operation or sign confusion
misconceptions.description: When solving 4x - 6 = 10, student adds 6 instead of adding 6 to isolate 4x, or when solving -2x + 8 = 14, student subtracts 8 from both sides and then multiplies instead of divides by -2. Often stems from confusion over which operation is inverse to the operation in the equation.

misconceptions.label: Arithmetic errors in intermediate steps and no verification
misconceptions.description: Student correctly identifies steps needed but makes calculation errors (e.g., 20 - 5 = 14 instead of 15, or 15 ÷ 3 = 4 instead of 5), and does not check by substituting back into the original equation to catch the error.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Equations with integer coefficients of ±1 or ±2 (e.g., 2x + 5 = 13)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Equations with larger integer coefficients, fractions, or negative coefficients (e.g., -3x + 8 = 2, or x/2 + 4 = 10)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Standard form ax + b = c where the constant appears on one side only (e.g., 5x + 3 = 18)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Equations requiring rearrangement or with variables and constants on both sides (e.g., 3x + 2 = x + 10)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Equations requiring only addition/subtraction of positive numbers in the second step (e.g., 4x + 7 = 23)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Equations with subtraction of positive numbers or division by negative coefficients (e.g., -2x - 5 = 1)

transferContexts.context: Real-world problem contexts requiring equation setup and solution
transferContexts.example: A phone costs £40 and a protective case costs £8. If the total spent is £136 for 3 phones and cases, write and solve an equation to find the cost per case (or number of items). Equation: 3(40 + c) = 136 → simplify and solve as two-step equation.

transferContexts.context: Solving equations embedded in perimeter or area problems
transferContexts.example: The perimeter of a rectangle is 26 cm. If the length is 5 cm more than the width, write an equation for the perimeter and solve for width. Equation: 2w + 2(w + 5) = 26 → simplify to 4w + 10 = 26 → solve as two-step.

transferContexts.context: Multi-step algebraic reasoning where two-step equations appear as intermediate steps
transferContexts.example: Solve the formula for a specific variable: Given A = 2πr + c, solve for r when A = 50 and c = 10. Simplifies to 2πr + 10 = 50, a two-step equation in r.

generativeContext: Two-step linear equations are the foundational gate to all further equation-solving. Students must internalise that solving means performing inverse operations in reverse order of operations: if the equation is ax + b = c (or ax - b = c), they subtract/add b first (the constant term added/subtracted to the variable), then divide/multiply by a. The critical conceptual insight is that every operation must preserve equality—what you do to one side must be done to the other. Common failure points: (1) reversing the order of inverse operations (trying to divide before subtracting), (2) applying an operation to only one side, (3) arithmetic slips in intermediate steps without verification. Verification by substitution is not optional; it is a habit students must form. Equations should range from basic (2x + 3 = 7) through mixed signs and larger coefficients (−3x + 5 = −4) to prepare students for multi-step equations, equations with variables on both sides, and eventually linear systems. Contextual applications should emphasize that equation-solving is a tool for modelling and reasoning about real constraints, not a mechanical skill.

name: Solve equations with unknowns on both sides
masteryDefinition: Students can solve linear equations where the unknown appears on both sides of the equals sign by collecting like terms on one side, using inverse operations correctly, and checking their solution. They understand that moving terms between sides requires applying the same operation to both sides, and can solve equations of the form ax + b = cx + d where a, b, c, d are integers.

misconceptions.label: Failing to apply inverse operation to both sides when moving terms
misconceptions.description: Student moves a term from one side to the other but only changes the sign on that term, rather than understanding they must apply the same operation to both sides. For example, solving 3x + 5 = x + 9, they write '3x - x + 5 = 9' (moving x but not adjusting the other side), leading to 2x + 5 = 9 which is correct by accident, but the reasoning is flawed. In other cases: 2x + 7 = x + 12, they incorrectly write 2x + 7 - 7 = x + 12 (removing 7 from left only), getting 2x = x + 12.

misconceptions.label: Incorrectly changing signs when moving terms across the equals sign
misconceptions.description: Student understands the need to move terms but changes the sign inconsistently or incorrectly. For example, solving 5x - 3 = 2x + 6, they write 5x - 2x - 3 = 6 (correct so far), but then when moving the -3, they write 3x - 3 = 6, then 3x = 6 - (-3) = 9, showing confusion about whether the -3 becomes +3 or stays as -3.

misconceptions.label: Collecting terms on the wrong side or incompletely
misconceptions.description: Student isolates the unknown on the right side instead of the left, or collects some terms with unknowns but not all. For example, solving 3x + 8 = x + 20, they move only one instance of x and get confused about which side should be 'final', writing 8 = x - 3x + 20, then struggling to solve. Or they move terms but leave unknowns on both sides: 3x = x + 12 and stop, not recognizing both sides still have x.

misconceptions.label: Arithmetic error in collecting like terms
misconceptions.description: Student correctly sets up the equation and moves terms correctly, but makes a basic arithmetic mistake when combining like terms. For example, solving 4x - 2 = 2x + 10, correctly gets 4x - 2x = 10 + 2, but then writes 2x = 12 as 2x = 11 or computes 10 + 2 = 11 instead of 12, leading to x = 5.5 instead of x = 6.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Coefficient complexity (integer coefficients of unknown terms)
difficultyDimensions.easy: Coefficients are 1 or simple like 2, 3 (e.g. 2x + 5 = x + 9)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Coefficients are larger or negative (e.g. -3x + 8 = 2x - 12 or 7x - 4 = -2x + 50)

difficultyDimensions.easy: One unknown term on each side and one constant on each side (e.g. 2x + 3 = x + 7)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple terms on at least one side or fractions/decimals (e.g. 4x + 2x - 5 = 3x + 10 or 0.5x + 2 = x - 1)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Mostly positive coefficients and constants (e.g. 2x + 5 = x + 9)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Mix of positive and negative terms on both sides (e.g. -2x + 7 = 3x - 8)

transferContexts.context: Balancing real-world quantities (e.g. weight, cost, age)
transferContexts.example: Tom's age plus 5 equals his sister's age minus 2. Tom is x, his sister is 2x - 3. Write and solve: x + 5 = 2x - 3 - 2. Or: two parcels must have equal weight; one weighs 4a + 200g, the other weighs a + 500g. Find a.

transferContexts.context: Geometry problems involving perimeter or equal lengths
transferContexts.example: Two sides of a rectangle: one side is (3x - 2) cm, the opposite side is (x + 6) cm. Since opposite sides are equal, solve 3x - 2 = x + 6 to find x.

transferContexts.context: Solving problems with formulas rearranged to unknowns on both sides
transferContexts.example: Using the formula F = 1.8C + 32, solve for when Fahrenheit equals Celsius: C = 1.8C + 32. Or distance problems: distance from A = 5t + 10, distance from B = 3t + 50. When are they equal?

generativeContext: A1.10 is the progression from solving one-sided equations (e.g. 2x + 5 = 13) to genuinely two-sided equations (e.g. 3x + 5 = x + 9). The conceptual shift is that students must now decide which side to collect the unknown on, and must systematically apply inverse operations to both sides to move terms. Key procedural demands: (1) identify like terms on each side; (2) choose a side to collect x on (either is valid, but left is conventional); (3) move terms by adding or subtracting the same value to both sides; (4) simplify and divide to isolate x; (5) check by substitution. Common scaffolding errors: students forget that moving a term means applying an operation to both sides, not just flipping its sign. They may also panic when x appears on both sides and either not attempt to move it, or move it incorrectly. Stress that the equation is like a balanced scale—whatever you do to one side, you must do to the other. Use colour-coding or explicit 'write the operation' language: 'Subtract x from both sides' not just 'move x'. Difficulty increases with negative coefficients, multiple terms per side, and decimals/fractions. Emphasise checking: substituting the answer back into the original equation is both a verification tool and a confidence-builder. Link to inverse operations (A1.5) and expressions (A1.1-2) from earlier in KS3.

name: Solve equations involving brackets
masteryDefinition: Students can fluently expand brackets in linear equations and solve for the unknown variable. They demonstrate understanding by correctly applying the distributive property to remove brackets, combining like terms, and isolating the variable through inverse operations. Mastery includes solving equations with brackets on one or both sides, recognising when solutions exist, and checking answers by substitution.

misconceptions.label: Distributing only to the first term inside brackets
misconceptions.description: Students expand 2(x + 3) as 2x + 3 instead of 2x + 6. They apply the coefficient only to the first term in the bracket, not recognising that the distributive property requires multiplication of every term inside the bracket.

misconceptions.label: Sign errors when distributing negative coefficients
misconceptions.description: Students expand -2(x + 3) as -2x + 6 instead of -2x - 6, or expand -(x - 5) as -x - 5 instead of -x + 5. They fail to distribute the negative sign consistently across all terms in the bracket.

misconceptions.label: Removing brackets without expansion
misconceptions.description: Students write 3(x + 2) = 3x + 2 by simply deleting the brackets without multiplying any terms. This suggests a procedural gap: they do not understand that brackets indicate multiplication applies to all terms inside.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect inverse operation after expanding brackets
misconceptions.description: Students correctly expand 2(x + 3) = 10 to 2x + 6 = 10, but then subtract 6 from both sides to get 2x = 4, then multiply by 2 instead of dividing by 2, yielding x = 8 instead of x = 2. They confuse the direction of inverse operations.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of bracket layers and terms
difficultyDimensions.easy: Single bracket with two terms: 2(x + 3) = 10
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple brackets or nested structures: 2(3(x + 1) - 2) = 16, or brackets on both sides: 2(x + 3) = 3(x - 1)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Sign complexity of coefficient and interior terms
difficultyDimensions.easy: Positive coefficient and positive terms inside: 3(x + 2) = 15
difficultyDimensions.hard: Negative coefficient or mixed signs: -2(x - 3) = 8 or 4(2x - 5) + 3(x + 1) = 0

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Presence of like terms outside and inside brackets
difficultyDimensions.easy: Bracket is the only algebraic term: 2(x + 5) = 18
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple bracket terms and constants to combine: 2(x + 3) + 4x - 5 = 21

transferContexts.example: The perimeter of a rectangle is 36 cm. The length is 2(x + 1) cm and the width is (x + 2) cm. Write and solve an equation to find x.

transferContexts.context: Real-world linear relationships and cost problems
transferContexts.example: A cinema charges £8 per ticket. A group buys 2(x + 3) tickets for £64. Write and solve an equation to find x, where x is the number of additional tickets beyond a base amount.

transferContexts.context: Algebraic identities and simplification before solving
transferContexts.example: Solve 5(2a + 1) - 3(a - 2) = 19 by first expanding and simplifying both sides.

generativeContext: A1.11 requires students to integrate two core competencies: distributive expansion and equation solving via inverse operations. The skill sits at the intersection of numerical and algebraic reasoning. Key teaching moves: (1) motivate expansion through area/scaling contexts (e.g., 3 groups of (x + 2) items); (2) drill sign-aware distribution with explicit left-to-right term-by-term expansion; (3) integrate solving by explicitly labeling each inverse operation step; (4) use substitution checks to reinforce understanding. Common generation traps: students may pass single-term brackets (2x = 10) but fail on multi-term (2(x+3)=10). Error diagnosis should distinguish between expansion errors (m1, m2, m3) and solving errors (m4). Stretch: nested brackets, brackets on both sides, fractional coefficients, or systems-of-equations setups. For typical cohort, prioritise fluency in positive-coefficient two-term cases before moving to negative or multi-bracket scenarios.

name: Generate terms of a sequence from a term-to-term rule
masteryDefinition: Students can reliably apply a given term-to-term rule (recursive rule) to generate the next term in a sequence, correctly identifying the starting value and systematically applying the rule to produce multiple consecutive terms. They can work both forward (given first term, produce subsequent terms) and verify backward (check if a given term fits the pattern). They recognise when a rule is ambiguous or incomplete.

misconceptions.label: Applying the rule to the term number rather than the term value
misconceptions.description: Students apply the rule using the position n instead of the actual term value. For example, given 'add 3' and starting value 5, they write: term 1 = 5, term 2 = 1+3 = 4, term 3 = 2+3 = 5, instead of 5, 8, 11. They confuse index with value.

misconceptions.label: Misunderstanding multiplication in the rule as separate from addition
misconceptions.description: Given a rule like 'multiply by 2 then add 1', students either multiply all previous terms or add the increment to every term cumulatively, losing track of the operation order or scope. For example, starting at 3: they write 3, 6, 12, 24 (only multiplying) instead of 3, 7, 15, 31.

misconceptions.label: Applying the rule inconsistently or forgetting intermediate steps
misconceptions.description: Students correctly apply the rule for the first 1–2 terms but then skip steps, apply different rules, or make arithmetic errors that compound. For example, given 'subtract 2' starting at 10: they write 10, 8, 6, 3 (error on the last term) or apply the rule only some of the time.

misconceptions.label: Confusing term-to-term rule with position-to-term (explicit) rule
misconceptions.description: Students treat a term-to-term rule as if it were an nth term formula. Given 'add 5' and told the first term is 2, they write: term n = n + 5, or they try to use the rule only once to jump directly to term 10, instead of iterating from term 1.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single operation with small integers (add 2, subtract 1, multiply by 2)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multi-step rules or operations with decimals/fractions (multiply by 0.5 then add 3, divide by 2 and round)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Generate 3–4 terms
difficultyDimensions.hard: Generate 8–10 terms or identify a specific term position without listing all previous terms

difficultyDimensions.easy: Positive starting value, rule produces increasing sequence
difficultyDimensions.hard: Negative or fractional starting value; rule produces decreasing or oscillating sequence

transferContexts.example: A student deposits £50 initially. Each week they add £8 more. Write the first 6 terms of the sequence of savings and identify how much they have after 4 weeks.
A pattern of matchsticks starts with 4. Each time you add a new shape, you add 3 more matchsticks. Generate the sequence of matchstick counts for the first 5 shapes.
A bacterial colony starts with 100 cells. Each hour the population triples. Generate the population sequence for hours 0–5.

generativeContext: A1.12 focuses on applying iterative, recursive rules. Core competency: take the current term, apply an arithmetic operation or sequence of operations, obtain the next term. Repeat. The rule is a transformation of one term to the next, not a formula indexed by position. Students must: (1) extract the rule correctly from language (add, subtract, multiply, divide, combinations); (2) identify or be given the first term; (3) apply the rule systematically without losing arithmetic accuracy or confusing the rule with something else; (4) extend to as many terms as required. Misconception a1-12-m1 is very common: students conflate term number (index) with term value. Misconception a1-12-m2 arises when operations are chained or phrased ambiguously. Misconception a1-12-m3 reflects careless arithmetic or inconsistent application—quality control is critical. Misconception a1-12-m4 shows category confusion between 'what is the next term?' (recursive) and 'what is the term at position n?' (explicit formula). Difficulty scales with rule complexity (single vs. multi-step), length of sequence, negativity/fractionality of terms, and whether the sequence grows, shrinks, or oscillates. Transfer contexts should always embed the rule in realistic or visual narrative; students must extract and apply the rule themselves, not just compute arithmetic.

name: Generate terms of a sequence from a position-to-term (nth term) rule
masteryDefinition: Students can fluently substitute position values (n) into an nth term rule to generate correct sequence terms. They understand that n represents position (1st, 2nd, 3rd, etc.), correctly apply order of operations when simplifying expressions involving n, and verify their answers by checking multiple terms. They can work with rules containing coefficients, constants, and operations including multiplication, division, and powers, and can generate both positive and negative terms.

misconceptions.label: Treating n as a coefficient multiplier rather than a substitution variable
misconceptions.description: Students write '2n + 3 with n=2' as '2(2)+3 = 7' but then mistakenly compute it as 2×2+3 = 7 (correct by accident) but conceptually think n is just 'the number in the sequence'. When the rule is 3n – 1, they may write n as a fixed digit (e.g. '31 – 1 = 30') rather than substituting. This reveals confusion about what n represents and how algebra works.

misconceptions.label: Arithmetic errors in order of operations when substituting
misconceptions.description: Students correctly identify what to substitute but apply operations in the wrong order. For example, with rule 2n + 5 and n=3, they compute 2×3+5 as 2×(3+5) = 16 instead of 6+5 = 11. Or with n² + 3 and n=2, they compute 2²+3 as (2+3)² = 25 instead of 4+3 = 7. Often occurs when rules contain powers or multiple operations.

misconceptions.label: Confusing position (n) with the term value itself
misconceptions.description: Students believe the nth term rule describes the position number, not the value. For instance, with rule 5n, they think the 3rd term is just 3, not 15. They may conflate 'the 5th term' (position) with 'term 5' (value), leading them to ignore or misapply the rule. This is especially common when sequences are presented informally without explicit notation.

misconceptions.label: Sign errors with negative coefficients or subtraction
misconceptions.description: Students struggle with negative numbers in the rule. For example, with rule –2n + 10 and n=3, they compute –2×3+10 as –6+10 = 4 (correct) but inconsistently apply the negative sign in other instances, sometimes writing –(2×3)+10 = –6+10 = 4 and other times writing –2×3+10 = –2×13 or similar. Also fails with rules like 20 – 3n, computing 20–3×4 as 20–12 = 8 correctly but then using 17 for the next term without recalculating.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Complexity of the nth term rule expression
difficultyDimensions.easy: Linear rules with positive coefficient and constant (e.g. 3n + 2, n + 5)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Rules with negative coefficients, division, or powers (e.g. –2n + 15, n²/2 + 1, 2n² – 3n + 4)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Range and sign of position values and resulting terms
difficultyDimensions.easy: Generating early terms (n = 1, 2, 3) yielding positive values (e.g. n=2 in rule 5n gives 10)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Generating terms where large n or negative coefficients yield negative outputs (e.g. n=10 in rule 20–3n gives –10), or handling fractional results

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of operations in the rule
difficultyDimensions.easy: Single operation (e.g. 3n, n + 5, 2n – 1)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple operations requiring careful order of operations (e.g. 2n² + 3n – 7, 5(n+1) – 2)

transferContexts.context: Real-world sequences (time, distance, cost, patterns in nature)
transferContexts.example: A parking charge is given by C = 2n + 1 pounds, where n is the number of hours. Find the cost for 4 hours, 8 hours, and 12 hours of parking.

transferContexts.context: Geometric or visual pattern sequences (tiles, dots, matchsticks)
transferContexts.example: A pattern of tiles grows such that the nth figure contains 4n + 2 tiles. How many tiles are in figures 1, 3, 5, and 10?

transferContexts.context: Validation and reversal (finding position from term value)
transferContexts.example: The nth term of a sequence is 3n – 2. Generate the first five terms, then work backwards: if a term equals 19, what is its position n?

generativeContext: A1.13 focuses on the substitution and simplification skill needed to generate sequence terms from an algebraic rule. This is foundational to understanding sequences and functions. The core operation is: given a rule T(n) = expression, substitute specific integer values of n (representing position: 1st, 2nd, etc.) and simplify to find the term value. Students must distinguish between n (position variable) and the term value (output). Common errors arise from: (1) misunderstanding what n represents; (2) failing to apply order of operations correctly after substitution, especially with powers, brackets, or negative numbers; (3) arithmetic slips in computation. Effective generation should vary rule complexity (linear, quadratic, with negatives, division), position values (small, large, mixed signs in output), and require explicit working. Scaffold with concrete position labels ('when n=', 'the 5th term where n=5') and demand simplified final answers. Misconception detection should flag incomplete substitution (n left in answer), wrong operation order (especially powers), and arithmetic errors in sign handling.

name: Find the nth term rule for a linear sequence
masteryDefinition: Students can identify the common difference in a linear sequence, use it to derive the nth term formula in the form un = an + b, verify the rule by substituting known terms, and use the rule to find any term in the sequence.

misconceptions.label: Confusing the common difference with the first coefficient
misconceptions.description: Students correctly identify the common difference d but believe the nth term rule is un = d·n without adding the constant term. For example, in the sequence 5, 8, 11, 14, they identify d = 3 but write un = 3n instead of un = 3n + 2, leading to incorrect terms like u1 = 3 instead of u1 = 5.

misconceptions.label: Using position number (n) inconsistently between 0-indexing and 1-indexing
misconceptions.description: Students find the correct coefficient and constant but mix up whether n starts at 0 or 1. They may derive un = 3n + 2 correctly but then claim u1 = 3(1) + 2 = 5 is the first term, when they should verify against the given sequence position. Alternatively, they assume n = 0 for the first term and write un = 3n + 5 for the sequence 5, 8, 11, leading to u1 = 8 instead of 5.

misconceptions.label: Treating the sequence as non-linear when the common difference is not immediately obvious
misconceptions.description: When a sequence has a common difference that requires calculation (e.g., 2, 5, 10, 17 with differences 3, 5, 7), students incorrectly conclude the sequence is quadratic or cannot be described by a linear rule, rather than recognizing they need to check if they are looking at the right sequence type.

misconceptions.label: Incorrectly calculating the constant term by substituting wrong position values
misconceptions.description: Students find the correct common difference a but make arithmetic errors when substituting a known term to find b. For example, with sequence 7, 10, 13, they correctly identify a = 3, but when using u2 = 10, they write 10 = 3(2) + b and solve b = 4 instead of correctly substituting u1 = 7 to get 7 = 3(1) + b, so b = 4 (correct), or making sign errors in the rearrangement.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Increasing sequences with positive common difference (e.g., 2, 5, 8, 11)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Decreasing sequences with negative common difference (e.g., 20, 17, 14, 11) or mixed-sign rules (e.g., un = -2n + 15)

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Size and complexity of the constant term
difficultyDimensions.easy: Sequences where the constant term is small and positive (e.g., un = 2n + 1)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Sequences with large or negative constant terms (e.g., un = 4n − 23 or un = n − 100)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Common differences that are whole numbers (e.g., d = 3)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Common differences that are fractions or decimals (e.g., d = 0.5 or d = 1/3, leading to rules like un = 0.5n + 2.5)

transferContexts.example: A mobile phone plan costs £10 upfront and £2 per month. Write the nth term rule for the total cost un (in pounds) after n months, and use it to find the cost after 12 months.
A pattern uses tiles: Figure 1 has 4 tiles, Figure 2 has 7 tiles, Figure 3 has 10 tiles. Find the rule un for the number of tiles in Figure n, and determine how many tiles are in Figure 20.
A sequence is known to follow un = an + b. The 5th term is 16 and the 8th term is 25. Find the values of a and b, then write the full rule.

generativeContext: Linear sequences have a constant common difference d between consecutive terms. The nth term rule has the form un = an + b, where a is the common difference and b is a constant. To find the rule: (1) Calculate d by subtracting consecutive terms. (2) Use any known term, say u1, to find b by solving u1 = a(1) + b, or more generally uj = a(j) + b for the jth term. (3) Verify by checking at least two terms from the original sequence. Students must maintain consistent indexing (n typically starts at 1 for the first term in KS3 contexts) and handle negative differences and fractional differences correctly. Common errors include omitting the constant term b, misidentifying which coefficient represents d, using incorrect position values when solving for b, and failing to verify the derived rule. When sequences contain negative terms, large constant offsets, or fractional differences, students require careful arithmetic and sense-checking.

name: Recognise and use sequences of triangular, square and cube numbers
masteryDefinition: Students can identify triangular numbers (1, 3, 6, 10, 15, ...), square numbers (1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ...), and cube numbers (1, 8, 27, 64, 125, ...) from sequences and lists. They understand the geometric or additive structure underlying each sequence, can find the nth term using the appropriate formula or pattern, and can verify membership of a number in each sequence. They can also apply these sequences to problem contexts involving arrangements or volumes.

misconceptions.label: Confusing triangular and square number sequences
misconceptions.description: Students treat triangular numbers as though they follow the same pattern as square numbers, often writing 1, 2, 3, 4, 5... or confusing the visual/additive structure. For example, they may claim 5 is a triangular number or that the 4th triangular number is 16 rather than 10. This arises from not recognising that triangular numbers grow by successive odd numbers (1, 1+2, 1+2+3) whereas square numbers grow by successive odd numbers starting from 1 (1, 1+3, 1+3+5).

misconceptions.label: Assuming all figurate sequences follow n² rule
misconceptions.description: Students apply the formula n² to triangular and cube numbers. For example, they may calculate the 5th triangular number as 5² = 25 instead of 1+2+3+4+5 = 15, or claim the 3rd cube number is 3² = 9 rather than 3³ = 27. This stems from over-generalising the square number formula without understanding the distinct additive or multiplicative structures.

misconceptions.label: Misremembering cube number sequence values
misconceptions.description: Students recall incorrect cube number values or skip terms. For example, they might list cube numbers as 1, 8, 25, 64 (including a square number), or claim the 4th cube number is 48 instead of 64. This typically occurs because cubes grow rapidly and are less frequently encountered than squares, making memorisation and pattern spotting harder.

misconceptions.label: Not recognising the additive pattern within triangular numbers
misconceptions.description: Students fail to see that consecutive triangular numbers differ by consecutive integers (T₄ - T₃ = 4, T₅ - T₄ = 5, etc.). Instead, they treat them as random or memorised values, unable to predict or extend the sequence. This limits their ability to find later triangular numbers without a formula or list.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Finding or identifying the 1st, 2nd, or 3rd term in each sequence (e.g. T₁ = 1, S₂ = 4, C₃ = 27)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Finding or identifying the 10th, 15th, or 20th term in each sequence without a list (e.g. finding T₁₅ = 120 or C₁₀ = 1000)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Identifying which sequence a given number belongs to from a short list (e.g. 'Is 36 a square number, triangular number, or cube number?')
difficultyDimensions.hard: Determining membership for large or ambiguous numbers, or explaining why a number does not belong to any sequence (e.g. 'Prove that 50 is not a triangular number')

difficultyDimensions.easy: Using a given sequence list or diagram to find a term or verify membership
difficultyDimensions.hard: Deriving or applying explicit formulas (Tₙ = n(n+1)/2, Sₙ = n², Cₙ = n³) to find terms or solve problems without reference materials

transferContexts.example: How many stacked spheres are needed to form a pyramid with 5 layers, where each layer is a square arrangement? (Answer requires recognising this as a sum of square numbers.)
A cube has a side length of 4 units. What is its volume? Is there a link to the sequence of cube numbers? If you scale up to a cube with side 5, how does the volume change?
The number of handshakes in a group of n people where everyone shakes hands with everyone else is T_(n-1). If there are 45 handshakes, how many people are in the group?

generativeContext: A1.15 focuses on three concrete figurate number sequences: triangular (T_n = 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + n = n(n+1)/2), square (S_n = n²), and cube (C_n = n³). Students must develop dual competence: (1) procedural — computing terms by position, extending sequences, verifying membership — and (2) conceptual — understanding the geometric or additive structure (triangular as sums, square as grid, cube as volume). High-frequency errors centre on formula confusion (applying n² to triangular numbers), sequence mixing (treating triangular like square), and weak additive intuition (not recognising the difference pattern 1, 2, 3, ... between consecutive triangular numbers). Difficulty scales with term position (finding T₂₀ is harder than T₃), with verification of large numbers, and with formula-free reasoning. Transfer is strongest to combinatorial problems (handshakes, stacked objects) and spatial/volume contexts. Emphasise visual/concrete models (dot arrays, stacking) before formulas; highlight that these are distinct patterns, not variations of one rule; and build automaticity with small values (T₁–T₁₀, S₁–S₁₀, C₁–C₅) to support efficient recognition.

name: Use coordinates in all four quadrants
masteryDefinition: Students can accurately identify, plot, and interpret points using coordinates (x, y) in all four quadrants of a Cartesian plane. They understand that the x-coordinate indicates horizontal position (positive right, negative left) and the y-coordinate indicates vertical position (positive up, negative down). They can read coordinates from a graph and plot points given their coordinates, correctly handling negative values in both axes.

misconceptions.label: Confusing x and y coordinates
misconceptions.description: Students reverse the order of coordinates, plotting (x, y) as (y, x). For example, plotting (3, -2) as 3 units up and 2 units left instead of 3 units right and 2 units down. This often stems from inconsistent labelling or misremembering 'x comes first'.

misconceptions.label: Treating negative coordinates as positive distances
misconceptions.description: Students correctly identify that a coordinate is negative but plot it as a positive distance in the opposite direction, or ignore the negative sign entirely. For example, plotting (-4, 2) at (4, 2) or moving left by 4 but not going into the second quadrant.

misconceptions.label: Misunderstanding the origin as a starting point rather than intersection
misconceptions.description: Students treat the origin as a reference point to count from but lose track of signed distance. They may count correctly on one axis but struggle when both coordinates are negative, or assume the origin must be 'included' in their counting process.

misconceptions.label: Confusing quadrant labels or quadrant location rules
misconceptions.description: Students misidentify which quadrant a point belongs to, such as thinking the second quadrant contains points where both x and y are negative, or incorrectly naming quadrants (e.g., bottom-left is Q2 rather than Q3). This reflects incomplete understanding of the sign rules for each region.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Points in quadrant 1 only (positive, positive) or single negative coordinate
difficultyDimensions.hard: Points in quadrant 3 (both negative) or mixed quadrants in same problem

difficultyDimensions.easy: Coordinates with small integer values (±1 to ±5)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Larger integer values (±10 or beyond) or non-integer coordinates (0.5, -2.5)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Reading a single point from a labelled graph or plotting a single point with axes clearly marked
difficultyDimensions.hard: Identifying multiple points, interpreting distance between points, or using coordinates to solve a geometric problem (e.g., finding a missing vertex of a rectangle)

transferContexts.example: A treasure map uses coordinates where (0, 0) is a landmark. North and East are positive; South and West are negative. Point A is at (-3, 5). Describe its position relative to the landmark and plot it.
The line y = 2x − 1 passes through which of these points: (−2, −5), (−1, −3), (0, −1), (1, 1)? Identify all correct points by substituting x-values and checking y-coordinates.
A rectangle has vertices at (1, 1), (4, 1), (4, −2), and (1, −2). Reflect it across the x-axis. What are the new coordinates of each vertex?

generativeContext: A1.16 requires fluent coordinate literacy across all four quadrants. The Cartesian plane is foundational for subsequent algebra, geometry, and statistics. Key cognitive demands: (1) internalising the sign convention (x: left ↔ right, y: down ↔ up) without reversal or confusion; (2) mapping ordered pairs to points without transposition; (3) handling negative values as directed distance, not magnitude; (4) locating and naming quadrants by sign rules. Common failure modes: reversal of x and y (very frequent in low-exposure cohorts); ignoring or misinterpreting the negative sign (treating −3 as a leftward distance of 3, then plotting at (3, y)); conflating quadrant names with location (Q2 vs Q3); and struggling when both coordinates are negative simultaneously. Vary problem structure systematically: begin with single quadrant, progress through adjacent quadrants, then introduce quadrant III (both negative) last. Use visual scaffolding (shaded quadrants, arrow annotations) initially, then fade. Emphasise the reading vs. plotting distinction: reading requires extraction from a given graph; plotting requires construction. Combine reading and plotting in the same problem to reinforce bidirectional understanding. Introduce coordinates as a system, not isolated points—ask students to identify all points satisfying x > 0, or all points on a specific line, to deepen conceptual grounding.

name: Plot and interpret straight-line graphs (y = mx + c)
masteryDefinition: Students can confidently identify and use the gradient (m) and y-intercept (c) from the equation y = mx + c to plot straight-line graphs accurately on a coordinate grid. They can interpret gradients as rates of change, read coordinates from plotted lines, and write equations of lines from graphs or given information. They understand that parallel lines share the same gradient and can apply this to real-world contexts.

misconceptions.label: Confusing gradient and y-intercept
misconceptions.description: Students treat m and c interchangeably, plotting the y-intercept as the gradient value or using the y-intercept to determine steepness. For example, in y = 2x + 3, plotting points as if the line starts at (2, 3) or rises by 3 for every 1 unit across.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect application of gradient as rise/run
misconceptions.description: Students reverse the gradient fraction or misinterpret negative gradients. For example, treating gradient 2/3 as 3/2, or plotting a line with gradient –2 by moving down 2 and left 1 instead of down 2 and right 1.

misconceptions.label: Misidentifying the y-intercept
misconceptions.description: Students incorrectly identify where the line crosses the y-axis, reading it as the x-intercept instead, or confusing it with a point the line passes through that is not on the y-axis. For instance, claiming the y-intercept is 5 when the line crosses at (0, –3).

misconceptions.label: Assuming all straight lines follow y = mx + c form
misconceptions.description: Students struggle with vertical lines (x = a) or horizontal lines (y = c), trying to force them into y = mx + c format or claiming vertical lines have undefined gradients without understanding why.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Positive gradients only, e.g. y = 2x + 1
difficultyDimensions.hard: Negative gradients and fractional gradients, e.g. y = –3/4 x – 2

difficultyDimensions.easy: Positive integer y-intercepts, e.g. y = x + 5
difficultyDimensions.hard: Negative, fractional, or zero y-intercepts, e.g. y = 2x – 7/2 or y = 3x

difficultyDimensions.easy: Given equation, plot the line; identify gradient and y-intercept from equation
difficultyDimensions.hard: Given two points or a graph, derive the equation; interpret gradient as a real-world rate; determine parallel or perpendicular lines

transferContexts.example: A taxi charges £2.50 per km plus a £3 base fare. Write the equation in the form y = mx + c where y is total cost and x is distance, then use it to find the cost of a 12 km journey.
Two mobile phone plans: Plan A costs £15 + £0.10 per minute; Plan B costs £20 + £0.05 per minute. Write both as equations and determine when Plan B becomes cheaper.
Find the equation of the line parallel to y = 3x – 4 that passes through the point (2, 5).

generativeContext: A1.17 focuses on the graphical and algebraic representation of linear relationships using y = mx + c. Students must master both directions: given an equation, accurately plot it using gradient and y-intercept; given a graph or points, extract or construct the equation. Key cognitive demands: (1) firmly linking m to steepness and direction via rise/run, (2) locating c as the y-coordinate when x = 0, (3) extending this to interpret real-world contexts where m represents a rate and c an initial value. Fractional and negative gradients are high-error zones. Vertical lines (undefined gradient) and horizontal lines (zero gradient) require explicit contrast. Word problems demand translation of context into the y = mx + c form and reverse interpretation of results. Emphasis should be on checking: does the y-intercept match the graph? Does the gradient match the steepness visually? Do predicted values make sense in context?

name: Inequalities on a number line (integer solutions)
masteryDefinition: Students can represent linear inequalities on a number line using correct notation (open/closed circles), identify all integer solutions from a graphical representation, and solve linear inequalities to find integer solution sets. They understand the difference between ≤/≥ (closed circle) and </> (open circle), can work with compound inequalities (e.g., −2 ≤ x < 5), and verify integer solutions by substitution.

misconceptions.label: Confusing open and closed circles
misconceptions.description: Students use a closed circle for strict inequalities (< or >) and an open circle for non-strict inequalities (≤ or ≥), reversing the correct notation. This often stems from misremembering or failing to connect the symbol to its visual representation (e.g., drawing a closed circle for x > 3).

misconceptions.label: Including or excluding boundary values incorrectly
misconceptions.description: When finding integer solutions from a number line, students either include a boundary integer when the circle is open (e.g., listing 3 as a solution for x > 3) or exclude it when the circle is closed (e.g., not listing −2 for x ≥ −2). This reflects confusion about whether the boundary point itself satisfies the inequality.

misconceptions.label: Reversing inequality direction when multiplying or dividing by negative numbers
misconceptions.description: When solving inequalities algebraically (before graphing), students forget to flip the inequality sign when multiplying or dividing by a negative number. For example, solving −2x < 6 yields x < −3 instead of x > −3, leading to an incorrectly positioned region on the number line.

misconceptions.label: Misinterpreting compound inequalities
misconceptions.description: Students struggle to represent or list integer solutions for compound inequalities (e.g., −3 < x ≤ 2). They may treat each part separately, fail to identify the overlapping region, or incorrectly assume the region should exclude one or both boundaries. Common error: listing integers from −3 to 2 inclusive when the correct set is {−2, −1, 0, 1, 2}.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Simple inequalities with one variable and no coefficients (e.g., x > 5, y ≤ −2)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multi-step inequalities requiring algebraic manipulation, including negative coefficients and division (e.g., −3x + 7 < 4, then finding integer solutions)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single inequalities with one boundary (e.g., x < 6)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Compound inequalities with two boundaries and different operators (e.g., −4 ≤ 2x − 1 < 9)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Reading integer solutions directly from a pre-drawn number line with marked points
difficultyDimensions.hard: Solving the inequality algebraically first, then drawing the number line representation and listing all integer solutions

transferContexts.example: A mobile phone plan costs £20 base fee plus £0.05 per text. If a customer can spend at most £35 per month, write and solve an inequality to find the maximum number of texts they can send. Represent the solution on a number line and identify the integer values within the allowable spending limit.

A student needs to score at least 70 marks but fewer than 90 marks to achieve a Grade B in an exam. Represent this range on a number line and list all possible integer scores that would result in a Grade B.

A engineering specification requires a part to have a length of 50 mm with a tolerance of ±2 mm (i.e., the length must satisfy 48 ≤ length ≤ 52). Draw this on a number line and determine which integer millimetre lengths fall within the acceptable range.

generativeContext: A1.18 focuses on the intersection of algebraic inequality solving and graphical representation using number lines. This is a stretch skill requiring students to move fluidly between symbolic and visual modes. Key cognitive demands: (1) understanding inequality symbols and their directional meaning; (2) translating between algebraic form (e.g., 2x − 5 > 3) and graphical form (shaded region on a number line with appropriately placed open/closed circle); (3) extracting discrete integer solutions from a continuous region; (4) handling compound inequalities where two conditions must both be satisfied. The skill demands precision in notation—open vs. closed circles must match the inequality type exactly. Algebraic solving introduces the critical rule that multiplying or dividing by a negative reverses the inequality, a rule students often misapply or forget. When generating questions, vary the algebraic complexity (single-step vs. multi-step), the form of the boundary (integer vs. fractional solutions that must be rounded to integers), and the presence of compound conditions. Verify that answer sets are explicitly enumerable (e.g., {−1, 0, 1, 2}) rather than requiring infinite precision. Include distractors that exploit common misconceptions: wrong circle type, inclusion/exclusion errors at boundaries, and sign-reversal mistakes.

name: Form and solve equations from context (word problems)
masteryDefinition: Students can independently identify the unknown variable in a real-world context, translate prose or symbolic descriptions into a correct linear equation, solve that equation using inverse operations or systematic methods, and verify their solution against the original context. They understand that the equation is a model of the situation and can interpret what the solution means in context.

misconceptions.label: Misidentifying the unknown or setting up the wrong variable
misconceptions.description: Students choose the wrong quantity to represent as the variable. For example, in 'John has 5 more marbles than Sarah. Together they have 27 marbles,' they set x = total marbles instead of x = Sarah's marbles. This leads to an incorrect equation structure. Often arises because students don't systematically identify what the question is actually asking.

misconceptions.label: Incorrectly translating 'more than' or 'less than' relationships
misconceptions.description: Students write x + 5 when the context says 'Sarah has 5 fewer than John' (should be x − 5), or reverse the operation entirely. This stems from confusing the order of operations in prose with the order in algebra. Example: 'A number is 8 less than double another number' → students write 2x − 8 when they should write 2x or 2y − 8 depending on which is the reference.

misconceptions.label: Forgetting to form a complete equation before solving
misconceptions.description: Students extract one expression from the problem (e.g., '3x + 4') but don't identify what it should equal. They jump to 'solving' an incomplete statement. This often happens when the context uses implicit equality ('the cost is...') rather than explicit '= ' notation.

misconceptions.label: Not checking the solution against the original context
misconceptions.description: Students solve the equation correctly to get x = 10, but fail to verify: 'Does 10 marbles make sense?' or 'Is the original condition satisfied?' This leads to accepting impossible answers (e.g., negative lengths, fractional people). Students treat the equation as an isolated exercise rather than a model.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of steps to form the equation
difficultyDimensions.easy: Single operation required (e.g., 'A number plus 5 equals 12')
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple relationships to translate (e.g., 'John has 3 times as many as Sarah; together they have 48')

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Clarity of the equality statement in context
difficultyDimensions.easy: Explicit statement: 'The total cost is...', 'equals...', or 'makes...'
difficultyDimensions.hard: Implicit or distributed: 'If we share 20 equally among 4 groups' or information embedded across sentences

difficultyDimensions.easy: One-step equation (x + 5 = 12) or two-step with whole-number solutions
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multi-step equations with fractions, negative coefficients, or solutions that require rearrangement of non-standard forms

transferContexts.example: A rectangular garden has length 3 metres more than its width. The perimeter is 26 metres. Form and solve an equation to find the width.
A shirt is reduced by 20% and costs £16. Form and solve an equation to find the original price.

transferContexts.context: Ratio and proportion in practical settings
transferContexts.example: Paint is mixed in the ratio 2:3 (blue to red). If there are 15 litres of red paint, form and solve an equation to find the volume of blue paint.

generativeContext: A1.19 is a stretch skill requiring students to synthesise interpretation, algebra, and contextual reasoning. The core challenge is bridging natural language to formal algebra: students must parse word problems to identify the unknown, extract relationships, build a valid equation, and then solve while remaining anchored to reality. Key teaching points: (1) Explicitly label the variable at the outset ('Let x = ...'); (2) identify the equality statement in context (often hidden); (3) translate each relationship sequentially, avoiding word-order traps ('5 less than x' = x − 5, not 5 − x); (4) enforce verification by substitution back into the original scenario. Common errors cluster around variable selection and directional mistakes in translation. Stretch tasks should include multi-step formation (e.g., 'two people, relationship given, combined total given'), implicit equality, and contexts requiring students to check feasibility (negative age, fractional items). Use concrete manipulatives or diagrams for formation; use verification checklists for solution interpretation.

name: Identify and name types of angles (acute, right, obtuse, reflex)
masteryDefinition: Student can correctly identify and name angles as acute (0° to 90°), right (90°), obtuse (90° to 180°), or reflex (180° to 360°) using visual recognition and understanding of the defining degree ranges. Student can distinguish between angles by their measure relative to a straight line or full rotation, and can apply this classification consistently across different diagram orientations and contexts.

misconceptions.label: Confusing obtuse and reflex angles
misconceptions.description: Student incorrectly identifies reflex angles (greater than 180°) as obtuse angles, or vice versa. Often stems from measuring only the 'obvious' smaller angle in a diagram rather than recognising which angle is actually being indicated. Common when the reflex angle is not explicitly marked with an arc.

misconceptions.label: Treating right angles and 90° as distinct categories
misconceptions.description: Student believes a right angle is a type separate from '90 degrees', rather than understanding that a right angle is exactly 90°. May classify a 90° angle as 'just a right angle' without recognising its precise measure, leading to confusion when angles are given numerically.

misconceptions.label: Misidentifying acute angles as right angles due to diagram scale
misconceptions.description: Student visually misjudges angles that are close to but not quite 90° (e.g. 85° or 95°) because the diagram is poorly scaled or drawn. Relies purely on visual appearance without reference to angle markers or numerical measures, leading to classification errors on near-right angles.

misconceptions.label: Confusing angle size with arm length
misconceptions.description: Student believes longer arms on an angle diagram indicate a larger angle measure, or shorter arms indicate smaller angles. Does not understand that angle size is determined by rotation between rays, not the physical length of the lines drawn.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Angles clearly within a category (e.g. 30° acute, 120° obtuse, 200° reflex)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Angles very close to category boundaries (e.g. 89° vs 91°, 179° vs 181°) requiring precise measure interpretation

difficultyDimensions.easy: Angles presented with clear numerical measures marked (e.g. '65°') and explicit category labels or colour-coding
difficultyDimensions.hard: Angles shown only as diagrams without numerical labels; student must rely on visual estimation and geometric intuition to classify

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single isolated angle with clearly marked arms and vertex in standard orientation; angle marked with a small arc
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple overlapping angles in a diagram; reflex angles indicated by the 'outside' arc; angles rotated so vertex or arms are not in conventional positions

transferContexts.context: Measuring and constructing angles with a protractor
transferContexts.example: A student uses a protractor to measure an angle marked in a diagram and records it as 145°. They must classify this measured angle as obtuse and explain why it is not acute or reflex.

transferContexts.context: Describing angles in geometric shapes and real-world objects
transferContexts.example: Given a diagram of a roof truss or a partially open door, identify whether the angles formed are acute, right, obtuse, or reflex, and explain the classification based on the angle measure.
If two angles are supplementary and one is acute, classify the second angle (must be obtuse) and verify the reasoning using angle naming conventions.

generativeContext: G1.1 targets foundational angle classification at KS3. Students must internalise four discrete categories defined by degree ranges: acute [0°, 90°), right = 90°, obtuse (90°, 180°), reflex (180°, 360°). The skill requires both visual pattern recognition (matching a diagram to a category) and numerical reasoning (relating measures to ranges). Key challenge: students often conflate visual 'size' with angle size, conflate obtuse/reflex (both > 90°), and misinterpret reflex angles when the arc is not explicitly drawn on the 'outside'. Diagrams vary in clarity, orientation, and arm length—all irrelevant to classification but visually salient to novices. Generation should include: (1) clear single-angle identification tasks with varied numerical proximity to boundaries; (2) visual-only tasks requiring estimation; (3) multi-angle diagrams requiring distinction between adjacent categories; (4) tasks explicitly asking 'why not acute/obtuse/reflex' to probe reasoning; (5) real-world contexts (doors, angles in polygons, compass directions) normalising all four types. Avoid ambiguous diagrams; include protractor or degree labels when pushing numerical boundaries. Emphasise that reflex angles are valid and common, not 'trick' angles.

name: Understand angle notation (e.g. ∠ABC) and label angles correctly
masteryDefinition: Students can correctly identify and name angles using three-point notation (∠ABC), understand that the vertex is the middle letter, distinguish between angles at a point and angles in geometric figures, and accurately label angles in diagrams using both notation and degree measures where appropriate.

misconceptions.label: Vertex position confusion
misconceptions.description: Students write ∠ABC with the vertex letter in the wrong position (e.g., writing ∠BAC when they mean the angle at B), not recognising that the middle letter must be the vertex of the angle. Often arises from reading left-to-right without understanding the three-point rule.

misconceptions.label: Naming angles at a point without disambiguating
misconceptions.description: When multiple angles meet at a point, students label them all with the same notation (e.g., all calling angles at O simply '∠ABC') without using a fourth letter or subscript to distinguish between different angles sharing the same vertex and ray pair.

misconceptions.label: Confusing angle notation with line segment notation
