difficultyDimensions.easy: Decimals that simplify to familiar fractions with small denominators: 0.3̄ = 1/3, 0.8̄ = 8/9
difficultyDimensions.hard: Decimals that yield large numerators and denominators requiring multi-step HCF: 0.1̄2̄3̄ = 123/999 = 41/333

difficultyDimensions.easy: Problems requiring only one multiplication and simple subtraction: 10x - x
difficultyDimensions.hard: Problems requiring two multiplications and careful alignment: 1000x - 10x for numbers with both non-repeating and repeating parts

transferContexts.example: If 0.2̄ = p/q in simplest form, and 2p - q = k, find k. [Requires converting 0.2̄ to 2/9, then computing 2(2) - 9 = -5]
Arrange 0.3̄, 0.3̄1̄, and 7/20 in order of size. [Requires converting both recurring decimals to fractions and comparing: 1/3 ≈ 0.333..., 31/99 ≈ 0.3131..., 7/20 = 0.35]
Show that 0.1̄2̄ is rational by expressing it as a fraction, then use this to explain why π cannot be written as a recurring decimal.

generativeContext: Converting recurring decimals to fractions is a stretch skill requiring fluent algebraic manipulation and deep understanding of decimal place value. The core algorithm involves multiplying the equation x = 0.abc̄ by powers of 10 such that subtracting the original isolates the repeating block. For pure recurring (0.7̄), multiply by 10^1. For mixed recurring (0.1̄7̄), if there are m non-repeating digits after the decimal and n repeating digits, multiply by 10^(m+n) and 10^m, then subtract to eliminate both the repeating and non-repeating parts in one step. Critical errors cluster around: (1) failing to account for non-repeating digits when choosing multipliers; (2) subtracting equations with misaligned place values; (3) arithmetic mistakes in subtraction; (4) forgetting to reduce the final fraction to lowest terms. Students must verify answers by long division. Stretch elements include handling multi-digit repeating blocks, recognising patterns (e.g., 0.9̄ = 1), and connecting to the theorem that rational numbers have terminating or recurring decimal expansions. Generate problems with explicit scaffolding of the multiplication-and-subtract process before asking for simplified answers.

name: Recognise recurring decimals from fraction division
masteryDefinition: Students can identify which fractions produce recurring (repeating) decimals, perform long division to reveal the repeating pattern, express recurring decimals using dot notation (e.g. 0.3̇ or 0.1̇6̇), and explain why certain fractions must produce recurring decimals (connection to divisibility and remainders).

misconceptions.label: Confuses terminating and recurring decimals
misconceptions.description: Students believe that all fractions produce either terminating decimals or infinite non-repeating decimals, and do not recognise that fractions like 1/3, 2/7, or 5/6 must produce recurring patterns. They may incorrectly round or truncate instead of identifying the repeat.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect identification of the recurring block
misconceptions.description: When performing long division, students identify the wrong repeating cycle. For example, with 1/6 = 0.1666..., they may claim the block is '66' instead of '6', or with 1/7 they may claim '142857' repeats from the start instead of recognising the full cycle.

misconceptions.label: Misapplies dot notation
misconceptions.description: Students incorrectly place dots in recurring decimal notation, e.g. writing 0.3̇ when they mean 0.3̇3̇, or placing a single dot over multiple digits without understanding that dots mark the start and end of the repeating block.

misconceptions.label: Assumes division must terminate after a set number of steps
misconceptions.description: Students perform long division but stop prematurely, believing 'the division is complete' when they have calculated a few decimal places. They do not recognise when remainders begin to repeat, indicating the start of a recurring cycle.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Fractions with denominators that are powers of 2 and 5 (e.g. 1/4, 3/8, 7/20) — these always terminate
difficultyDimensions.hard: Fractions with prime factors other than 2 and 5 in the denominator (e.g. 1/3, 2/7, 5/11, 1/13) — these always recur

difficultyDimensions.easy: Simple fractions with short repeating blocks (e.g. 1/3 = 0.3̇, 1/6 = 0.1̇6̇, 1/9 = 0.1̇)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Fractions with long or complex recurring cycles (e.g. 1/7 = 0.1̇4̇2̇8̇5̇7̇, 1/13 with a 6-digit cycle)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Pure recurring decimals where the cycle starts immediately (e.g. 1/3 = 0.3̇, 2/7)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Mixed recurring decimals with non-repeating digits before the cycle (e.g. 1/6 = 0.1̇6̇, 5/12 = 0.41̇6̇)

transferContexts.context: Comparing and ordering fractions using decimal equivalents
transferContexts.example: Order 1/3, 2/7, and 3/11 from smallest to largest by converting to recurring decimals and comparing the repeating values.

transferContexts.context: Solving problems involving measurement and precision
transferContexts.example: A piece of fabric is 5/6 metres long. Express this as a decimal and explain why you cannot give an exact terminating decimal; discuss how recurring decimals affect practical measurements.

transferContexts.context: Connecting to algebraic manipulation of recurring decimals
transferContexts.example: If x = 0.2̇, use algebraic methods to convert the recurring decimal back to a fraction (multiplying by powers of 10 and subtracting).

generativeContext: Recurring decimals emerge naturally when long division of certain fractions never terminates but repeats a pattern. The key insight is that a fraction a/b (in lowest terms) produces a terminating decimal if and only if b has only prime factors 2 and 5; any other prime factor guarantees recurrence. During long division, recurrence is detected when a remainder repeats—at that point, the quotient digits will also repeat. Dot notation (or bar notation) compactly represents the repeating block: the first dot marks where repetition starts, the second marks where it ends. Common errors include: (1) assuming all divisions terminate, (2) misidentifying which remainder signals the start of recurrence, (3) incorrectly placing dots or transcribing the repeating cycle, and (4) stopping division prematurely. Mastery requires fluent long division, recognition of remainder patterns, correct notation, and understanding why certain denominators force recurrence. Emphasise the remainder-tracking strategy: as soon as a remainder repeats, the cycle restarts. Connect to fraction properties (equivalent fractions, denominators' prime factorisation) and later to rational number theory.

name: Find a percentage of an amount (with a calculator)
masteryDefinition: Students can reliably use a calculator to find a percentage of any amount by converting the percentage to a decimal and multiplying, or by using the percentage button where available. They can interpret the result in context and check whether their answer is reasonable relative to the original amount.

misconceptions.label: Dividing by percentage value instead of converting to decimal
misconceptions.description: Student divides the amount by the percentage number rather than converting the percentage to a decimal first. For example, to find 20% of 150, they calculate 150 ÷ 20 = 7.5 instead of 150 × 0.2 = 30. This often stems from confusing the operation needed or misremembering the procedure.

misconceptions.label: Multiplying by the percentage value instead of the decimal equivalent
misconceptions.description: Student multiplies the amount by the percentage number directly rather than its decimal form. For example, to find 15% of 80, they calculate 80 × 15 = 1200 instead of 80 × 0.15 = 12. This shows incomplete understanding of percentage-to-decimal conversion.

misconceptions.label: Confusing percentage of an amount with percentage change or increase
misconceptions.description: Student adds the percentage to the original amount, treating it as a percentage increase problem. For example, when asked 'Find 25% of 80', they calculate 80 + (80 × 0.25) = 100 instead of 80 × 0.25 = 20. This reflects unclear distinction between 'percentage of' and 'percentage more than'.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect decimal placement from calculator result
misconceptions.description: Student obtains the correct numerical result from the calculator but misinterprets decimal position. For example, calculator shows 24 for 20% of 120, but student writes 2.4 or 240, often due to not checking whether the result is sensible relative to the original amount.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Finding common percentages (10%, 25%, 50%) where mental conversion to decimal is straightforward
difficultyDimensions.hard: Finding less familiar percentages (7%, 34%, 87%) requiring reliable decimal conversion and calculator reliance

difficultyDimensions.easy: Round whole numbers (e.g. 20% of 100) where calculator display is unambiguous and reasonable-ness check is easy
difficultyDimensions.hard: Decimal amounts or very large/very small numbers (e.g. 15% of 2.8 or 6% of 50000) requiring careful interpretation of calculator output and magnitude awareness

difficultyDimensions.easy: Isolated numerical task with no need to interpret result in real-world terms
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multi-step problem where percentage is one operation and result must be used further or evaluated for reasonableness in context (e.g. discount, tax, growth)

transferContexts.context: Financial contexts involving discounts, sales, and tax
transferContexts.example: A jacket costs £65. It is reduced by 30% in a sale. Use a calculator to find the reduction amount, then work out the new sale price.
A school has 480 students. 35% of them take part in after-school clubs. Use a calculator to find how many students this is.
A company's revenue was £2.5 million. It increased by 18% the following year. Calculate the increase in revenue using a calculator, then find the new total revenue.

generativeContext: N6.10 addresses the core procedural skill of using a calculator to find a percentage of an amount. The canonical method is: convert percentage to decimal by dividing by 100, then multiply the amount by this decimal. Key error patterns: (1) reversing operations (dividing by percentage instead of multiplying by decimal), (2) not converting percentage to decimal (multiplying by percentage value directly), (3) confusing 'percentage of' with 'percentage more than' (addition instead of multiplication alone), (4) correct calculation but misreading or misplacing the decimal in the calculator display. Difficulty varies by percentage commonality (10%, 50% vs 7%, 34%), amount type (integers vs decimals, magnitude), and whether reasonableness checking is required. Mastery means reliable conversion, correct calculator entry, and ability to sanity-check output. Transfer occurs when finding parts of wholes in finance (discounts, tax, commissions), population/resource allocation, and data scaling. Use varied percentage values (not just multiples of 10) to ensure procedural understanding rather than pattern-matching. Include problems where students must interpret a multi-decimal result sensibly and problems requiring two-step reasoning (find percentage, then use in further context).

name: Express one quantity as a percentage of another
masteryDefinition: Students can fluently convert a ratio of two quantities into a percentage by forming the fraction (part/whole) and converting to a percentage out of 100. They can work with quantities in the same or different units, recognize when a quantity exceeds 100%, and justify their method using proportional reasoning.

misconceptions.label: Inverted fraction (part ÷ whole vs whole ÷ part)
misconceptions.description: Student divides the whole by the part instead of the part by the whole, then converts to a percentage. For example, when expressing 15 as a percentage of 60, they calculate 60 ÷ 15 = 4, then 4 × 100 = 400%, rather than 15 ÷ 60 = 0.25, then 0.25 × 100 = 25%.

misconceptions.label: Confusion between percentage of and percentage increase
misconceptions.description: Student treats 'express X as a percentage of Y' as a percentage change problem. For instance, given 'express 18 as a percentage of 20', they calculate (20 − 18)/20 × 100 = 10%, treating it as a decrease, rather than 18/20 × 100 = 90%.

misconceptions.label: Fails to convert fraction to percentage correctly
misconceptions.description: Student correctly forms the fraction (part/whole) but makes errors in converting to a percentage—either forgetting to multiply by 100, multiplying by 100 but placing the decimal incorrectly, or adding rather than multiplying. For example, 3/8 = 0.375 becomes 37.5 instead of 37.5%, or they write 0.375 + 100 = 100.375.

misconceptions.label: Ignores unit conversion when quantities have different units
misconceptions.description: Student attempts to form a fraction without first converting both quantities to the same unit. For example, expressing 300 g as a percentage of 2 kg, they calculate 300/2 × 100 = 15000% rather than converting 2 kg to 2000 g first, then 300/2000 × 100 = 15%.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Magnitude of the part relative to whole
difficultyDimensions.easy: Part is significantly smaller than whole (e.g. 12 out of 50); result is a 'natural' percentage like 24%
difficultyDimensions.hard: Part is close to or exceeds whole (e.g. 48 out of 50, or 85 out of 50); result requires recognition of percentages near 100% or exceeding 100%

difficultyDimensions.easy: Fraction converts easily (e.g. 1/4, 1/5, 3/10) or the denominator is a factor of 100
difficultyDimensions.hard: Fraction requires long division or rounding (e.g. 7/15, 11/13); result is a recurring decimal or non-terminating percentage

difficultyDimensions.easy: Both quantities are in the same unit already (e.g. both in cm, both in grams, both in pounds)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Quantities are in different units requiring conversion (e.g. expressing 450 m as a percentage of 2 km, or 350 mL as a percentage of 1.5 L)

transferContexts.context: Real-world contexts involving test scores, survey data, or resource allocation
transferContexts.example: In a class of 28 students, 21 passed the exam. Express 21 as a percentage of 28. (Alternative: A survey asked 150 people; 102 said yes. What percentage said yes?)

transferContexts.context: Measurement and scaling contexts where students compare actual measurements to targets or limits
transferContexts.example: A recipe calls for 400 g of flour. If you only have 320 g, express what you have as a percentage of what the recipe requires.

transferContexts.context: Contexts involving change or efficiency, where students express achieved values against potential or target values
transferContexts.example: A water tank has a capacity of 150 litres and currently holds 96 litres. What percentage of the tank is full?

generativeContext: Students at N6.11 need to understand percentage as a standardized ratio expressed out of 100. Core procedural knowledge: form the fraction (part ÷ whole), then multiply by 100 or convert to a decimal first and multiply. Emphasize the language: 'express X as a percentage of Y' means X is the numerator (part), Y is the denominator (whole). Key conceptual point: percentage is a way of comparing quantities by standardizing the whole to 100. Watch for: inverted fractions (very common error), forgetting to multiply by 100, and failing to handle mixed units. When quantities are in different units (e.g. 450 m and 2 km), require explicit unit conversion before forming the fraction. Extend to percentages exceeding 100% (when part > whole) and link to ratio and proportion work. Use visual representations (bar models, grids marked to 100) to reinforce part–whole relationships. Avoid language that conflates 'percentage of' with 'percentage increase/decrease'—these are distinct operations.

name: Percentage increase and decrease
masteryDefinition: Students can calculate percentage increases and decreases by finding the percentage of an original amount and adding to or subtracting from it. They understand that a percentage change is always relative to the original amount, can work backwards from a final amount to find the original, and can apply this to real-world contexts such as discounts, price rises, and compound percentage changes.

misconceptions.label: Treating percentage increase and decrease as commutative
misconceptions.description: Students believe that a 10% increase followed by a 10% decrease returns to the original amount. They fail to recognise that the second percentage is applied to a different base amount.

misconceptions.label: Adding or subtracting the percentage value directly instead of calculating the percentage of the original
misconceptions.description: When asked to increase £50 by 20%, students calculate 50 + 20 = 70 instead of finding 20% of 50 and adding it. They confuse the percentage symbol with a direct additive operation.

misconceptions.label: Using the final amount as the base for reverse percentage calculations
misconceptions.description: When finding the original amount after a percentage change (e.g., 'A price is now £60 after a 20% increase; find the original'), students calculate 20% of 60 and subtract, rather than recognising they need to work backwards from 120% of the original.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring the sign when calculating percentage change from a decrease
misconceptions.description: Students calculate the percentage change from 80 to 60 as +25% instead of -25%, treating all percentage changes as increases or losing track of whether the quantity grew or shrank.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Multiplier is a simple decimal (e.g. ×1.1 for +10%) or percentage is a multiple of 10 (10%, 20%, 50%)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiplier requires rounding or percentage is non-standard (e.g. +3.5%, +17%) and must be converted accurately

difficultyDimensions.easy: Forward calculation: given original amount and percentage change, find new amount
difficultyDimensions.hard: Reverse calculation: given final amount and percentage change, find original amount or identify the percentage change from two amounts

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single percentage increase or decrease applied once
difficultyDimensions.hard: Compound changes (e.g. increase by 10% then decrease by 15%, or successive annual increases) requiring multiple multiplication steps

transferContexts.context: Financial contexts with realistic price and discount scenarios
transferContexts.example: A shop reduces a coat from £80 to £60. By what percentage has the price been reduced? OR A savings account earns 3.5% interest per year. If I start with £200, what will I have after one year?
A town's population increases from 15,000 to 18,000. Express this as a percentage increase. OR A recipe calls for 400g flour, but I want to make 125% of the recipe. How much flour do I need?
A car depreciates by 15% each year. If it was worth £12,000 new, what is it worth after one year? After two years?

generativeContext: Percentage increase and decrease requires students to recognise that a percentage change is always relative to the original (or base) amount, not an absolute value. The core operation is: New Amount = Original Amount × (1 ± percentage/100). For a decrease, the multiplier is less than 1; for an increase, greater than 1. Reverse problems demand students divide by the multiplier or set up an equation: Final Amount = Original × multiplier, then solve for Original. Compound changes multiply successive multipliers: e.g. increase by 10% then decrease by 15% means ×1.1 × 0.85 = ×0.935. Real-world contexts—discounts, price rises, interest, depreciation, population change—anchor understanding. Common errors stem from: (1) treating changes as commutative (10% up, 10% down ≠ unchanged), (2) confusing percentage value with percentage of the amount (adding 20 instead of 20% of the amount), (3) using the wrong base in reverse calculations, and (4) losing track of direction (increase vs. decrease). Scaffold by separating the percentage-finding step from the addition/subtraction step, use multiplier notation explicitly, and contrast forward vs. reverse problems side-by-side. Emphasise that 'percent' literally means 'per 100' and that the multiplier method is faster and less error-prone than finding the percentage and then adding/subtracting separately.

name: Find the original value after a percentage change (reverse percentages)
masteryDefinition: Students can correctly identify and calculate the original value when given a final value after a percentage increase or decrease. This requires understanding that the final value represents a percentage of the original (e.g., 120% or 80% of the original), setting up the correct equation, and solving for the unknown original value. Students should be able to work fluently with multipliers (0.8, 1.2, etc.) and use division or algebraic methods to reverse the percentage change.

misconceptions.label: Applying the percentage change instead of reversing it
misconceptions.description: Students calculate the percentage change forwards from the given final value instead of backwards from it. For example, given 'a jacket costs £84 after a 30% increase', they calculate 84 × 1.3 = 109.2 instead of 84 ÷ 1.3 = ~64.62. They confuse the direction of the operation.

misconceptions.label: Using the percentage directly as a divisor or subtracting from 100% incorrectly
misconceptions.description: Students treat the percentage as if it can be used directly in division (e.g., dividing by 30 instead of by 1.3, or calculating 100 - 30 = 70 and using 0.7 when they should use 1.3). They fail to construct the correct multiplier that represents the relationship between original and final.

misconceptions.label: Confusing percentage increase and percentage decrease multipliers
misconceptions.description: Students use 0.7 when they should use 1.3, or vice versa. For instance, given 'after a 30% increase the value is 78', they might calculate 78 ÷ 0.7 instead of 78 ÷ 1.3. They incorrectly identify whether the multiplier should be greater than or less than 1.

misconceptions.label: Applying percentage to the final value instead of the original
misconceptions.description: Students calculate the percentage of the final value rather than understanding that the final value is already a percentage of the original. For example, given 'after a 20% increase the value is £60', they calculate 20% of 60 (= 12) and add or subtract it, rather than recognising 60 = 1.2 × original.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Percentage increase only (multiplier > 1, e.g. 120%, 150%)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Mixed increases and decreases, or decreases only (multiplier < 1, e.g. 75%, 60%); requires careful attention to which direction the change went

difficultyDimensions.easy: Simple multipliers like 1.2, 0.8, 1.5, 0.5 or percentage changes of 20%, 50%
difficultyDimensions.hard: Non-integer percentage changes like 15%, 33%, 8%; multipliers like 1.15, 0.85, 1.08 requiring careful division

difficultyDimensions.easy: Pure numerical problems with exact answers; no rounding required
difficultyDimensions.hard: Real-world contexts (prices, populations, wages) with non-terminating decimals requiring rounding decisions

transferContexts.context: Financial problems (sale discounts, price increases, VAT)
transferContexts.example: A book was sold in a sale for £12 after a 40% discount. What was the original price?
A town's population is now 24,500 after a 2% increase over the past year. What was the population last year?
After a 5% pay rise, an employee earns £21,000. What was their salary before the rise?

generativeContext: Reverse percentages (finding the original value) is a critical inverse operation that requires students to recognise the multiplier relationship between original and final values. The final value is always equal to the original value multiplied by a multiplier: Final = Original × Multiplier, so Original = Final ÷ Multiplier. For a percentage increase of p%, the multiplier is (100 + p)/100; for a decrease of p%, it is (100 - p)/100. Students must set up and solve this division correctly, distinguishing between increase (multiplier > 1) and decrease (multiplier < 1). Common errors include: reversing the operation (multiplying instead of dividing), miscalculating the multiplier (using 30 instead of 1.3 or 0.7), confusing increase/decrease directions, or treating the percentage as if it applies to the final value. Mastery requires fluency with decimal multipliers, secure understanding of the inverse relationship, and careful attention to problem context. Real-world applications demand sensitivity to rounding and reasonableness checks.

name: Repeated percentage change (compound interest, depreciation)
masteryDefinition: Students can calculate the result of applying the same percentage change multiple times to an initial amount, using either the multiplier method (as a power) or iterative application. They understand that compound percentage change is non-linear and can distinguish between simple and compound scenarios. They can solve problems involving compound interest, depreciation, population growth, and exponential decay across at least 2–3 applications, and can work backwards from a final amount to find the initial amount or rate of change.

misconceptions.label: Adding percentages instead of multiplying
misconceptions.description: Students add the percentage change each year rather than applying it multiplicatively. For example, for 10% growth over 3 years, they calculate 100 + 10 + 10 + 10 = 130 instead of 100 × 1.1³ = 133.1. This leads to linear rather than exponential growth.

misconceptions.label: Incorrect multiplier calculation from percentage
misconceptions.description: Students make errors converting the percentage to a multiplier. Common errors include using 0.1 instead of 1.1 for a 10% increase, or using 1.10 instead of 0.90 for a 10% decrease. This fundamentally breaks the calculation.

misconceptions.label: Confusion between compound and simple interest
misconceptions.description: Students apply simple interest logic (calculating interest on the original amount only) to compound interest problems, especially when percentages are stated as annual rates. They calculate I = PRT rather than A = P(1+r)^n, leading to undercalculation of compound returns.

misconceptions.label: Misinterpreting negative percentage change as negative multiplier
misconceptions.description: When depreciation or decay occurs, students write the multiplier as negative (e.g., 100 × (–0.9)³ for 10% annual depreciation) instead of positive less-than-one (100 × 0.9³). This produces nonsensical alternating signs in the result.

difficultyDimensions.easy: 2 time periods (e.g., 2 years of compound interest)
difficultyDimensions.hard: 5+ time periods, or fractional periods (e.g., quarterly compounding over 3 years)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Simple percentages (5%, 10%, 20%) with clear direction (all growth or all decay)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Non-integer percentages (3.5%), mixed scenarios (growth then decay), or very small rates (0.5%)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Given initial amount and rate, find final amount after n periods
difficultyDimensions.hard: Work backwards (given final amount, find initial or rate), or optimisation (compare two schemes over time)

transferContexts.example: A bank account offers 2.5% annual compound interest. If you deposit £500, how much will you have after 5 years?
A car depreciates by 15% each year. If its initial value is £12,000, what is its value after 4 years?
A bacterial colony triples in size every hour. If it starts with 100 bacteria, how many will there be after 6 hours? After how many hours will it exceed 10,000?

generativeContext: N6.14 requires students to apply the same proportional change iteratively, building toward understanding exponential functions. The core concept is the multiplier method: if a quantity changes by p% per time period, after n periods the new amount is initial × (1 + r)ⁿ where r = p/100. For decrease (depreciation, decay), the multiplier is (1 – r). Key discriminators: (1) distinguishing iterative from additive change; (2) correctly constructing the multiplier from a percentage (especially sign and magnitude); (3) recognizing when to use negative exponents or reciprocals (e.g., reversing growth, or compounding at different frequencies); (4) understanding that compound growth is non-linear and leads to larger final amounts than simple interest. Stretch students by: asking them to work backwards (solve for initial amount or rate given final amount); compare two schemes (fixed vs. variable rates); handle mixed scenarios (growth then depreciation); or introduce fractional exponents (e.g., quarterly compounding). Misconceptions frequently arise from confusing additive and multiplicative logic, making sign errors in multipliers, and conflating compound with simple interest. Use visual comparisons (tables, graphs) of linear vs. exponential growth to anchor understanding.

name: Express one number as a fraction or percentage of another in context
masteryDefinition: Students can fluently identify the correct base (denominator/whole) when expressing one quantity as a fraction or percentage of another, correctly calculate the fraction or percentage in both abstract and contextualised problems, and interpret their answer in context. They recognise when a quantity should be expressed as a fraction versus percentage based on context, and can work backwards from a fraction or percentage to find an original or derived quantity.

misconceptions.label: Inverted base (denominator reversal)
misconceptions.description: Student writes the given number as numerator and the base number as denominator in the wrong order. For example, when asked 'What fraction of 20 is 5?', student calculates 20/5 instead of 5/20. This often occurs because students read the sentence left-to-right and use 'first number mentioned' as the numerator.

misconceptions.label: Confuses 'of' with multiplicative operation
misconceptions.description: Student multiplies instead of dividing when converting to a fraction or percentage. For example, when asked 'Express 8 as a fraction of 40', student calculates 8 × 40 = 320 instead of 8 ÷ 40 = 0.2. This stems from misinterpreting 'of' as a multiplication signal.

misconceptions.label: Percentage conversion calculation error
misconceptions.description: Student correctly identifies the fraction but fails to multiply by 100 (or divides by 100 instead) when converting to a percentage. For example, correctly finds 5/20 = 0.25 but states the answer as 0.25% instead of 25%, or states 25/100 = 25 and then writes 0.25%.

misconceptions.label: Ignores or misinterprets context units
misconceptions.description: Student calculates correctly but fails to apply the result meaningfully to the context, such as forgetting to express the answer relative to the specified base or mixing up different quantity types. For example, in 'What percentage of the 50 students passed?', student calculates a fraction but reports it as a whole number without the % symbol, or conflates two different populations in the comparison.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Base is a simple whole number (10, 20, 50) that easily relates to percentages or halves
difficultyDimensions.hard: Base is an awkward whole number (37, 84) or a decimal (2.5, 12.8) requiring mental arithmetic or extended division

difficultyDimensions.easy: Numerator is much smaller than denominator (3 out of 60), yielding a simple percentage or recurring decimal
difficultyDimensions.hard: Numerator is close to or greater than denominator (38 out of 40 or 45 out of 30), requiring conversion to percentage > 100% or improper fractions

difficultyDimensions.easy: Direct sentence structure: 'Express 6 as a fraction of 15'—explicit, unambiguous language
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multi-step narrative context: 'In a group of 32 Year 7 students, 12 play football. What percentage of the year group plays football?'—requires extraction of relevant numbers and identification of base

transferContexts.context: Real-world ratio and proportion problems in science or geography
transferContexts.example: A survey shows 18 out of 45 students prefer online learning. Express this as a fraction in simplest form and as a percentage. How does your answer help interpret the survey result?
A book costs £12 and is reduced by £3. Express the reduction as a percentage of the original price. If another book costing £15 has the same percentage reduction, what is its sale price?

In a class of 30 students, 22 completed homework. In another class of 25 students, 18 completed homework. Express each completion rate as a percentage and compare which class has the higher completion rate.

generativeContext: N6.15 requires students to identify and use the correct base (whole quantity) when expressing part-to-whole relationships. The skill bridges fractions, decimals, and percentages as equivalent representations. Key linguistic cues are 'as a fraction of', 'as a percentage of', 'out of'—these always signal that the second number is the base. Students must distinguish this from comparison ratios (e.g. 'the ratio of boys to girls'). Misconceptions cluster around base inversion, arithmetic operation (multiply vs. divide), and percentage conversion (missing ×100 step). Contextual problems require students to extract two quantities and identify which is part and which is whole—this extraction step is a major difficulty spike. Simplification of fractions should be automatic but not required unless explicitly requested; students should recognise 12/30 = 2/5 = 40%, but writing 12/30 = 40% is acceptable if the context permits. Percentage values above 100% arise naturally when part > whole (e.g. '150 out of 100 students passed an exam across retakes'). Use concrete scenarios (recipes, surveys, prices, sports statistics) to anchor understanding. Reverse questions ('If 35% is 14 students, how many total?') extend mastery and help diagnose whether the student understands the multiplicative structure, not just the surface procedure.

name: Use ratio notation; simplify ratios
masteryDefinition: Students can write ratios in correct notation (a:b), simplify ratios to lowest terms by finding the greatest common divisor, and recognise when ratios are equivalent. They can work with ratios expressed in different forms and apply simplification systematically across a variety of numerical contexts.

misconceptions.label: Confusing ratio simplification with subtraction
misconceptions.description: Students subtract the same number from both parts instead of dividing by a common factor. For example, simplifying 8:12 by subtracting 2 to get 6:10, rather than dividing both by 4 to get 2:3.

misconceptions.label: Stopping simplification prematurely
misconceptions.description: Students simplify a ratio once and believe it is in simplest form, even when a common factor remains. For example, leaving 6:9 as it is after dividing by 3, without recognising that 6:9 can be simplified further to 2:3.

misconceptions.label: Treating ratio as fraction and reducing incorrectly
misconceptions.description: Students cancel individual digits rather than finding the GCD of both numbers. For example, simplifying 15:25 by cancelling the 5s to get 1:2, instead of dividing both by 5 to get 3:5.

misconceptions.label: Applying different operations to each part
misconceptions.description: Students divide one part and multiply the other, or apply different factors to each number. For example, attempting to simplify 10:15 by dividing the first by 5 and the second by 3, resulting in an incorrect ratio.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single-digit numbers with obvious common factors (e.g. 2:4, 3:6)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Large two- or three-digit numbers with less obvious GCDs (e.g. 48:72, 105:175)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Ratios requiring one division step to reach simplest form (e.g. 6:8 → 3:4)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Ratios requiring multiple divisions or recognition of less obvious factors (e.g. 12:18 → 6:9 → 2:3)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Ratios with clearly recognisable common factors (even numbers, multiples of 5 or 10)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Ratios with less obvious common factors requiring systematic factor-finding (e.g. 14:35, where GCD is 7)

transferContexts.context: Real-world ratio scenarios involving ingredient quantities or mixing
transferContexts.example: A recipe uses 150g flour to 100g sugar. Simplify the ratio and use it to scale the recipe for different batch sizes.

transferContexts.context: Comparing quantities and identifying equivalent ratios
transferContexts.example: A shop sells two packs: Pack A has 6 apples and 9 oranges; Pack B has 8 apples and 12 oranges. Simplify each ratio and determine which pack has the same ratio of apples to oranges.

transferContexts.context: Expressing scale ratios in maps and models
transferContexts.example: A map scale is given as 20cm : 500m. Simplify this ratio to express how many metres are represented by 1cm on the map.

generativeContext: N6.16 focuses on ratio notation and simplification—core procedural skills that bridge arithmetic and proportional reasoning. Students must understand that a ratio a:b represents a multiplicative relationship, and simplification preserves this relationship by dividing both parts by their GCD. Key cognitive demands: identifying common factors efficiently (especially for larger numbers), distinguishing simplification (divide both by same number) from other operations, and recognising when a ratio is fully simplified. Common errors cluster around procedural confusion (subtraction vs. division), incomplete simplification, and digit-level cancellation borrowed incorrectly from fraction work. Ratio simplification is foundational for equivalent ratios (N6.17), scaling (N6.18), and later proportion and similarity work. Vary problems systematically across number size and factor obviousness; explicitly contrast simplification with other operations; use visual or list-based factor-finding strategies to build systematic GCD identification. Real-world contexts (recipes, maps, pack comparisons) anchor abstract ratio work and support transfer to proportional reasoning tasks.

name: Share a quantity in a given ratio
masteryDefinition: Students can divide a total quantity into parts according to a given ratio by: (1) finding the total number of ratio parts, (2) calculating the value of one part by division, (3) multiplying to find each share. They can work with ratios expressed as a:b or a:b:c, handle both whole number and fractional shares, and verify their answer sums to the original quantity.

misconceptions.label: Treating ratio parts as individual amounts rather than scale factors
misconceptions.description: Student interprets a 3:2 ratio as 'give 3 to one person and 2 to another' rather than 'divide into 5 equal parts, then give 3 parts to one and 2 parts to the other'. For example, sharing £50 in ratio 3:2, they write 3 + 2 = £5 total instead of calculating (50÷5)×3 and (50÷5)×2.

misconceptions.label: Adding ratio numbers directly to the total instead of using them as part indicators
misconceptions.description: When sharing £60 in ratio 2:3:5, student calculates 2+3+5 = 10, then incorrectly gives shares as £2, £3, and £5 instead of recognising these as part counts and finding (60÷10)×2, (60÷10)×3, (60÷10)×5.

misconceptions.label: Dividing the total by only one ratio number rather than the sum of all ratio parts
misconceptions.description: For sharing £80 in ratio 3:5, student calculates 80÷3 or 80÷5 and stops, rather than first finding 3+5=8 parts, then dividing 80÷8 to get the unit value. Results in incorrect share sizes that don't sum to the original total.

misconceptions.label: Confusing ratio notation with fractions of the whole
misconceptions.description: Student interprets ratio 1:3 as 'one-third' rather than 'one part out of four total parts'. When sharing 400ml in ratio 1:3, they calculate 400÷3 for the first share instead of 400÷4 for the first share, leading to shares that sum to more than 400ml.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of parts in the ratio
difficultyDimensions.easy: Two-part ratios (a:b, e.g. 2:3)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Three or four-part ratios (a:b:c:d, e.g. 2:3:1:4)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Total is a multiple of the sum of ratio parts (e.g. share £60 in 2:3; sum=5, and 60÷5 is a whole number)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Total does not divide evenly or requires fractional/decimal shares (e.g. share £50 in 2:3; sum=5, giving shares of £20 and £30, or share £75 in 2:4:3 requiring £13.64... type reasoning)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Small integers or simple whole-number quantities (share 20 sweets in 1:4)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Large numbers, decimals, or metric quantities requiring multi-step calculation (share 2400ml in ratio 5:7, or £156.50 in ratio 3:5)

transferContexts.context: Sharing money in proportion to contributions or ownership
transferContexts.example: Three friends contribute money in the ratio 4:5:6 to buy a gift costing £45. How much does each person pay?

transferContexts.context: Dividing ingredients or materials according to recipe or mixture ratios
transferContexts.example: A concrete mix requires cement, sand, and gravel in the ratio 1:3:5. If you need 180kg of concrete, how much of each material is needed?

transferContexts.context: Allocating populations or resources across regions or groups proportionally
transferContexts.example: A school has 840 Year 7 students divided among three form groups in the ratio 4:4:3. How many students are in each form group?

generativeContext: N6.17 is the procedural application of ratio understanding to partition problems. The core task is: given a total quantity Q and a ratio a:b (or a:b:c...), find the individual shares. The student must: (1) sum all ratio parts to get the total number of equal divisions, (2) divide Q by this sum to find the unit value (one part), (3) multiply this unit value by each ratio component to get each share, (4) verify all shares sum to Q. Key learning progression: students often confuse 'parts in a ratio' with 'amounts to give directly'—this is the fundamental misconception. Emphasise the intermediate step of finding 'one part' explicitly. Ratio simplification is not required for this skill, but check whether the ratio is already in simplest form or not. Contextual framing matters: money, ingredients, and group allocations make the partitioning intuitive. For three-part and four-part ratios, tracking working clearly in a table format (Ratio | Parts | Unit Value | Share) prevents errors. Fractional or decimal shares arise naturally when Q is not a perfect multiple of the sum of parts—model this with confidence. Common validation: ask 'Do all the shares add up to the total?' as a routine check.

name: Use the unitary method for ratio and proportion problems
masteryDefinition: Students can identify when the unitary method is appropriate, find the value of one unit by division, and scale up or down to find the required quantity in direct proportion problems. They can apply this systematically to solve real-world problems involving ratios, unit rates, and proportional relationships, and can verify their answers make sense in context.

misconceptions.label: Dividing by the wrong number when finding the unit value
misconceptions.description: When finding the unitary value (the amount for 1 unit), students divide the known total by the number of items instead of correctly identifying which quantity divides by which. For example, given '5 pens cost £3', writing 5 ÷ 3 = 1.67 instead of 3 ÷ 5 = 0.60, reversing the division and producing an answer in wrong units or magnitude.

misconceptions.label: Multiplying when scaling should divide (or vice versa)
misconceptions.description: Students correctly find the unit value but then apply the wrong operation when scaling to the required quantity. For instance, finding cost per item correctly (£0.60) but then multiplying by 8 when the problem asks 'how many items for £4.80', resulting in £4.80 × 0.60 instead of 4.80 ÷ 0.60.

misconceptions.label: Confusing ratio notation with fractional shares
misconceptions.description: When given a ratio like 3:2, students treat it as though one part is 3/5 of the whole and another is 2/5, rather than recognising 3 and 2 as separate comparable quantities. This leads to finding the unit value by dividing by the total (3+2) when only one part should be divided.

misconceptions.label: Forgetting to maintain consistent units throughout
misconceptions.description: Students apply the unitary method mechanically but fail to check that units remain consistent or sensible. For example, finding cost per kg correctly but then forgetting to convert mass from grams to kg before multiplying, or mixing currencies without conversion, leading to dimensionally incorrect answers.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single-step scaling: given 'cost for 5 items', find 'cost for 10 items'
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multi-step with unit conversion: given 'cost for 250g', find 'cost for 2kg', requiring conversion first

difficultyDimensions.easy: Unitary value is a whole number or simple decimal (e.g. 2 or 0.5)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Unitary value is a non-terminating decimal or requires rounding (e.g. £0.33 per item from 3 items for £1)

difficultyDimensions.easy: One-to-one matching: given information directly relates to one item or unit (e.g. 'cost for 1 hour')
difficultyDimensions.hard: Compound contexts requiring inference: ratios between different quantities where the unit is implicit (e.g. mixing paints in ratio 2:3, then scaling to a total amount)

transferContexts.example: A cake recipe uses 200g flour for 4 servings. How much flour is needed for 10 servings? Use the unitary method to find flour per serving, then scale up.

transferContexts.context: Currency exchange and unit rates in shopping
transferContexts.example: A shop sells oranges at 5 for £1.20. How much would 8 oranges cost? Find the cost per orange, then multiply.
A car travels 180 km in 3 hours at constant speed. How far will it travel in 5 hours? Use the unitary method to find distance per hour.

generativeContext: The unitary method is a fundamental proportional reasoning technique that reduces a multiplicative relationship to a single-unit value, making scaling transparent and auditable. In ratio problems, students must first recognise the context: is it a simple rate (cost per item, distance per hour) or a ratio comparison (parts to parts or parts to whole)? For simple rates, identify what quantity corresponds to 1 unit of another (e.g. cost ÷ items = cost per item), then multiply or divide by the required quantity. For ratios, clarify whether the problem is finding one part (given a ratio and total), or scaling all parts equally. Critical error points: reversing division when finding the unit value (often stemming from weak proportional reasoning), applying wrong operations during scaling, conflating ratio parts with fractional shares, and neglecting unit conversions. Instruction should emphasise the structure: [1] identify what '1 unit' means in the problem, [2] calculate the unit value (state the operation and units clearly), [3] scale to the required quantity (check direction: more items → multiply, fewer items → divide), [4] sense-check magnitude and units. Use bar models and ratio tables to make the unitary value and scaling steps visible. Connect to real-world contexts (cooking, shopping, travel) where proportional reasoning is intuitive but execution is error-prone.

name: Convert between fractions, decimals and percentages (including recurring)
masteryDefinition: Students can fluently convert between fractions, decimals and percentages in both directions, including recognition and notation of recurring decimals (e.g. 0.3̇ for 1/3). They understand the equivalence between these representations, can justify conversions using place value and multiplicative reasoning, and can work with mixed numbers and improper fractions in all three forms.

misconceptions.label: Confusing the position of the decimal point when converting percentages to decimals
misconceptions.description: Students divide by 10 instead of 100, writing 35% as 0.35 correctly but then treating 120% as 12.0 instead of 1.2, or writing 5% as 0.05 when they meant 0.5. Often stems from misremembering the rule or treating % as 'move decimal one place' rather than 'divide by 100'.

misconceptions.label: Treating recurring decimals as terminating or misrepresenting their notation
misconceptions.description: Students write 1/3 as 0.33 and assume it terminates, or confuse 0.3̇ (0.333...) with 0.3. They may also incorrectly round 0.333... to 0.33 and treat it as exact, leading to wrong percentage conversions (e.g., 0.33 = 33% instead of 33.3̇% or 33⅓%).

misconceptions.label: Inverting fraction-to-decimal conversion by dividing the denominator by the numerator
misconceptions.description: Students write 3/8 as 8÷3 instead of 3÷8, resulting in answers like 2.67 instead of 0.375. This reflects a fundamental confusion about the meaning of the fraction bar as division, often exacerbated when the numerator is smaller than the denominator.

misconceptions.label: Adding or concatenating digits when converting fractions to percentages
misconceptions.description: Students convert 3/5 to 0.6, then write 60% as '0.6%' or write the digits separately as '06%' instead of recognising 0.6 = 60%. Or they multiply by 10 instead of 100, writing 3/5 as 6% instead of 60%.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Complexity of the fraction (denominator size and relationship to powers of 10)
difficultyDimensions.easy: Fractions with denominators 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 100 (convert exactly to terminating decimals)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Fractions with denominators 3, 6, 7, 8, 9, 11 that produce non-terminating or recurring decimals; fractions where the denominator doesn't divide evenly into powers of 10

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Type of starting representation and direction of conversion
difficultyDimensions.easy: Converting percentages to decimals; converting terminating decimals to fractions in simplest form
difficultyDimensions.hard: Converting recurring decimals to fractions; converting fractions to percentages; working with mixed numbers or improper fractions across all three forms

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Magnitude and context (proper, improper, greater than 100%)
difficultyDimensions.easy: Proper fractions and percentages between 0% and 100%
difficultyDimensions.hard: Improper fractions, percentages over 100%, and fractions less than 1% (e.g., 0.5%, 1/200)

transferContexts.example: A fair coin is flipped 4 times and lands on heads 3 times. Express the relative frequency as a fraction, decimal and percentage. Then identify which representation makes it easiest to compare with the theoretical probability of 0.5.

A shop offers 1/3 off an item originally priced at £60. Calculate the discount as a decimal, as a percentage, and the final price. Justify why expressing the discount as a percentage helps the customer understand the saving.

transferContexts.context: Ratio and proportion in real-world scenarios
transferContexts.example: In a survey, 7 out of 20 students prefer digital learning. Express this as a fraction, decimal and percentage. Use your answer to predict how many students in a year group of 240 would prefer digital learning.

generativeContext: N6.19 requires secure understanding of the equivalence and interchangeability of fractions, decimals and percentages. The core mathematical structure involves: (1) fraction-to-decimal via division (numerator ÷ denominator); (2) decimal-to-percentage via multiplying by 100 or shifting the decimal point; (3) fraction-to-percentage via the chain fraction → decimal → percentage or via equivalence (e.g., 3/5 = 60/100 = 60%); (4) recognition of recurring decimals and their notation (0.3̇, 0.16̇, 0.142857̇). Key misconceptions centre on procedural errors (wrong divisor, decimal place confusion) and conceptual gaps (not recognising recurring patterns, inverting division). Difficulty scales with denominator complexity (denominators not factors of powers of 10 require long division and demand fluency with recurring notation) and with working across mixed numbers or improper fractions. Students must also develop the metacognitive habit of choosing the most efficient representation for a given context (e.g., 1/3 is clearest as a fraction or recurring decimal; 50% is clearest as a percentage or decimal). Emphasise the multiplicative structure: × 100 links decimal to percentage; ÷ 100 does the reverse. Use bar models, number lines and place-value grids to reinforce the magnitude relationships, especially for fractions less than 1 and percentages over 100.

name: Solve problems involving FDP in context (best buy, discounts, profit/loss)
masteryDefinition: Students can solve multi-step word problems involving fractions, decimals and percentages in realistic contexts including best buy comparisons (unit pricing, value for money), discounts (calculating sale price, finding original price), and profit/loss (markup, margin, break-even). They select appropriate representations (fraction, decimal, percentage) for the context, perform calculations accurately, and interpret their answer in context with correct units and reasonableness checking.

misconceptions.label: Confusing discount amount with sale price
misconceptions.description: Student calculates the discount correctly (e.g. 20% of £50 = £10) but then states the answer as £10 instead of recognising this is the reduction and the sale price is £40. Often seen when students say 'the discount is £40' instead of 'the price after discount is £40'.

misconceptions.label: Applying percentage changes in wrong direction or order
misconceptions.description: In profit/loss problems, student calculates profit as cost price + percentage of selling price, rather than percentage of cost price. Example: cost £100, mark up 20% on selling price instead of cost price, leading to £120 ÷ 1.2 confusion. Also: applying successive discounts multiplicatively when they should be sequential, or applying second discount to original price instead of reduced price.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring units or context in best buy decisions
misconceptions.description: Student correctly calculates unit prices (e.g. price per 100g) but then compares them without converting to same units, or selects the option with the smaller number without considering what that number represents. Example: comparing £0.25 per 100g with £1.50 per kg and choosing £1.50 because it's a 'bigger' number.

misconceptions.label: Treating profit margin and markup as identical
misconceptions.description: Student confuses profit margin (profit as percentage of cost price) with markup (profit as percentage of selling price), using the wrong denominator. Example: calculating 20% profit on cost price of £100 as 20% of £120 (selling price) giving £24, rather than 20% of £100 = £20.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single-step context problems (e.g. find discount on one item) or pre-identified what to calculate
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multi-step problems requiring extraction of relevant information, intermediate calculations, and interpretation (e.g. compare two best buys involving unit conversion, then apply a discount to the cheaper option)

difficultyDimensions.easy: All values given in same form (all percentages, all fractions); or only one representation needed
difficultyDimensions.hard: Mixed inputs (some prices as decimals, some comparisons as fractions or percentages) requiring conversion between FDP representations to solve

difficultyDimensions.easy: Concrete, familiar scenarios with clear quantities (e.g. price tags, simple discount labels)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Abstract or business-oriented language (e.g. 'margin', 'break-even', 'cost price' vs 'selling price' distinction unclear from phrasing, or embedded in longer narrative)

transferContexts.context: Real-world consumer decisions and value for money
transferContexts.example: A supermarket sells 500g of cereal for £3.50 or 750g for £4.80. Which is better value? If there's a 15% discount on the 750g box this week, which option should a shopper choose?

transferContexts.context: Business/enterprise contexts (small business profit and pricing)
transferContexts.example: A craft maker buys materials for £8 per item. She wants to make a 40% profit on cost price. A shop offers to stock her items but takes a 25% cut of the selling price. What must her selling price be to achieve her profit target?
A worker earns £2000 per month. She gets a 5% pay rise, then is offered a new role at £2150 per month. Which is better financially? By what percentage is the new offer better than her current salary?

generativeContext: N6.20 is the capstone FDP skill requiring integrated fluency across fractions, decimals and percentages applied to realistic scenarios. Mastery means solving contextual problems where students must: (1) identify what calculation is needed from a word problem; (2) choose or convert between FDP representations strategically; (3) perform multi-step calculations (often involving repeated proportional reasoning); (4) interpret and validate the numerical answer within the problem context. Key contexts are best buy (comparing value per unit, unit conversion), discounts (finding sale price or original price, chaining discounts), and profit/loss (markup on cost, margin on revenue, break-even). Common errors cluster around direction of percentage change (discount/markup applied to wrong base), conflating related but distinct quantities (discount vs sale price, profit vs margin), and ignoring units or reasonableness. Difficulty scales with: number of intermediate steps, need to convert between FDP or units, abstractness of business language, and whether the quantity to find must be inferred. Generation should include realistic price points, mixed FDP inputs, and scenarios requiring decision-making or comparison, not just mechanical calculation. Validate answers make sense in context (e.g. sale price < original price, profit > 0 for viable business).

name: Use the vocabulary of probability and the probability scale
masteryDefinition: Students can correctly identify and use probability vocabulary (certain, likely, unlikely, impossible, even chance) and understand that probability can be represented on a 0–1 scale where 0 = impossible and 1 = certain. They can position events on a probability scale, justify their placement, and distinguish between theoretical and experimental language when appropriate.

misconceptions.label: Confusing 'likely' with 'certain'
misconceptions.description: Students use 'likely' and 'certain' interchangeably, failing to recognise that 'likely' describes events with probability greater than 0.5 but less than 1, while 'certain' means probability = 1. Often seen when students say 'it's certain it will rain tomorrow' when they mean 'it's likely'.

misconceptions.label: Treating probability scale as a ranking rather than a measure
misconceptions.description: Students position events on a probability scale but do not understand that the distance from 0 or 1 has numerical meaning. They may place 'rolling a 6 on a die' and 'picking a red card from a standard deck' at the same position because both feel 'unlikely', without recognising the first has probability 1/6 and the second has probability 1/2.

misconceptions.label: Assuming all events on the probability scale apply equally to the future
misconceptions.description: Students struggle to distinguish between describing the likelihood of an event in general ('a fair coin has probability 0.5 of landing heads') and predicting the outcome of a specific upcoming trial. They may believe that if a coin landed heads 5 times, the next flip is 'now unlikely' to be heads, conflating frequency with changing probability.

misconceptions.label: Misplacing 'even chance' on the probability scale
misconceptions.description: Students do not reliably locate 'even chance' at 0.5 on the probability scale. Some place it at 0 (thinking 'even' means 'flat' or 'none'), others struggle to connect 'even chance' to the numerical value 0.5, treating it as a separate category rather than a specific position.

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Familiarity with the underlying random context
difficultyDimensions.easy: Coin flips, standard dice rolls, or named everyday scenarios (e.g. 'will it rain tomorrow')
difficultyDimensions.hard: Compound events, unfamiliar games, conditional scenarios (e.g. 'probability a student chosen at random speaks French given they speak Spanish')

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Number of probability words required in a single task
difficultyDimensions.easy: Single word identification ('Is this certain, likely, unlikely, or impossible?')
difficultyDimensions.hard: Multiple words with nuanced comparison ('Rank these four events from most likely to least likely and justify using probability language')

difficultyDimensions.dimension: Abstractness of the probability scale representation
difficultyDimensions.easy: Labelled probability scale (0 marked as 'impossible', 0.5 marked as 'even chance', 1 marked as 'certain')
difficultyDimensions.hard: Blank 0–1 number line with no labels; or verbal description without visual aid

transferContexts.context: Risk and decision-making in everyday life
transferContexts.example: A student is deciding whether to carry an umbrella. Explain why saying 'there is a 30% chance of rain' is more helpful than saying 'rain is unlikely' when making this decision.
A weather forecast states 'a 70% chance of snow tomorrow'. Use probability vocabulary to explain what this means and how it differs from saying 'snow is certain'.
A game involves drawing a card from a shuffled standard deck. A player claims 'the probability of drawing a heart is even chance'. Is this correct? Use the probability scale to explain your answer.

generativeContext: S1.1 introduces Year 7 students to the language and structure of probability as a foundation for quantitative reasoning about uncertainty. The skill has three tightly interlinked components: (1) vocabulary mastery—students must use 'impossible', 'unlikely', 'even chance', 'likely', and 'certain' with precision and consistency; (2) scale understanding—students must recognise that probability ranges from 0 to 1, that these values correspond to the vocabulary endpoints, and that intermediate values (e.g. 0.3, 0.7) have numerical meaning; (3) context sensitivity—students must apply these ideas to familiar random events (coins, dice, spinners) and everyday scenarios (weather, sport, games). Common errors centre on vocabulary confusion (conflating 'likely' with 'certain'), treating the scale as ordinal rather than continuous (failing to see that the position between 0 and 1 conveys magnitude), and misunderstanding the distinction between describing a process (a fair coin has probability 0.5 heads) and predicting a single outcome ('this flip will be heads'). Difficulty scales with context unfamiliarity, number of comparisons required, and degree of visual scaffolding. Effective generation should include concrete random events with transparent sample spaces (dice, cards, spinners), anchor events at 0, 0.5, and 1, and tasks requiring both placement and justification. Avoid abstract or compound probability scenarios; avoid language that conflates frequency with probability.

name: Understand that probabilities sum to 1 (using fractions, decimals and percentages)
masteryDefinition: Students can identify all mutually exclusive outcomes for a given scenario, assign probabilities to each using fractions, decimals, or percentages, and verify that the sum equals 1. They can convert between representations (e.g. 0.25 = 1/4 = 25%) and recognise when probabilities do not sum to 1 as indicating missing or overlapping outcomes.

misconceptions.label: Treating percentages as decimals without conversion
misconceptions.description: Students add probability values without converting to the same form, e.g. adding 25 (from 25%) + 0.5 + 1/4 and claiming the sum is 25.75 or 26.75, rather than first converting all to the same representation (0.25 + 0.5 + 0.25 = 1).

misconceptions.label: Confusing probability of an outcome with proportion of probability
misconceptions.description: When given one probability (e.g. P(red) = 0.6), students incorrectly assume the remaining probability must be split equally among other outcomes rather than understanding it represents the combined probability of all non-red outcomes. For example, if P(red) = 0.6 and there are 3 other colours, they assign 0.1 to each rather than 0.4 total to distribute.

misconceptions.label: Believing probabilities must sum to 100 (not 1)
misconceptions.description: Students confuse the rule that percentages sum to 100 with the rule that probabilities sum to 1, sometimes writing probabilities as 30, 40, 30 without recognising these should be 0.3, 0.4, 0.3 or 3/10, 4/10, 3/10.

misconceptions.label: Ignoring the possibility of impossible outcomes
misconceptions.description: When asked to find a missing probability, students assume all outcomes listed are possible and forget that an outcome with probability 0 (impossible) may exist. For example, in a spinner with sections summing to 1, they don't recognise that an unlabelled colour has probability 0.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Two outcomes (e.g. coin flip: heads/tails)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Five or more outcomes where some probabilities must be inferred or calculated (e.g. spinner with unlabelled sections or combined events)

difficultyDimensions.easy: All probabilities given in the same form (all decimals or all fractions with the same denominator)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Mixed representations (fractions with different denominators, decimals, and percentages all in one problem) requiring conversion before summing

difficultyDimensions.easy: Probabilities explicitly listed; student only verifies the sum
difficultyDimensions.hard: One or more probabilities missing; student must calculate missing value(s) using the constraint that the sum equals 1

transferContexts.context: Everyday probability contexts (games, weather, real-world events)
transferContexts.example: A weather forecast states the probability of rain is 30%, probability of cloud is 45%, and probability of sun is 20%. A student argues that these do not represent all possible weather outcomes for the day. How should the missing probability be distributed, and what does it represent?

A game uses a spinner divided into three sections with angles 90°, 135°, and unknown. Express the probabilities of landing on each section as fractions and decimals, and determine the angle of the missing section.
A survey of 200 students finds that 0.4 prefer pizza, 25% prefer pasta, and the rest prefer salad. Express each probability as a fraction and verify they sum to 1. How many students prefer salad?

generativeContext: This skill consolidates the fundamental principle that all possible outcomes of a random event partition the sample space with total probability 1. Students must work flexibly across three representational systems (fractions, decimals, percentages), recognising that 1, 1.0, and 100% are equivalent expressions of certainty. The skill underpins all subsequent probability work, including conditional probability and theoretical vs. experimental probability. Key challenges: (1) converting between representations accurately before combining; (2) recognising that if some outcomes are listed, the sum of their probabilities is the total probability of those listed outcomes—and the complement (probability of NOT those outcomes) must be calculated to complete the sample space to 1; (3) interpreting missing or given probabilities contextually (e.g. distinguishing between 'not mentioned' and 'impossible'). Use concrete contexts (spinners, dice, drawn objects, event categories) to anchor abstract reasoning. Probe understanding by asking 'What outcomes have we not accounted for?' and 'Why must the total equal exactly 1?'

name: Calculate the probability of a single event
masteryDefinition: Students can calculate the probability of a single event using the formula P(event) = number of favourable outcomes / total number of possible outcomes. They can express probabilities as fractions, decimals, and percentages; identify when events are equally likely; and recognise that probabilities lie between 0 and 1 inclusive. They can work with both theoretical (fair) and experimental (observed frequency) probabilities, and justify their reasoning about likelihood.

misconceptions.label: Denominator confusion in theoretical probability
misconceptions.description: Students count only the favourable outcomes as the denominator, or use an incorrect total. For example, when finding P(rolling a 3 on a fair die), they write 3/6 as the answer but believe the denominator should be 3, or they count overlapping outcomes. Common error: P(red card from a deck) = 26/52 but student writes 26/26 or 52/26.

misconceptions.label: Confusing experimental and theoretical probability
misconceptions.description: Students treat experimental (observed) frequencies as if they must equal theoretical probabilities, or vice versa. For example, after 10 coin flips showing 7 heads, they claim P(heads) = 0.7 and expect this to hold always, or they reject experimental results as 'wrong' if they don't match theory exactly.

misconceptions.label: Treating mutually exclusive events as independent
misconceptions.description: When asked for P(rolling a 2 or a 4 on a die), students add fractions incorrectly or fail to recognise that outcomes cannot overlap. They may write 1/6 + 1/6 = 2/12 instead of 2/6, or they confuse 'or' (union) with 'and' (intersection), writing P(2 and 4) = 1/36.

misconceptions.label: Probability outside the range [0, 1]
misconceptions.description: Students write probabilities greater than 1 or less than 0, either through miscalculation (e.g. P = 7/5) or misunderstanding scale. They may also treat probability like a ratio and not recognise that P = 1 means certainty or P = 0 means impossibility, instead thinking 'high numbers mean more likely' without bound.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Single discrete event with clearly countable outcomes (e.g. rolling one die, picking one card from a small labelled set)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Compound or overlapping sample spaces requiring systematic listing or tree diagrams (e.g. outcomes from two dice, or drawing without replacement)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Probability expressed as a single form (fraction only, or decimal only)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Converting between fractions, decimals, percentages, and ratios; identifying equivalent forms; interpreting or comparing probabilities across representations

difficultyDimensions.easy: Explicit statement that an object is fair or unfair; outcomes clearly equally likely (e.g. fair die, fair coin)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Identifying hidden assumptions (e.g. is the spinner fair?); comparing theoretical and experimental data to infer fairness; recognising bias

transferContexts.context: Risk assessment in games and decision-making
transferContexts.example: A board game has a spinner with 5 equal sections: 3 red, 2 blue. Calculate the probability of landing on blue. If you need blue to win, would you play? Justify.
A factory inspects 200 items and finds 8 defective. Estimate the probability that a randomly selected item from the next batch is defective. How would you verify this estimate?
A weather forecast says there is a 60% chance of rain tomorrow. Explain what this probability means. If it rains on 60 out of 100 similar days, is the forecast accurate?

generativeContext: S1.3 focuses on single-event probability calculation using P(event) = favourable/total. Students must: (1) Correctly identify and count all possible equally-likely outcomes (sample space) without double-counting or omission; (2) Identify all favourable outcomes without overlap; (3) Form the correct fraction and simplify if required; (4) Convert between fractions, decimals, percentages as appropriate; (5) Distinguish between theoretical probability (based on equally-likely outcomes) and experimental probability (relative frequency from data); (6) Understand that all probabilities satisfy 0 ≤ P ≤ 1, with P = 0 (impossible) and P = 1 (certain) as boundaries; (7) Justify why a given sample space is correct (e.g. 'a die has 6 faces, all equally likely if fair'). Key contexts: fair/unfair objects, drawing from collections, spinning wheels, dice, cards. Common errors: using only favourable count as denominator, writing P > 1, ignoring fairness assumption, reversing numerator/denominator, treating 'or' as multiplication. Scaffold by: listing outcomes visually (lists or tables before probability), checking sample space is exhaustive and mutually exclusive, comparing theoretical prediction to actual experimental data over many trials.

name: Construct sample space diagrams for two events
masteryDefinition: Students can systematically construct and interpret sample space diagrams (tables, grids, or tree diagrams) for two independent or dependent events, correctly identifying all possible outcomes, calculating probabilities from the diagram, and using the diagram to solve probability problems involving combined events.

misconceptions.label: Incomplete enumeration of outcomes
misconceptions.description: Students fail to list all possible outcomes in a sample space diagram. Common error: when rolling two dice, listing only 6 outcomes instead of 36, or only listing outcomes they think are 'likely' rather than all possibilities. This shows misunderstanding that sample space must be exhaustive.

misconceptions.label: Treating order as irrelevant when it matters
misconceptions.description: Students treat (H,T) and (T,H) as the same outcome, or list 'one heads, one tails' as a single outcome rather than two distinct outcomes. This leads to underestimation of probability for non-extreme events. Particularly common with coin tosses and selecting without replacement.

misconceptions.label: Assuming equal probability when events are dependent
misconceptions.description: Students construct a sample space diagram for dependent events (e.g. drawing cards without replacement) but treat all branches or outcomes as equally likely. They fail to adjust probabilities along branches or fail to recognize that the sample space changes after the first event.

misconceptions.label: Confusing diagram type with solution method
misconceptions.description: Students construct a two-way table correctly but then attempt to read off probabilities directly without considering relative frequencies or totals. For example, reading '3' as a probability rather than recognizing it must be divided by the total number of outcomes.

difficultyDimensions.easy: Two events with 2 outcomes each (e.g., two coin flips: 4 total outcomes)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Two events with 6+ outcomes each (e.g., rolling two dice: 36 outcomes, or drawing two cards from a deck)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Independent events where probabilities don't change (e.g., two rolls of a die, two spins of a spinner)
difficultyDimensions.hard: Dependent events requiring conditional probability (e.g., drawing cards without replacement, selecting coloured balls from a bag without returning them)

difficultyDimensions.easy: Two-way table with clearly labelled rows and columns for simple symmetric outcomes
difficultyDimensions.hard: Tree diagram with unequal branch probabilities or varying sample spaces at each stage

transferContexts.context: Calculating combined event probabilities from a diagram
transferContexts.example: A two-way table shows outcomes of spinning two spinners. Use the diagram to find the probability of getting a total greater than 5, or the probability of getting at least one red.

transferContexts.context: Determining fairness and comparing probability models
transferContexts.example: Two games are described using sample space diagrams. Which game is fairer for both players? Construct a sample space diagram to justify your answer.

transferContexts.context: Conditional probability and restricted sample spaces
transferContexts.example: Given a sample space diagram for drawing two cards without replacement, find the probability that the second card is a heart given that the first card was a heart.

generativeContext: S1.4 focuses on the systematic construction and use of sample space diagrams (two-way tables and tree diagrams) to represent all outcomes of compound events. Mastery requires students to: (1) exhaustively enumerate all possible outcomes in an organized format; (2) preserve ordering and distinctness of outcomes where relevant (e.g., (H,T) ≠ (T,H) in sequential events); (3) correctly represent dependent events by adjusting the sample space or branch probabilities after the first event; (4) extract and calculate probabilities from the diagram by identifying favourable outcomes and relating them to total outcomes. Key errors arise from incomplete listing (missing outcomes), collapsing distinct ordered pairs into single entries, treating dependent events as independent, and misreading frequency counts as probabilities without normalization. Difficulty increases with outcome cardinality (2 vs 36), event structure (independent vs conditional), and diagram format (table vs tree with varying branch weights). Transfer requires applying diagrams to probability calculation, fairness comparison, and conditional reasoning.

name: Calculate probabilities from sample space diagrams for two events
masteryDefinition: Students can construct and interpret sample space diagrams (two-way tables, grids, or tree diagrams) for two independent or dependent events, identify all equally likely outcomes, and calculate probabilities of combined events using the sample space. They can distinguish between theoretical probability from a diagram and empirical probability, and explain why sample sizes affect reliability.

misconceptions.label: Counting outcomes incorrectly in a two-way table or grid
misconceptions.description: Students miscount the total number of outcomes, often by forgetting to include all rows or columns, or by counting cells rather than outcomes. For example, in a 3×4 grid, they count 7 cells instead of 12 total outcomes, leading to incorrect probability denominators.

misconceptions.label: Treating dependent events as independent in sample space diagrams
misconceptions.description: When constructing a sample space for dependent events (e.g. drawing two cards without replacement), students use the same outcomes on both branches as if replacement occurred. They fail to adjust the second event's sample space based on the first outcome.

misconceptions.label: Confusing probability with number of outcomes
misconceptions.description: Students state a probability as the count of favorable outcomes (e.g. 'the probability is 3') rather than as a fraction, decimal, or ratio. They do not recognise that probability must be a number between 0 and 1, or that it requires division of favorable outcomes by total outcomes.

misconceptions.label: Assuming all branches in a tree diagram have equal probability
